coredata.dc:title: Adsorptive removal of heavy metals, dyes, and pharmaceuticals: Carbon-based nanomaterials in focus
coredata.dc:creator: Thakur, Abhinay
Kumar, Ashish
Singh, Ambrish
coredata.dc:description: The use of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, graphene/graphene oxide, fullerene, and their derivatives, in the removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater has become a promising method in water treatment. These nanomaterials exhibit exceptional adsorption capabilities and selectivity due to their extensive surface area, customizable surface chemistry, and unique structural features, which involve factors like π-π interactions, electrostatic interactions, and specific surface functional groups. A notable experiment demonstrated the effectiveness of carbon nanotubes as adsorbents for removing Ismate violet 2R dye from contaminated water. The results showed a remarkable maximum removal efficiency of 91.71 % at a concentration of 10 mg/L within a brief 120-min period. This article conducts a comprehensive evaluation of the adsorptive removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater using carbon-based nanomaterials, with a focus on developments between 2015 and 2023. An in-depth bibliometric analysis of 1285 research publications retrieved from the Web of Science (WOS) and Scopus databases reveals a substantial increase in the number of publications, rising from 50 in 2015 to 158 in 2023. This signifies a burgeoning emphasis on both practical application and technological advancement in this domain. Furthermore, this review explores the intricacies of adsorption mechanisms, factors influencing the adsorption performance of carbon-based nanomaterials, and the associated challenges and limitations when applied in wastewater treatment. By thoroughly investigating these constraints and potential advantages, this article provides valuable insights for the development of future carbon-based nanomaterials, aiming to enhance their efficiency and expand their applications in the field of wastewater treatment.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Xia et al. [224] developed a straightforward and environmentally friendly way for developing a CD-based dual-mode fluorescence sensor from the biomass waste of wintersweet flowers (FW-CDs) for sequential and semi-quantitative visual assessment of Fe3+ and Cr(VI). The fluorescence durations of FW-CDs prior and afterward introducing Fe3+ and Cr(VI) were investigated, accordingly, to elucidate the specific quenching processes of Fe3+ and Cr(VI) to FW-CDs. It was determined that the mean fluorescence duration of FW-CDs was approximately 6.6 ns, barely changing in the inclusion or exclusion of Fe3+ and Cr(VI), presiding out the possibility of dynamic quenching systems, such as the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) methods. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum was then examined in order to further elucidate the quenching process. Fig. 25a shows that the UV–vis absorbance spectra of the FW-CDs/Cr(VI) blended media were very compatible with the cumulative Cr(VI) and FW-CDs absorption spectra, proving that no novel material was created throughout the quenching procedure and eliminating the probability of SQE. It was hypothesized that the quenching process of FW-CDs spurred on by Cr(VI) was an internal filter impact (IFE) since the excitation wavelength of FW-CDs had a significant overlap with the absorption spectra of Cr(VI). To additionally support the conclusion, the rectified fluorescence intensity (Fcor) of FW-CDs was computed utilizing the Parker eqn. prior to taking IFE into account. The correction aspect (CF = Fcor/Fobsd) statistics and the difference across Eobsd and Ecor enhanced constantly with increasing Cr(VI) composition, as shown in Fig. 25c, indicating that IFE accounts for a significant fraction of the measured overall suppression efficacy in the fluorescence-quenching method. IFE consequently dominates the procedure of quenching Cr(VI) to FW-CDs. As seen in Fig. 25b, the discrepancy among the UV–vis absorption region of the FW-CDs/Fe3+ blended media and the combination of the Fe3+ and FW-CDs absorption spectra indicates the production of the ground-state complex among the two molecules. The fact that the excitation frequency of FW-CDs barely coincided with the absorption region of Fe3+ was also significant since it shows that the IFE was not the main factor in quenching the fluorescent Fe3+ to the FW-CDs. In particular, the variance of absorption band levels of intensity approximately 3415 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1, which were connected to –OH, –NH, and –COOH, was exhibited to fluctuate. The exceptional sensitivity of the FW-CDs to Fe3+, in particular, might be due to the rapid chelating dynamics and the significant binding affinity of Fe3+ for the carboxyl and hydroxyl moieties over the substrate of the FW-CDs when contrasted to other metal ions. These elements might result in a nonradiative electron transmission from the excited phase of the FW-CDs, dimming their fluorescence. SQE as a whole served as the fundamental technique for quenching Fe3+ from the FW-CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, graphene/graphene oxide, fullerene, and their derivatives, in the removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater has become a promising method in water treatment. These nanomaterials exhibit exceptional adsorption capabilities and selectivity due to their extensive surface area, customizable surface chemistry, and unique structural features, which involve factors like π-π interactions, electrostatic interactions, and specific surface functional groups. A notable experiment demonstrated the effectiveness of carbon nanotubes as adsorbents for removing Ismate violet 2R dye from contaminated water. The results showed a remarkable maximum removal efficiency of 91.71 % at a concentration of 10 mg/L within a brief 120-min period. This article conducts a comprehensive evaluation of the adsorptive removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater using carbon-based nanomaterials, with a focus on developments between 2015 and 2023. An in-depth bibliometric analysis of 1285 research publications retrieved from the Web of Science (WOS) and Scopus databases reveals a substantial increase in the number of publications, rising from 50 in 2015 to 158 in 2023. This signifies a burgeoning emphasis on both practical application and technological advancement in this domain. Furthermore, this review explores the intricacies of adsorption mechanisms, factors influencing the adsorption performance of carbon-based nanomaterials, and the associated challenges and limitations when applied in wastewater treatment. By thoroughly investigating these constraints and potential advantages, this article provides valuable insights for the development of future carbon-based nanomaterials, aiming to enhance their efficiency and expand their applications in the field of wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water: A global Essentiality and its scarcity
ce:section.ce:para:
Water, a vital resource for various sectors including agriculture, human consumption, industries, and energy production, constantly moves across the Earth's surface through rivers, oceans, tributaries, snowfall, mountain glaciers, and underground channels [1–3]. Water is one of the most important commodities that has been accessible to us since the inception of life and remains indispensable for the well-being of humanity. Its availability in the form of surface water and reservoirs is essential for sustaining life and supporting human activities [4,5]. 97.5 % of the water on earth is salt water, which cannot be utilized without purification, as per statistics from the World Health Organization (WHO) and other organizations [6–9]. The remainder of the water (2.5 %) is often fresh, although the majority of it (70 %) is confined to ice caps and glaciers [9,10]. The leftover water is primarily found in the ground as moisture or is located in subterranean reservoirs. The amount of pure water that is usually accessible for human usage is 1 %, or 0.007 %, of the entire amount of water on earth [11,12]. One of humanity's key concerns has constantly been the desire for pure, refreshing, and consumable water. Water resources hold immense significance across the globe, serving as the lifeblood for communities and ecosystems in various regions [13,14]. Here are several examples that highlight the importance of water [15–17].
•
Sub-Saharan Africa: In many countries of Sub-Saharan Africa, access to clean water is a pressing issue. Rivers like the Niger, Congo, and Zambezi are vital sources of water for drinking, agriculture, and sanitation. A reliable water supply is crucial for combating waterborne diseases, supporting crop irrigation, and promoting overall well-being.
•
South Asia: The rivers in South Asia, such as the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Indus, play a critical role in sustaining densely populated regions. They provide water for agriculture, domestic use, and religious practices. These rivers are deeply intertwined with the cultural fabric of the region and hold religious significance for millions of people.
•
Middle East: In arid regions like the Middle East, water scarcity is a significant challenge. The Tigris and Euphrates rivers have historically been vital for the Mesopotamian civilizations, supporting agriculture and trade. The equitable and sustainable management of water resources in this region is crucial for maintaining stability and economic development.
•
North America: The Great Lakes in North America, consisting of Lake Superior, Lake Michigan, Lake Huron, Lake Erie, and Lake Ontario, is a vast freshwater resource. They not only provide drinking water to millions of people but also support industries, recreational activities, and a diverse ecosystem.
•
Europe: The rivers in Europe, including the Rhine, Danube, and Thames, have played significant roles in the region's history, culture, and economy. They serve as essential transportation routes, supply water for agriculture, and support hydropower generation. Sustainable water management practices are crucial to preserve these vital waterways.
•
South America: The Amazon River, with its extensive network of tributaries, is the largest river system globally, sustaining the Amazon rainforest and its incredible biodiversity. The Amazon River is a critical source of water for indigenous communities, as well as providing habitat for numerous species.

These examples highlight the diverse ways in which water resources shape societies, economies, and ecosystems worldwide. Access to clean and reliable water is essential for human well-being, agriculture, industry, and the preservation of natural environments. Ensuring sustainable management of water resources is key to meeting the growing demand for water and safeguarding the planet's future. However, the water from streams, seas, and underground aquifers was once clean and suitable for human sustenance before the advent of commercial operations [18,19]. Regrettably, with rapid urbanization and the adoption of modern agricultural and residential practices, there has been a substantial increase in water demand. This heightened demand has resulted in the generation of significant quantities of wastewater that pose a threat to human and wildlife survival. For example, in many industrialized regions, the discharge of effluents from factories and manufacturing facilities has contaminated water bodies (Fig. 1 ) [20]. These effluents often contain toxic chemicals, heavy metals, and pollutants that are harmful to both aquatic life and human health [21,22]. Similarly, the rapid expansion of urban areas has led to the discharge of domestic wastewater, including sewage and household chemicals, into rivers and lakes, degrading water quality. In agricultural regions, the use of fertilizers, pesticides, and herbicides has resulted in the contamination of water sources [23–25]. Excessive irrigation and improper management of agricultural runoff contribute to the accumulation of nutrients and harmful substances in water bodies, leading to eutrophication and the proliferation of harmful algal blooms. Furthermore, the improper disposal of pharmaceuticals and personal care products has emerged as a growing concern [26–28]. These substances, when flushed down toilets or discarded into sinks, can find their way into water sources, posing potential risks to aquatic ecosystems and potentially impacting human health through the consumption of contaminated drinking water. As per a recent report, approximately 2 million tons of garbage are dumped into recipient waters a single day, comprising chemicals and manufacturing pollutants, and domestic and farming effluents, as per the United Nations World Water Development Survey [29,30]. Additionally, additional wastewater is produced and distributed nowadays more than at any previous point in the existence of the earth, and more than one in six people lacks accessibility to clean drinking water, as per the World Water Council and the WHO [31,32]. Additionally, WHO research estimates that 2.6 billion people, or nearly 42 % of the world's people, lack access to adequate sanitary amenities and that 1.1 billion people lack access to any form of enhanced drinking water source [33–36]. Moreover, it is predicted that around the middle of this century, water will be limited for at least 7 billion individuals in 60 nations and at least 2 billion individuals in 48 nations [37–40]. Untreated wastewater which comprises a bunch of organic substances often utilizes all the solubilized oxygen in the water channel to achieve its biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), which is necessary for marine life [41,42]. Untreated wastewater also frequently includes a lot of infective, or disease-causing bacteria [43,44]. Discharging untreated wastewater into watercourses without any treatment procedures also causes several ecological issues. Additionally, wastewater might comprise nutrients that encourage the proliferation of aquatic flora and algae blooms, causing rivers and lakes to become eutrophic. Additionally, the breakdown of organic compounds in wastewater may produce large quantities of odiferous gases which is hazardous for our ecosystem.

As a result of emerging pollutants (EPs) including dyes, heavy metals, pesticides, and organic chemicals being released into water channels, the wastewater produced by various sources might comprise some of the pollutants indicated above [45–48]. In this section, we will briefly discuss the several types of abovementioned emerging pollutants that led to the contamination of water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emerging pollutants in the water ecosystem

ce:section.ce:para:
Heavy metals are regarded to be pollutants that need to be removed because of their enormous harmful impacts. The tanner, batteries, glassware, electroplating, ceramic, mining, coatings, and photography enterprises are among the several enterprises that produce wastewater [49]. Lead, cadmium, chromium, arsenic, manganese, copper, nickel, cobalt, iron, vanadium, mercury, molybdenum, and bismuth are just a few of the heavy metals found in these wastewaters [50,51]. Any wastewater's metal content is intently linked to the activities performed in an industry in terms of quantity and variety. For instance, tanneries release Cr into wastewater; mercury pollution is caused by the manufacturing of electrical devices; chromium, copper, zinc, and cadmium are frequently produced during metal plating; quarrying, smelting, and the burning of fossil fuels; and lead is produced from a variety of mining and industrial inputs [52–55]. The majority of wastewaters have heavy metal concentrations that are substantially higher than the acceptable allowed levels, necessitating their removal. There are several global reports and incidents due to the discharge of heavy metals in water systems, such as.
•
Minamata Disease, Japan: One of the most infamous incidents of heavy metal contamination occurred in Minamata, Japan, in the mid-20th century. Industrial wastewater discharged from a chemical factory contained methylmercury, resulting in widespread mercury poisoning. The contaminated seafood, primarily fish, consumed by local communities led to severe health effects, including neurological disorders and birth defects [56].
•
Lead Contamination in Flint, United States: The water crisis in Flint, Michigan, brought attention to the issue of lead contamination in water systems. In 2014, the city switched its water source to the Flint River without proper corrosion control measures [57]. This resulted in the leaching of lead from aging pipes, contaminating the drinking water supply. The incident exposed thousands of residents, including children, to lead poisoning, leading to long-term health consequences.
•
Acid Mine Drainage in Rio Tinto, Spain: The Rio Tinto River in Spain has been heavily impacted by acid mine drainage, a phenomenon caused by the oxidation of sulfide minerals in mining areas. The river's waters have become highly acidic and contaminated with heavy metals, including copper and iron. The pollution has severely affected aquatic life and has had ecological implications for the entire river ecosystem.
•
Industrial Pollution in the Citarum River, Indonesia: The Citarum River in Indonesia, considered one of the most polluted rivers globally, has been extensively contaminated by heavy metals and industrial waste. Numerous industries, including textile factories and tanneries, discharge untreated wastewater directly into the river. This has resulted in severe pollution, affecting both the environment and the health of communities relying on the river for their water supply.
•
Gold Mining Contamination in the Amazon, South America: Gold mining activities, particularly illegal and informal mining, have caused significant heavy metal contamination in water systems across the Amazon rainforest. Mercury, used in the gold extraction process, is released into rivers and streams, leading to mercury poisoning in local populations and environmental degradation [58,59]. This has had detrimental effects on the health of indigenous communities and the fragile ecosystem of the Amazon.

These examples highlight the global nature of heavy metal contamination incidents in water systems. They demonstrate the devastating impacts of improper waste management and industrial practices, underscoring the need for effective regulations, responsible industrial practices, and robust monitoring systems to prevent and address heavy metal pollution in water resources.

Organic dyes, a significant ingredient used nowadays for both residential and commercial uses, are yet another proliferating contaminant. More than 8000 different types of organic dyes have been produced and therefore employed for various applications since the development of synthesized dyes in 1856 [60,61]. Both insoluble and water-soluble dyes are now being used. The textiles, coloring, leather tanning, paper & pulp and paint sectors are the biggest importers of dyes. Because dyes color water, which is unacceptable on aesthetic considerations, and because they are hazardous and negatively impact life, the pollutants of these sectors and those from units that make dyes frequently comprise enough dye to be classified as an unpleasant form of pollutant [62–64]. When these dyes are discharged into water bodies, either through industrial effluents or inadequate wastewater treatment, they can pose a serious threat to aquatic ecosystems and water quality. The discharge of untreated wastewater containing organic dyes can result in the following consequences.
•
Ecological Implications: Organic dyes have the potential to disrupt aquatic ecosystems by affecting the photosynthetic activity of algae and other aquatic plants. Dyes can inhibit light penetration into the water, hampering photosynthesis and reducing oxygen levels. This can lead to a decrease in biodiversity, harmful algal blooms, and even the death of aquatic organisms.
•
Water Contamination: Organic dyes are often non-biodegradable and persist in water bodies for extended periods. They can leach into groundwater or remain in surface water, resulting in the contamination of drinking water sources. Exposure to these dyes through drinking water consumption can have adverse effects on human health.
•
Environmental Persistence: Some organic dyes, particularly azo dyes, may undergo transformation or breakdown processes, producing toxic byproducts or aromatic amines. These aromatic amines are known to be potentially carcinogenic and can persist in the environment, posing long-term risks to both humans and wildlife [65–67].
•
Aesthetic and Recreational Impacts: The presence of organic dyes in water bodies can cause discoloration, altering the visual appearance and reducing the aesthetic value of natural water resources. Additionally, it can impact recreational activities such as swimming and fishing, as the discolored water may indicate poor water quality and pose potential health risks.

The discharge of organic dyes into water systems has been a global environmental concern. Here are several reports and incidents highlighting the impact of dye discharge on water systems.
•
Textile Industry Pollution in Bangladesh: The textile industry in Bangladesh has been associated with significant water pollution due to the discharge of untreated wastewater containing organic dyes. The Buriganga River, which flows through Dhaka, the capital city, has been severely affected. Reports indicate that the river has turned into a highly polluted water body due to dye discharge, posing risks to aquatic life and human health [68].
•
River Pollution in China: China, being one of the largest textile producers, has witnessed instances of severe dye pollution in water systems. The discharge of untreated wastewater from textile factories, including dye-containing effluents, has led to the contamination of rivers and lakes. The Pearl River and Yangtze River are among the affected water bodies, impacting both the environment and the availability of clean drinking water [69].
•
Contamination in the Citarum River, Indonesia: The Citarum River in Indonesia, known as one of the most polluted rivers globally, suffers from severe organic dye pollution. The textile and leather industries, which discharge untreated wastewater containing dyes and other pollutants, contribute to the contamination of the river [70]. The presence of dyes has led to the loss of biodiversity and has adverse effects on local communities depending on the river for water supply.
•
Dye Pollution in the Palar River, India: The Palar River in India has experienced significant pollution due to the discharge of untreated wastewater from textile dyeing units. The river has been heavily contaminated with organic dyes and other chemical pollutants, resulting in the deterioration of water quality and the disruption of the river ecosystem. The pollution has raised concerns about the health and livelihoods of communities residing along the riverbanks.
•
Industrial Pollution in the Matanza-Riachuelo Basin, Argentina: The Matanza-Riachuelo Basin, located in the Buenos Aires metropolitan area, has been subjected to severe pollution, including the discharge of organic dyes. Industrial activities, particularly those related to the textile industry, have contributed to the contamination of the basin [71]. The polluted water poses risks to human health, affecting the residents living in the vicinity.

These incidents and reports highlight the global issue of organic dye pollution in water systems, particularly in regions with a significant textile industry presence. The discharge of untreated wastewater containing dyes poses risks to both the environment and human populations, jeopardizing water quality, biodiversity, and public health. These cases emphasize the urgent need for stricter regulations, improved wastewater treatment practices, and the adoption of sustainable dyeing technologies to minimize the environmental impact of organic dyes.

Pharmaceuticals are essential for human and animal health, providing treatment for various medical conditions. However, their usage and improper disposal can contribute to water contamination, raising concerns about their environmental impact [72,73]. When pharmaceuticals are consumed, a portion of the compounds is excreted from the body and enters wastewater through sewage systems. Additionally, the improper disposal of unused medications, such as flushing them down toilets or drains, can directly introduce pharmaceuticals into the water system [74,75]. Conventional wastewater treatment plants are often not equipped to effectively remove pharmaceutical compounds, allowing them to pass through the treatment process and enter water bodies. The presence of pharmaceuticals in water systems can have ecological and health implications. These compounds can persist in the environment and accumulate over time, adversely affecting aquatic organisms and disrupting ecosystems. Moreover, the presence of antibiotics in water systems contributes to the development of antibiotic resistance, a growing concern in the medical field. Human exposure to pharmaceutical-contaminated water is a potential risk, although the direct health effects are still being studied [76,77]. Prolonged exposure to certain pharmaceuticals may have consequences such as endocrine disruption and potential long-term effects on human development. The discharge of pharmaceuticals into water systems has been a global concern with various reports and incidents highlighting the potential risks and impacts. Here are several examples.
•
United States: The U.S. Geological Survey conducted a study in 1999–2000 that detected pharmaceutical compounds in 80 % of water samples collected from 139 streams across 30 states [78]. The presence of pharmaceuticals, including antibiotics, hormones, and antidepressants, raised concerns about their potential effects on aquatic ecosystems and human health.
•
Europe: The European Environment Agency reported in 2013 that pharmaceuticals were widespread in European waters [79]. Studies in countries such as Germany, France, and the Netherlands have found pharmaceutical residues in rivers, lakes, and groundwater sources. This has prompted concerns about the long-term impacts on aquatic organisms and the potential for antimicrobial resistance development.
•
India: A study published in 2016 highlighted the presence of pharmaceutical residues in the Yamuna River, one of the major rivers in India [80,81]. The study detected several pharmaceutical compounds, including antibiotics and painkillers, which were traced back to the discharge of untreated wastewater from pharmaceutical manufacturing units. This raised concerns about the contamination of drinking water sources and its potential impact on public health.
•
Australia: Reports have emerged regarding the presence of pharmaceuticals in waterways across Australia. Studies conducted in major cities such as Sydney and Melbourne have detected a wide range of pharmaceutical compounds in river systems, raising concerns about potential ecological impacts and the need for improved wastewater treatment practices.
•
China: The rapid growth of the pharmaceutical industry in China has raised concerns about the discharge of pharmaceutical residues into water systems [82]. Reports have highlighted the presence of various pharmaceutical compounds in rivers and lakes, including antibiotics and hormones. This has prompted discussions on the need for stricter regulations and improved wastewater treatment infrastructure.
•
Africa: Limited studies have been conducted on pharmaceutical pollution in water systems in Africa. However, emerging reports suggest the presence of pharmaceutical compounds in rivers and lakes in countries such as Nigeria and South Africa. These findings underline the global nature of pharmaceutical pollution and the need for further research in diverse regions.

These reports and incidents underscore the global issue of pharmaceutical contamination in water systems. The discharge of pharmaceuticals, whether from manufacturing units, healthcare facilities, or human excretion, has the potential to impact water quality, aquatic ecosystems, and potentially human health. It highlights the importance of implementing proper wastewater treatment processes, adopting responsible medication disposal practices, and promoting awareness about the potential environmental impacts of pharmaceuticals.

While several other pollutants, including pesticides and organic chemicals, are primarily a concern associated with rural and urban surroundings [83–85]. As weed control chemicals are employed more frequently throughout field cultivation and currently constitute the bulk of farm pesticides, herbicide manufacturing has expanded dramatically. Despite the fact that DDT (dichloro-diphenyl-trichloroethane) has been outlawed, a number of its alternatives, including parathion, toxaphene, lindane, malathion, endrin and heptachlor could still harm the ecosystem [86–88]. Pesticide manufacturing facilities as well as agriculture activities are both responsible for the pesticide contamination issue. The environment is also threatened by diverse organic chemicals, such as trihalomethanes like bromoform and chloroform, tetrachloroethylene, trichloroethylene, aromatic hydrocarbons like toluene, benzene, xylene, and biphenyls, halogenated aromatics like dichlorobenzene, chlorobenzene, chloroxylenol and cholorotoluene [89–91]. These contaminants need to be rectified utilizing efficient and ecologically friendly filtering techniques before they can be discarded. Water supplies could be recovered owing to treating wastewater, and toxic waste discharge could be reduced as well. There are three main phases to the wastewater treatment procedure: primary, secondary, and tertiary water treatment. While secondary processing typically eliminates approximately 90 % of the waste, primary processing often only eliminates about 60 % of the dispersed waste [92–96]. Furthermore, if fecal sludge extraction from the system is not done regularly, the system as a whole might begin functioning efficiently. Nanomaterials (NMs) have attracted a lot of interest over time owing to their distinctive features and crucial roles in water treatment operations. NMs could be used to purify water in two different approaches to increase the efficiency of wastewater treatment procedures: (i) by adding inexpensive nano adsorbents; and (ii) via nanofiltration [97,98]. The most challenging pollutants to remove from wastewater are pollutants with a size between 1 and 100 nm, thus nano-based techniques are a suitable solution. The superior physicochemical characteristics of NMs as well as the geometrical realm of nanotechnologies make them very helpful for water treatment. Nanotechnology investigation and innovation are advancing quickly on a global scale, with possibilities in food conservation, solar energy, healthcare, textiles, and water treatment [99–102]. Each year, the US invests 6 billion us dollars in the investigation and advancement of nanotechnology [103–105]. Inorganic and organic contaminants from wastewater that are typically challenging to eliminate could be absorbed by nanoparticles (NPs). Utilizing NPs has the additional benefit of preventing the emission of toxic byproducts throughout production. These benefits are achieved by their special characteristics, which include their compact dimension, wide surface region, excellent reactivity and significant permeability [106]. Additionally, they feature special visual characteristics like transparency and the existence of prismatic coatings. The most crucial factor is the huge surface region since it provides more active adsorbing spots. Moreover, reusability is a key sustaining feature of several NPs. NPs could be created artificially as well as through natural origins. The manufacturing of new NMs using substances having favorable qualities has received extensive scientific attention, especially for enhancing the integrity of the pollutant outflow from diverse origins [106–108].

This review article aims to provide a comprehensive evaluation of the adsorptive removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater using carbon-based nanomaterials. By examining the latest developments and advancements between 2015 and 2023, the study will contribute to the existing knowledge on the subject and highlight the potential benefits and challenges associated with the use of carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment. The significance of this study lies in its ability to.
•
Consolidate the current understanding of the adsorptive removal of emerging pollutants using carbon-based nanomaterials.
•
Identify the unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials that contribute to their high adsorption capacities and selectivity.
•
Evaluate the performance and effectiveness of different carbon-based nanomaterials for the removal of heavy metals, organic dyes, and pharmaceutical pollutants from wastewater.
•
Highlight the factors influencing the adsorption process and discuss potential solutions to enhance adsorption efficiency.
•
Discuss the constraints and potential benefits of using carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment, including cost-effectiveness, scalability, environmental implications, energy efficiency, and health and safety considerations.

The primary objectives of this review article are.
•
To provide a comprehensive overview of the adsorptive removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater using carbon-based nanomaterials.
•
To examine the latest developments and advancements in the field between 2015 and 2023.
•
To analyze the unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials that contribute to their adsorption capabilities and selectivity.
•
To evaluate the performance of different carbon-based nanomaterials for the removal of heavy metals, organic dyes, and pharmaceutical pollutants from wastewater.
•
To discuss the factors influencing the adsorption process and potential solutions to enhance adsorption efficiency.
•
To highlight the constraints and potential benefits of using carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment, including cost-effectiveness, scalability, environmental implications, energy efficiency, and health and safety considerations.

By pursuing these objectives, this review article seeks to contribute fresh insights and drive the progression of knowledge in the realm of employing carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment, particularly in the removal of emerging pollutants through adsorption. While prior reviews have undeniably made substantial contributions, it is essential to acknowledge the dynamic and ever-evolving nature of this field of study [109–112]. What sets this review apart is its comprehensive synthesis and analysis of the most recent research, advancements, and discoveries in the domain. By presenting an updated perspective and analysis, this review aims to provide a novel outlook on the subject matter, capturing the latest breakthroughs and revelations that may not have received adequate attention or have emerged subsequent to the publication of earlier reviews.

In this review, we consolidate the progress and innovations concerning the utilization of carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment. We underscore the utility of carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene/graphene oxide (GO), fullerenes, and carbon dots (CDs) due to their exceptional physicochemical properties, including their small size, remarkable surface-area-to-volume ratio, tunability through modification/functionalization, and outstanding adsorption characteristics. Furthermore, tailoring the surface properties of carbon-based materials makes it relatively straightforward to target specific water contaminants. This article critically examines works that showcase substantial advancements in this domain from 2015 to 2023. Our objective is to offer a more nuanced and current comprehension of the subject matter, ensuring that readers access the most pertinent and valuable information available.

To conduct our research, we initiated a systematic search on the Web of Science Core Collection using keywords such as “adsorptive removal,” “carbon-based nanomaterials,” “emerging pollutants,” “wastewater treatment,” and “adsorption mechanism.” This search yielded a total of 1285 publications. Subsequently, we meticulously collected various data points associated with these publications, including keywords, author details, institutional affiliations, journal sources, and citation counts [113,114]. This extensive dataset enabled us to gain insights into the academic landscape, key trends, and potential solutions within the realm of wastewater treatment utilizing carbon-based nanomaterials. We performed a descriptive statistical analysis utilizing Microsoft Excel 2019 software. This analysis encompassed an examination of the annual growth rate, the contributions from different countries, and the frequencies of keywords in the collected publications. Notably, we observed an annual growth rate of 15.47 % in this field, reflecting a significant and sustained increase in research output over time. Moreover, our analysis revealed the involvement of a total of 3470 authors in these publications. Notably, international collaboration was a noteworthy aspect, accounting for 23.5 % of authorship, highlighting the global nature of research in this area. Additionally, the average number of citations per document was found to be 24.05, indicating the impact and relevance of the research conducted in the field of wastewater treatment using carbon-based nanomaterials. This information collectively offers a comprehensive overview of the state of the field and the collaborative dynamics among researchers worldwide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Annual growth rate and most cited countries
ce:section.ce:para: The annual progress rate in wastewater treatment using carbon-based nanomaterials over the years 2015–2023 reveals a dynamic field. As shown in Fig. 2 , it started with 50 articles in 2015 and surged by 74 %–87 articles in 2016, indicating increasing interest. There was a slight dip in 2017 with 73 articles, followed by a substantial 39 % increase to 121 articles in 2018. The field remained relatively steady with 115 articles in 2019 and 104 in 2020. A growth trend resumed in 2021, with 133 articles, and continued into 2022, reaching 159 articles. In 2023, there were 158 articles, showing little change from the previous year. This annual progress rate signifies sustained research activity and highlights the field's importance in addressing water pollution challenges. Additionally, the data presented in Fig. 3 reveals the research performance of several countries in the field of wastewater treatment using carbon-based nanomaterials. Notably, China leads with a substantial total citation count of 12,255 and an average article citation count of 25.90, reflecting both prolific output and influence. The USA also stands out with 666 total citations and a remarkable average article citation count of 51.20, indicating high recognition. India has a strong presence with 2142 total citations and an average article citation count of 20.20. Pakistan boasts the highest average article citation count at 40.00, with a total of 1281 citations, demonstrating significant influence. Poland exhibits high-quality research with a total citation count of 577 and an average article citation count of 44.40. Meanwhile, Australia showcases substantial influence, with 514 total citations and an impressive average article citation count of 57.10. Iran is notable with 1070 total citations and an average article citation count of 25.50. Egypt is actively engaged in the field with 405 total citations and an average article citation count of 27.00. Korea actively contributes to the field with 638 total citations and an average article citation count of 19.30, while Malaysia demonstrates involvement with 411 total citations and an average article citation count of 18.70.

Fig. 4 reflects the research production of various countries or regions in the field of wastewater treatment using carbon-based nanomaterials. China leads the pack with a substantial 1905 research articles, underscoring its significant commitment to advancing this field. India follows closely with 386 articles, indicating a substantial research presence. Iran contributes actively with 185 articles, while Pakistan showcases its research activity with 152 articles. South Korea maintains an active research community, producing 123 articles, and Malaysia has generated 86 research articles. Other contributors include Russia with 76 articles, the USA with 74, Egypt with 70, and Australia with 61 articles. Together, these countries demonstrate the global interest and collaborative effort in addressing water pollution challenges through research in this field.

Moreover, Fig. 5 provides an overview of research article distribution across several countries in the context of wastewater treatment using carbon-based nanomaterials, with a focus on three key parameters: SCP (Single-Country Publications), MCP (Multiple-Country Publications), and the MCP_Ratio (Multiple-Country Publication Ratio). China leads with 474 articles, and it's notable for having a sizeable portion of single-country publications (SCP) at 393. International collaboration is also significant, with an MCP_Ratio of 0.171. India follows with 106 articles, and a noteworthy 86 of these are SCP. India shows a strong commitment to international collaboration, reflected in an MCP_Ratio of 0.189. Korea is another active contributor with 33 articles, 23 of which are SCP. The MCP_Ratio is relatively high at 0.303, indicating a substantial focus on international collaborations. Pakistan demonstrates a strong inclination toward international collaborations, with a high MCP_Ratio of 0.438 out of 32 articles. Of these, 18 are SCP. The USA also has a substantial focus on international research partnerships, with an MCP_Ratio of 0.538 out of 13 articles, although it has a relatively low SCP count of 6. Other countries like Iran, Malaysia, Egypt, Poland, and Russia contribute to the research landscape, each with its unique mix of SCP and MCP. These findings highlight the diverse research output and collaboration patterns among these countries, emphasizing the global nature of research in this field and the importance of cross-border partnerships in advancing knowledge in wastewater treatment using carbon-based nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Most frequent words, tree map, and word cloud
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 provides valuable insights into the prevalent keywords within research dedicated to wastewater treatment involving carbon-based nanomaterials. Among the most frequently occurring terms, “removal” and “heavy metals” take the lead, shedding light on the research community's strong emphasis on the removal of pollutants, especially heavy metals, from water sources. Following closely is the term “adsorption,” which underscores the crucial role played by nanomaterials in adsorbing these contaminants. Moreover, the terms “pollution” and “contamination” underscore the primary concern of researchers in the field: the mitigation and prevention of water pollution, revealing the urgency of addressing environmental challenges. Specific heavy metal contaminants such as “cadmium” and “lead” emerge frequently, highlighting the importance of addressing these specific pollutants in water treatment research. Furthermore, the term “water” itself is fundamental, emphasizing the core theme of purifying and safeguarding water resources, which are vital for both human and environmental well-being. “Aqueous solution” signals the practical applications of this research, underlining its real-world relevance in addressing water quality issues [115,116]. The term “accumulation” suggests a growing concern regarding the buildup of contaminants over time, emphasizing the need for effective pollutant removal methods to prevent long-term environmental damage.

These findings have valuable implications for a wide audience, encompassing academic researchers, practitioners, and policy-makers with an interest in this research domain. They offer insights into potential avenues for future research, including technological advancements, commercial applications, and resource reutilization within the wastewater treatment sector. Subsequent sections will delve into a comprehensive overview and practical utilization of Carbon-Based Nanomaterials for the Adsorptive Removal of various Emerging Pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based nanomaterials for adsorptive removal of emerging pollutants

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have gained significant attention as promising adsorbents for the removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater [117–119]. These nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerenes, and graphene/graphene oxides (GO), possess unique properties that make them suitable for adsorption processes. One of the key advantages of carbon-based nanomaterials is their large surface area, which provides ample sites for the adsorption of pollutants [120]. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are cylindrical carbon structures with a high aspect ratio and excellent mechanical strength. They can be classified into single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), depending on the number of concentric layers. CNTs have a large surface area and unique structural properties that contribute to their adsorption capacity. They are particularly effective in the removal of various EPs, including heavy metals, organic pollutants, and pharmaceutical compounds, due to their high adsorption efficiency and chemical stability. Graphene and graphene oxide (GO) are two-dimensional carbon materials composed of a single layer of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. Graphene is highly conductive and possesses a large surface area, making it an attractive adsorbent for EP removal [121–123]. However, its tendency to aggregate can limit its application. On the other hand, GO, which is an oxidized form of graphene, exhibits good dispersibility in water and can be easily functionalized with various surface functional groups. GO has been extensively studied for the adsorption of organic dyes, heavy metals, and pharmaceuticals from wastewater, demonstrating high adsorption capacities and excellent removal efficiencies.

Carbon dots (CDs) are small carbon nanoparticles with sizes typically less than 10 nm. They exhibit unique optical, electronic, and surface properties. CDs can be synthesized from various carbon sources, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, or organic molecules. CDs have attracted attention for their potential in wastewater treatment due to their high surface area, tunable surface chemistry, and excellent dispersibility. They have shown promising adsorption capabilities for various EPs, including heavy metals, dyes, and pharmaceuticals. Fullerenes, such as C60 and its derivatives, are spherical carbon molecules composed of carbon atoms arranged in a cage-like structure. They possess a high degree of conjugation and unique electronic properties. Fullerenes have been investigated for their adsorption potential in wastewater treatment, particularly for the removal of organic pollutants and disinfection by-products. The large π-electron cloud of fullerenes enables them to interact with EPs through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, leading to efficient adsorption [124–126]. In addition to these carbon-based nanomaterials, hybrid nanomaterials combining carbon-based nanomaterials with other functional materials have also been explored for wastewater treatment. These hybrid materials can exhibit enhanced adsorption capacities, selectivity, and stability. Examples include carbon nanotube-based composites, graphene-based composites, and carbon dot-based composites, which incorporate additional materials such as metals, metal oxides, or polymers to enhance the adsorption performance [45,127,128]. To increase the sorption and recyclability of carbon-based NMs, many different surface modifications have been made which have been discussed below with the explanation of various carbon-based NMs as adsorbents for the treatment of wastewater.

In an experiment, in order to adsorb methylene blue (MB), Zhang et al. [129] created a unique gelatin-based functionalized CNT@MOF (F-CNTs@MOF@Gel) adsorbent. The MOF form was validated as Cu-BTC, commonly referred to as HKUST-1. In this experiment, gelatin was used to glue the F-CNTs@Cu- BTC particles, preventing environmental contamination from carbon NMs particulate. To enhance the number of functional adsorption spots accessible over the interface of the adsorbent's intrinsic porous network, CNTs were utilized as the linking framework. The following is a description of the precise synthesis procedure used in this research. In a mixture of 70 mL DMF: ethanol:H2O = 1:1:1 (v/v), 1.50 g Cu(NO3)23H2O, 0.20 g F-CNTs and 0.75 g H3BTC were added. They were then agitated for 2 h before being thoroughly disseminated using ultrasound for 20 min. Then a 100 mL (Teflon-stainless steel) autoclave was filled with the completely blended liquid. For 48 h, the hydrothermal reactor was kept in the furnace at 373 K. The F-CNTs' carboxyl moiety (-COOH) served as the Cu-growth BTC's sites in this stage. Furthermore, both π-π EDA and hydrogen bonding held the F-CNTs to Cu-BTC in a robust bind (see Fig. 9 for the whole stated mechanism). The black solid was cleaned 3 times using ethanol and water following the reaction and freezing. Subsequently, the excessive DMF and ethanol from the substrate of the black solid were removed by drying it at 393 K for 8 h.

Additionally, the optimum sorption capacities of F-CNTs, Cu-BTC, Gel, F–C, C-G, and F–C-G to MB are shown in Fig. 10 and are 121, 114, 54, 158, 88, and 106 mg/g. The typical deviations (σ) of the highest adsorption capacities of each substance are 3, 2, 1.7, 2.8, 2.2, and 2.6. The F–C had a better adsorption capability over either of its two parental substances following being created by merging F-CNTs and Cu-BTC. The Gel exhibited the weakest adsorption capability owing to its lowest relative surface region, as could be observed from the findings of the adsorption capability of several materials mentioned previously. When combined with the gel, the F–C-G and C-G had adsorption capabilities that were superior to those of the gel but inferior to those of the powdered three-carbon compounds. The effective surface region of the composite was lower as contrasted to the carbon dust as a result of the Gel covering the majority of the carbon powder molecules.

Similar to this, fullerenes are frequently used as adsorbents in the remediation of industrialized wastewater due to their enormous surface region, which aids in the removal of EPs. Additionally, graphene is the term for the 2D allotrope of carbon [130]. Owing to its molecular filtering property, non-permeable graphene/GO is being explored for sophisticated and contemporary membrane segregation procedures. In order to obtain desired qualities that were not originally evident in pure graphene, the surface of the material is modified [130–141]. Typically, Hummer's technique is used for chemical oxidation to produce layers of GO. This technique includes the functional moieties including carboxylic acid (–COOH) and hydroxyl group (–OH) in the graphene lattices. Graphene/GO sheets are utilized for treating wastewater because they have an extremely permeable architecture and surface coverage. Similarly, In order to treat the Rhodamine B (Rh B) aqueous solutions, Shi et al. [60] explored into rGO-supported nanoscale zero-valent iron (nZVI/rGO). In this experiment, an artificial neural network hybridized comprising genetic algorithm (ANN-GA) and response surface methods (RSM) was utilized to optimize the impacts of several significant variables (quantity, preliminary pH, contact period and temperature) on the elimination of Rh B by nZVI/rGO. By applying the ANN-GA approach, the anticipated maximum Rh B elimination effectiveness (90 %) was found to agree with the empirical value (86.4 %). Additionally, the Freundlich isotherm was the best model for characterizing the mechanism for the adsorption of Rh B upon the nZVI/rGO composites. According to the Langmuir isotherm, the maximal sorption capability was 87.72 mg/g. The pseudo-second-order kinetic model accurately predicted that Rh B might be removed within 20 min using this innovative approach. The outcomes indicated that the ANN-GA strategy was superior to the RSM strategy in terms of accuracy.

Similarly, to ascertain the effectiveness of CNT manufactured by microwave irradiation as an adsorbent in absorbing heavy metal ions from wastewater, Mubarak et al. [142] conducted quantitative research on the elimination of Cr3+ and Pb2+ ions. According to the analytical study, 50 min of agitating, pH 4.0, 0.09 g of CNTs and 150 rpm of agitation were the ideal variables for the maximum elimination of Pb2+. The maximum expulsion of Cr3+ ions, however, was noticed at 50 min of agitating, pH 8.0, 0.09 g of CNTs, 60 min and 150 rpm of agitation. The extraction efficacy of Cr3+ and Pb2+ ions were 95.5 and 99.9 %, respectively, at the preliminary content of 2 mg/L. Cr3+ and Pb2+ ions had maximal adsorption capabilities of 24.45 mg/g for Cr3+ ions and 15.34 mg/g for Pb2+ ions upon the CNT. In addition, the elimination of Pb2+ ions required Freundlich and Langmuir characteristics of 1.438 and 0.073 L/mg, whereas the elimination of Cr3+ ions required 1.317 and 0.071 L/mg. The quantitative study demonstrated that the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm models suited the elimination of Cr3+ and Pb2+ ions and that both systems adhered to the pseudo-second-order principle. Additionally, when discussing reactors for adsorption processes, several innovations and critical considerations can be highlighted. These innovations aim to enhance the efficiency, capacity, and sustainability of adsorption-based systems [143–145].
•
Continuous Flow Reactors: Continuous flow reactors are gaining prominence due to their ability to provide a steady stream of treated water or gas. This is particularly important in large-scale applications such as municipal water treatment and industrial emissions control. Their design allows for longer contact times between the adsorbent and the target pollutants, increasing removal efficiency. Moreover, they are amenable to automation, reducing the need for constant human intervention.
•
Multifunctional Adsorbents: Multifunctional adsorbents represent a significant advancement in adsorption technology. These adsorbents are engineered to target multiple pollutants with high specificity. For instance, a single adsorbent may effectively remove heavy metals, organic compounds, and microorganisms simultaneously. This innovation streamlines the treatment process, reducing the need for multiple reactors and simplifying the overall system.
•
Hierarchical Pore Structures: The concept of hierarchical pore structures in adsorbents involves creating a range of pore sizes within the material. Micro-, meso-, and macropores allow for the rapid diffusion of pollutants into the adsorbent. This is particularly relevant for large molecules or colloids, enhancing mass transfer and overall adsorption kinetics. Hierarchical pore structures are being explored in various materials, including activated carbons and zeolites [146,147].
•
Functionalization and Surface Modification: Tailoring the surface of adsorbents with specific functional groups or modifications is a powerful tool for targeting emerging contaminants. For example, if pharmaceuticals or personal care products are present in wastewater, the adsorbent's surface can be functionalized to interact strongly with these compounds. This level of customization enhances selectivity and efficiency.
•
Smart Reactor Design: Smart reactors equipped with sensors, actuators, and automated control systems are revolutionizing the field. These reactors can adapt to changing influent conditions in real-time. If pollutant concentrations fluctuate, the reactor can adjust parameters such as flow rate, contact time, and temperature to optimize removal efficiency. Machine learning algorithms can predict future conditions and make proactive adjustments. Smart reactor design also extends to remote monitoring, allowing operators to oversee treatment plants from afar.
•
Electrochemical Regeneration: Electrochemical regeneration techniques are eco-friendly and cost-effective. These methods use low levels of electricity to desorb pollutants from the adsorbent, allowing for regeneration and reuse. Electrochemical regeneration is especially relevant for precious adsorbents like metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which can be expensive. It reduces the need for thermal regeneration, which consumes more energy.
•
Sustainable Adsorbents: Sustainable adsorbents sourced from agricultural waste, such as coconut shells, sawdust, or rice husks, are not only environmentally friendly but also cost-effective. By repurposing waste materials, these adsorbents contribute to a circular economy while effectively removing contaminants. This approach aligns with the principles of sustainability and resource conservation.
•
Hybrid Systems: Integrating adsorption with complementary technologies is another critical discussion point. Hybrid systems combine the strengths of adsorption with processes like membrane filtration, biological treatment, or advanced oxidation. For example, combining adsorption with biological degradation can effectively remove persistent organic pollutants. These hybrid systems offer a more comprehensive approach to pollutant removal.
•
Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA): Traditionally applied in gas separation, PSA is increasingly being adopted in liquid-phase adsorption. The cyclical change in pressure conditions facilitates the release of adsorbed contaminants. In addition to its efficiency, PSA can be a valuable tool in applications such as drinking water treatment and wastewater purification [148–150].
•
Scale-Up and Commercialization: As researchers develop novel adsorption technologies in laboratories, scaling up these innovations for industrial and municipal use becomes a challenge. Critical discussions revolve around maintaining the performance and efficiency of adsorption reactors when transitioning from small-scale prototypes to full-scale systems. Factors such as material availability, reactor design, and cost-effectiveness play a pivotal role in the commercialization of these technologies.

In conclusion, these innovations and critical discussions are at the forefront of advancing adsorption reactor technologies. They contribute to the efficacy, sustainability, and scalability of adsorption-based processes across various applications, from wastewater treatment and carbon capture to gas separation and renewable energy storage. Their continued development is essential in the global effort to address environmental and sustainability challenges. Table 1 lists the various carbon-based NMs that are used as adsorbents to eliminate various EPs in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of several carbon-based NMs as adsorbents for wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Over the past years, water treatment has made extensive use of membrane or adsorbent-dependent segregation methods [161,162]. Adsorption originates as inorganic and organic substances attach to an adsorbent material's surfaces as a result of numerous physical and chemical adhesive pathways. The rapid adsorption renewal period, low-cost and efficient renders them quite practical in waste water treatment. Moreover, wastewater treatment in the industrial sector is possible owing to the huge surface region, improved sensitivity, and rapid kinetics of NM-based adsorption. Some of the commonly used carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment include CNTs, GO, CDs, and fullerenes [163,164]. In the following sections, the aforementioned nanomaterials have been succinctly explored, each presenting unique advantages and characteristics that render them suitable for various applications in the realm of wastewater treatment.

The emergence of carbon-based nanomaterials has garnered significant attention in modern wastewater management due to the large volume of wastewater generated and the abundance of carbon in the environment. One such carbon-based nanomaterial is carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which are graphene sheets rolled into tubular coils with diameters as narrow as 1 nm [165–167]. CNTs were first discovered in 1991 and have since been extensively studied for their advantageous structural, mechanical, thermodynamic, and electrical properties in various fields, including wastewater treatment [168]. CNTs have been investigated for their potential applications in nanostructured materials, therapeutic devices, optoelectronics, energy storage, pollutant remediation, and adsorption processes, among others. In the context of wastewater treatment, CNTs have demonstrated remarkable performance in the removal of various pollutants from water bodies, particularly heavy metals, insecticides, and dyes. In the realm of wastewater treatment, CNTs have emerged as remarkable materials due to their exceptional adsorption and sorption capabilities. These mechanisms are integral to the efficient removal of contaminants from water sources. CNTs offer an exceptionally high surface area due to their nanoscale structure, which provides an extensive interface for pollutants to interact with. The interactions are primarily driven by forces like van der Waals and electrostatic attractions, which lead to the physical adsorption of contaminants onto the CNT surface. Moreover, CNTs feature a distinctive pore structure, both external and internal, through which pollutants can access. This physical adsorption is size and shape-dependent, as smaller molecules penetrate deeper into the CNT structure, thereby influencing adsorption efficiency. Additionally, CNTs can be chemically functionalized with specific groups like carboxyl or hydroxyl, enabling chemical bonding interactions with pollutants, and further enhancing the adsorption process. Electrostatic interactions play a crucial role, especially when dealing with charged species in wastewater. The surface charge of CNTs determines whether pollutants are attracted or repelled. This selectivity based on charge is instrumental in removing ions or molecules of interest. Furthermore, CNTs possess a feature known as "π-π stacking,” which involves the interaction between their delocalized π electrons and the π electrons of aromatic rings in organic pollutants. This interaction is particularly effective in the case of aromatic compounds, such as dyes, resulting in their physical adsorption on the CNT surface. The hydrophobic nature of CNTs, which repels water, aids in the adsorption of nonpolar organic compounds that share this characteristic. This hydrophobic interaction enhances the removal of pollutants with low water solubility. CNTs also exhibit sorption behavior, encompassing both adsorption and absorption, making them versatile in capturing gas-phase pollutants and smaller organic molecules. The ability of CNTs to be regenerated and reused is another significant advantage. The pollutants adsorbed on their surface can often be desorbed, allowing the CNTs to be employed for multiple cycles. In some cases, chemical reactions can occur between the CNT surface and certain pollutants, a process known as chemisorption. This mechanism typically involves strong covalent bonds and is highly effective for specific pollutants that can undergo chemical transformations on the CNT surface. Moreover, CNTs can be tailored to exhibit selectivity in adsorption, making them invaluable in addressing specific water pollution challenges [169,170].

CNTs offer several advantages in wastewater treatment. They exhibit minimal transmembrane clogging, which is essential for the operation and maintenance of filtration systems. Moreover, CNTs have a rapid equilibrium time, enabling quick adsorption of pollutants from wastewater. Their high mechanical strength ensures durability and longevity, making them suitable for various treatment processes [171,172]. Furthermore, the compatibility of CNTs with other materials allows for their incorporation into hybrid nanomaterials, enhancing their adsorption performance and versatility. The widespread application of CNTs in wastewater treatment is driven by their unique properties and the potential for tailored functionalization. The surface chemistry of CNTs can be modified through various methods, such as functionalization with specific functional groups or the deposition of metal nanoparticles. These modifications can enhance the adsorption selectivity and efficiency of CNTs for targeted pollutants. For instance, in an experiment, to create a suitable adsorption substance for naphthalene, Alamaro et al. [173] coated CNTs containing varying concentrations of Ag NPs using a laser-assisted approach, particularly in a liquid medium. The quantity of decorative Ag NPs over CNTs was used to modulate the laser ablation duration. In this work, the edges of CNTs were equally adorned with round Ag NPs, which had a tiny size dispersion of 29 nm. The pulsed laser ablation technique was predicated on producing a pulse frequency of 7 ns, laser energy of 120 mJ and laser frequency of 1064 nm utilizing a nanosecond pulsed laser. A 7 cm plano-convex optics was utilized to concentrate this laser stream and channel it to the top face of the Ag sheet which was submerged in functionalized CNTs. This laser stream exhibited an optimum spot diameter of around 0.78 mm. The quantity of Ag NPs created and utilized to adorn the CNT-covered exterior of the tube was altered according to the ablation duration, as illustrated in Fig. 11 . By using the PLAL technique, a concentrated laser pulse was aimed at the targeted object in a narrow cross-section, producing a significant-energy laser pulse that was then accompanied by the formation of several concentrated photons under particularly specific circumstances. This situation was created from within a liquid media, enabling the creation of laser-induced plasma encircled by a liquid stack, creating plasma-induced stress, and producing charged microscopic particulates (positively charged) that were approaching the submicron and had an elevated potential for interacting with functionalized components levitated in the encircling substrate during the ablation procedure. As a result, charged NPs from Ag were produced and distributed in the functionalized CNT media to ablate an Ag plate submerged in a liquid media. To create CNTs containing Ag NP decorations, the anionic active spots in the cylindrical aspect of the CNTs were drawn to the cationic charge onto the metal Ag NPs' outermost layer. Consequently, the decorating procedure would not be possible without the functionalization of CNTs.

As can be observed in Fig. 12 , the chemical contents of the produced nanocomposite framework were determined using the EDS investigation. Using an SEM, the elemental composition was examined upon the chosen region (1 μm × 1 μm) at a scaled bar of 500 nm. When Ag NPs were decorated amid functionalized CNTs, the resulting spectra displayed the typical elements comprising C, Ag and O with various proportions depending on the period of ablation, as opposed to CNTs, which only consistently contain the elements O and C. In this graph, the percentage variations of these basic elements were seen. According to this experiment, Ag concentration improves with increasing ablation duration whereas O and C concentrations drop. Additionally, no other elements other than the primary framework of Ag elements and CNTs were present.

Similarly, Abussaud et al. [51] used CNTs that were coated with ZnO NPs to adsorb toluene, benzene, and p-xylene (BTX) from groundwater. In this experiment, wet impregnation was used to carry out the impregnation. Additionally, the impact of adsorbent quantity, beginning intensity, and contact duration was studied in batch adsorption tests. The % elimination was in the following sequence: p-xylene > toluene > benzene for a specific period and quantity. The calibration of the kinetics hypotheses demonstrated that the pseudo-second-order theory, having R2 > 99.4 %, matches the sorption of toluene, benzene, and p-xylene effectively. Adsorption isotherm fitting findings showed that the Sips isotherm paradigm provided the optimum suit (R2 > 99.7 %), and the capability for sorption was as follows: toluene: 105 mg/g; benzene: 70 mg/g and p-xylene: 125 mg/g. This phenomenon was noticed most likely as a result of BTX's increasing molecular mass and declining dispersion. Furthermore, Wang et al. [86] used the oxidizing of an activator and a magnetic NPs alteration to create a novel kind of reusable adsorbent. In addition to inheriting the benefits of MWCNTs (6O-MWCNTs), the novel adsorbent also demonstrates a magnetic behavior and significantly increased adsorption capability, which was advantageous for the magnetic segregation and recuperation of heavy metals. The findings of the Pb(II) specific adsorption show that 6O-MWCNTs@Fe3O4 performed well, having a maximal adsorption capability of 215.05 mg/g; significantly greater than the current adsorption ability of the identical kind of adsorbents. 6O-MWCNTs@Fe3O4 with adsorbed metal ions rapidly obtained excellent segregation from the media when subjected to an additional magnetic field. The combined outcomes of XPS and FTIR characterization demonstrate that the metal ions caused the CO bond in the –COOH moiety to access and change into an ionic bond in the influence of electrostatic and coordination attraction, and the metal ions were also sustainably attached to the surface of 6O-MWCNTs@Fe3O4. The lone pair of electrons in O atoms were more attracted to Pb (II) compared to Cd (II) or Cu(II) in order to produce complexes because Pb's covalent coefficient was bigger (6.41) than those of Cu(II) and Cd(II), which were 2.98 and 2.71. These findings expand on existing conceptual understanding and offer a novel reusable adsorbent for the effective eradication of heavy metal ions presented in the wastewater.

By using a less expensive residue from the steel industry as a substrate for the production of CNTs by chemical vapor deposition (CVD), Yang et al. [174] created a novel type of effective and affordable absorbent devoid of the need for any preprocessing. The created CNT-steel slag composite (SS@CNT synthesis)'s variables were optimized, as well as its Cu(II) and Pb(II) adsorption capabilities were assessed. In this experiment, at 25 °C, a sequence of tests was carried out using a pH of 6.5 for the preliminary media. The contact duration ranged between 15 and 300 min (Fig. 13 a). The adsorption activity was noticeably significant for the first 15 min, then it went down and reached equilibrium after around 90 min. The initial phase of accelerated adsorption revealed that the adsorbent interface had a large number of adsorption pores that rapidly interacted with heavy metal ions. For Cu(II) and Pb(II), the maximal equilibrium adsorption capabilities of SS@CNTs were 132.79 mg/g and 427.26 mg/g, correspondingly. Fig. 13b displays the kinetic model variables and linear fit graphs for those variables. The pseudo-second-order kinetic paradigm represented the adsorption mechanism more correctly than the pseudo-first-order kinetic paradigm, which had R2 statistics of 0.99 for Cu(II) and 0.980 for Pb(II). The elimination activity was suggested to be chemical adsorption because the empirical findings and the pseudo-second-order procedure agreed. This research shows that the SS@CNTs matrix is an effective and reasonably priced adsorbent for heavy metals like copper and zinc when contrasted to other adsorbent materials.

By binding 4th gen aromatic poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) over CNTs and successively depositing Ag NPs, Neelgund et al. [175] created an effective adsorbent known as CNTs-PAMAM-Ag. By calculating the adsorption of two harmful pollutants in water, As(III) and Pb(II), the absorption efficacy of CNTs-PAMAM-Ag was assessed. After 15 min, approximately 99 and 76 % of Pb(II) and As(III) adsorption, correspondingly, were achieved utilizing CNTs-PAMAM-Ag. The sorption of As (III) and Pb(II) was explored by a pseudo-second-order kinetic theory. Thus, the rate-controlling phase in the adsorption mechanism might be the existence of chemisorption caused by the exchanging or transferring of electrons among As(III) and Pb(II) ions and CNTs-PAMAM-Ag. Fig. 14 shows the kinetics curve for the adsorption of As(III) and Pb(II). It was discovered that As(III) and Pb(II) adsorption is time-dependent and develops over the duration. As a result, contact duration is crucial and has a substantial influence on the sorption of As(III) and Pb(II). In the adsorption of As(III) and Pb(II), the behavior was the same. The adsorption rate was considered in the early stages, and until equilibrium was reached, the subsequent stages of the process advanced very slowly. Around 76 % of As(III) and 99 % of Pb(II) were adsorbed in just 15 min. For As(III) and Pb(II), the equilibration times were 70 and 20 min, respectively. The adsorption of As(III) and Pb(II) was minimal following overcoming equilibrium until the monitored duration of 120 min. The active sites that were readily available upon the substrate of CNTs-PAMAM-Ag may be the cause of the earliest stage's quick adsorption. As more As(III) and Pb(II) ions were adsorbed over the active sites over the duration, the sites became saturated.

In their research, Liu et al. [176] examined the effects of various coupling reagents and coupling durations over the wettability of a polyurethane (PU) sponge substrate. The best coupling reagent to create the superhydrophobic sponge was determined to be octadecyl trichlorosilane (OTS). The perfect superhydrophobicity was visible as the contact angle grew from 64.1° to 151.3°. Superhydrophobic sponges having an absorption capability of 14.99–86.53 % of the mass of the sponge material were able to quickly and precisely absorb organic solvents and oil of various densities and viscosities without absorbing any water. Chloroform and hexadecane were employed in this study's research to observe the absorption procedure. Oil-red tinted hexadecane was introduced to water, as shown in Fig. 15 a, and afterward, the OTS-CNTs/PU sponge was immersed into the insoluble oil-water solution. The OTS-CNTs/PU sponge entirely soaked the oil in a matter of seconds. The surface of the water became transparent and clear once the sponge was removed with a pair of pliers. Fig. 15b depicts a sponge extracting chloroform from the water, which has been colored red with oil. A three-stage sponge-air-water interaction could be formed when the OTS-CNTs/PU sponge was submerged in water. The sponge was super-hydrophobic, which prevented water from entering it. Chloroform was quickly adsorbed into the superhydrophobic sponge when it was close by. In a brief amount of duration, the oil-water selective segregation procedure was successfully finished.

For the production of CNTs, Haleem et al. [177] employed the pyrolysis method with Ni/Mo/MgO as a catalyst. The ideal catalysts (Ni/Mo/MgO) proportion for higher production of carbon byproduct was discovered to be 4:1:0.2:1. For improved carbon output, the processing variables were also modified. The catalyst mass was 100 mg, the pyrolysis period was 12 min, the temperature was 825 °C, and the yield of CNTs was satisfactory. In a laboratory batch operation, the ecological applicability of the nanotubes was evaluated in a synthesized chromium media. A UV–visible spectrometer was utilized to determine the absorption of Cr (VI) at 540 nm. The findings showed that by utilizing 8 mg of CNTs with 400 rpm at pH 3 and 180 min of contact duration, up to 81.83 % of Cr (VI) extraction was accomplished. Therefore, it was determined that poultry trash could be a helpful resource for the creation of MWCNTs, which might remove Cr (VI) from the wastewater produced by the commercial leather industry.

Additionally, MWCNTs created by Abualnaja et al. [178] were utilized as adsorbents for the removal of the Ismate violet 2R dye from polluted water. Utilizing a batch technique, the impacts of an MWCNT on the elimination of IV2R were investigated. A number of variables were taken into consideration, including pH, agitating duration, adsorbent quantity, initial dye intensity and temperature. The findings demonstrated that, at an acidic pH of 4, 0.08 g of an MWCNT containing 10 mg/L at 120 min successfully removed IV2R dye. The maximal elimination efficacy for actual wastewater achieved 88.2 % under these operating scenarios. With just 0.005 g of MWCNTs, a significant quantity of dye (about 85.9 %) can be removed from the mixture as quickly as 10 min. Various models were also evaluated at the investigative isotherm information. The most suitable isotherm paradigm was demonstrated using the formulae for error variables in the isotherm paradigm. The MWCNT has the maximum dye adsorption capability, measuring 76.92 mg/g. Additionally, the regression results showed that the adsorption kinetics were suitable exhibiting a pseudo-second degree with a R2 of 0.999. With 91.71 % renewal following three cycles, the MWCNT compound seems to be a novel, effective adsorbent in the field of water remediation.

In order to better understand the adsorption processes, Solic et al. [40] glanced into the effects that the functionalization (oxidation) procedure period (3 h/6 h) had on MWCNTs' capacity to remediate water polluted with reduced doses of Ni(II), Cu(II) and Cr(VI) (preliminary levels 0.5–5 mg/L). Owing to the elevated extent of oxidation, the adsorbent characteristic revealed that the molar proportion of O and C in these substances was marginally lesser for the oxMWCNT6h, but the particular surface regions and mesopore quantities of these substances were very equivalent, indicating that extending the functionalization period had little impact on the physical properties of oxidized MWCNTs (oxMWCNTs). The elimination of Ni(II) and Cu(II) by oxMWCNT was significantly enhanced by raising the pH of the media from Ph 2 to Ph 8, however, the adsorption of Cr(VI) was decreased. The impacts of the media's ionic intensity, though, were far lesser noticeable. They conclude that the delayed adsorption of Cr(VI) over the oxMWCNTs explored has been dominated by a supplemental chemisorption phase in which Cr(VI) has been reduced to Cr, along with the outcomes of incorporating the kinetics statistics to the Weber-Morris and Elowich models. Adsorption of Ni(II) and Cu(II) was induced by surface complexation and electrostatic interactions at the interface of the adsorb/adsorbate. Including adsorption capabilities varying from 1.24 to 13.2 (mg/g), both oxMWCNT6h and oxMWCNT3h offered strong adsorption inclinations for the heavy metals explored.
