This highlights the significant possibility these adsorbents possess in the extraction of heavy metals from wastewater.

Unique MWCNTs were created by Faiyz et al. [179] and were synthesized and characterized using hydroxamic acid derivatives. Following the initial oxidation of the MWCNTs, MWCNT-COOH (A) was then subjected to an oxalyl chloride reaction to produce MWCNT-COCl. The R–NHOH hydroxylamine derivatives might be reacted using the functionalized MWCNTs to generate MWCNTs complexed with MWCNT-CONOHCOMe, MWCNT-CONOHMe and MWCNT-CONOHPh. These substances were investigated as potential novel Pb(II) adsorbents to investigate their chelation capacity. Utilizing 30 mg/L of Pb(II) at 30 °C and 10 mg of functionalized CNT at a pH of 6, Fig. 16 illustrates the influence of contact duration on Pb(II) extraction efficacy. The findings showed that for all four adsorbent materials, optimum elimination was achieved following roughly ∼50 min; afterward, the adsorption stabilizes. Vacant spots were a major contributor to the extremely efficient Pb(II) extraction, and their numbers constantly decreased over the period. The four adsorbents showed exceptional adsorption efficiency for Pb(II) in the manner: MWCNT-CONOHCOMe is followed by MWCNT-CONOHMe, MWCNT-COOH, and finally MWCNT-CONOHCOPh. Remarkably, synthesized MWCNTs (B), (C), and (A) have higher Pb(II) absorption rates than MWCNT-CONOHCOPh (D). This might be a result of the phenyl group's steric action, which lowers the number of binding spots on the generated MWCNTs.

Using the sol-gel process, Verma et al. [46] created magnetic magnesium ferrite (MgFe2O4) NPs (MMFNPs). In this experiment, the NPs were ultrasonically imprinted over the MWCNTs. The materials' potential to remediate wastewater containing heavy metals was examined as synthesized. The Cr(VI) adsorption isotherm onto the as-prepared MMFNC provided the greatest fit for the Langmuir adsorption isotherm framework. In ideal circumstances, the strong adsorption capability of 175.43 mg/g was accomplished at a temperature of 40 °C. MMFNC are inexpensive because they are readily retrieved from the aqueous medium owing to their magnetic character. The MMFNC demonstrated an efficacy reduction for the elimination of Cr(VI) ions of lower than 20 % even following seven continuous adsorption-desorption processes. The suggested strategy for generating an extremely efficient magnetic adsorbent for wastewater heavy metal reduction presents opportunities. Similarly, employing conventional CNT as a precursor source, Silva et al. [180] generated various CNT with altered surface functionality, including CNT, oxidized featuring HNO3 (CNT HNO3) and CNT loaded with nitrogen (CNT N MB) in a pelletizing technique. The biological degradation of the azo dye Acid Orange 10 (AO10) using an anaerobic particulate waste for more than 48 h was studied using the novel CNM as electron acceptors (RM), 0.1 g/L. To confirm the microorganism's activities and the impact of the CNM on the methanogenic function, methane output was also measured. When contrast to the reference without CNM (32.4 ± 0.3 %), the biological elimination of AO10 improved using all CNM (around 90 %). The most effective elimination of biological AO10, 98.2 ± 0.1 %, and an 11-fold enhancement in decrease rate were obtained by CNT N MB. Customized CNT was saturated with 2 % iron specimens, including CNT@2 % Fe, CNT@2 % Fe–N-MB, and CNT@2 % Fe HNO3, in an attempt to receive the CNM magnetic characteristics. With CNT@2 % Fe–N-MB, the superior efficiency of the nitrogen-doped CNT was verified, and the magnetic characteristics of the CNT did not interfere with its excellent catalytic activity. The elimination was not caused by sorption on the CNM because no dye elimination was seen in the abiotic experiments. Moreover, the inclusion of the CNM had no impact on the microorganism's survivability throughout the test or its capacity to produce methane. Considering the hazardous nature of the produced aromatic amines, following a biological mechanism with thermally processed CNT, decontamination was seen.

The adsorption performance of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in wastewater treatment can be influenced by several factors. Understanding these factors is crucial for optimizing the adsorption process and achieving efficient removal of pollutants [181]. Some of the key factors affecting the adsorption performance of CNTs in wastewater treatment are discussed below.
•
Diameter and Length of CNTs: The diameter and length of CNTs can impact their adsorption performance. CNTs with smaller diameters have larger surface areas, providing more adsorption sites for pollutants. Longer CNTs can also offer increased adsorption capacity due to their larger contact area with the wastewater. Therefore, the size of CNTs is an important factor to consider when selecting CNTs for wastewater treatment.
•
Surface Functionalization: The surface chemistry of CNTs can be modified through functionalization, which involves the introduction of specific functional groups onto the CNT surface. Functionalization can enhance the adsorption selectivity of CNTs by introducing specific interactions with target pollutants [182–184]. It can also modify the surface charge and hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of CNTs, influencing the adsorption behavior. The choice and extent of functionalization play a significant role in determining the adsorption performance of CNTs.
•
Purity and Structural Integrity: The purity and structural integrity of CNTs can affect their adsorption performance. Impurities and defects in CNTs can decrease their adsorption capacity and efficiency. It is crucial to select high-quality CNTs with minimal impurities and structural defects to ensure optimal adsorption performance.
•
Surface Charge: The surface charge of CNTs can influence the electrostatic interactions between CNTs and pollutants. The adsorption of charged pollutants can be facilitated by manipulating the surface charge of CNTs through pH adjustment or surface modification. Understanding the surface charge properties of CNTs and the charge characteristics of pollutants is essential for optimizing the adsorption process.
•
Solution pH: The pH of the wastewater solution can affect the adsorption performance of CNTs. The surface charge of CNTs and the ionization state of pollutants are pH-dependent. Changes in pH can influence the electrostatic interactions between CNTs and pollutants, affecting the adsorption efficiency [185,186]. The pH conditions of the wastewater should be carefully considered to optimize the adsorption process.
•
Coexisting Substances: The presence of coexisting substances, such as inorganic ions, natural organic matter, and competing pollutants, can impact the adsorption performance of CNTs. Coexisting substances can compete with pollutants for adsorption sites or interact with CNTs, reducing their adsorption capacity. Understanding the composition and characteristics of the wastewater matrix is crucial for assessing the potential interference of coexisting substances.
•
Contact Time and Adsorbent Dosage: The contact time between CNTs and wastewater, as well as the dosage of CNTs, are important parameters for achieving efficient adsorption. Longer contact times allow for greater equilibration between CNTs and pollutants, leading to improved removal efficiency. Increasing the dosage of CNTs can provide more available adsorption sites, enhancing the overall adsorption capacity. Optimization of contact time and adsorbent dosage is necessary to achieve optimal adsorption performance.
•
Temperature: Temperature can affect the adsorption kinetics and thermodynamics of CNTs. Generally, an increase in temperature can enhance the adsorption rate due to increased molecular motion and diffusion. However, the effect of temperature on adsorption performance can vary depending on the specific system and adsorbate. It is important to consider and optimize the temperature conditions for achieving the desired adsorption efficiency.
By considering and optimizing these factors, the adsorption performance of carbon nanotubes in wastewater treatment can be improved, leading to more efficient removal of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advancements in the use of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: Enhanced Adsorption Capacity: CNTs have shown remarkable adsorption capacity for various pollutants, including heavy metals, organic dyes, pharmaceuticals, and emerging micropollutants. The large surface area, unique structure, and functionalization options of CNTs contribute to their enhanced adsorption performance [187,188]. Additionally, the ability to tailor CNTs' properties through surface modification has further improved their adsorption capacity and selectivity.

Selective Adsorption: The surface functionalization of CNTs allows for selective adsorption of specific pollutants. By introducing functional groups with an affinity towards certain contaminants, CNTs can effectively target and remove specific pollutants from wastewater. This selectivity is advantageous in cases where the removal of specific contaminants is crucial, such as the removal of heavy metals or pharmaceutical compounds.

Regenerability: CNTs can be regenerated and reused multiple times, making them cost-effective and sustainable adsorbents. After adsorption, CNTs can be regenerated through desorption techniques, such as thermal treatment or solvent extraction, and then reintroduced into the wastewater treatment process. This renewability reduces the need for frequent replacement of adsorbents, resulting in cost savings.

Synergistic Effects: CNTs can exhibit synergistic effects when combined with other materials or processes in wastewater treatment. For example, the combination of CNTs with membranes or other filtration techniques can enhance the overall removal efficiency of pollutants [189,190]. The unique properties of CNTs, such as their high aspect ratio and hydrophobicity, can complement the performance of other treatment technologies.

Advanced Treatment Processes: CNTs can be utilized in advanced treatment processes, such as hybrid systems and nanocomposites, to enhance the overall efficiency of wastewater treatment. By incorporating CNTs into membranes, catalysts, or electrodes, novel treatment systems with improved adsorption and degradation capabilities can be developed. These advancements offer potential solutions for the removal of challenging pollutants and the treatment of complex wastewater streams.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations and challenges of carbon nanotubes in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: Cost: The production of high-quality CNTs can be costly, limiting their widespread application in large-scale wastewater treatment systems. The synthesis and purification processes involved in CNT production contribute to their higher cost compared to conventional adsorbents. Efforts are being made to develop cost-effective production methods and explore the use of low-cost CNTs or their composites.

Dispersion and Aggregation: CNTs tend to form aggregates or agglomerates in aqueous environments, which can limit their accessibility and hinder the adsorption process. Achieving uniform dispersion of CNTs in wastewater matrices is a challenge that needs to be addressed to maximize their adsorption efficiency [191]. Surface modifications and the use of dispersing agents can help mitigate aggregation issues.

Potential Environmental Impact: The potential release of CNTs into the environment during their production, use, and disposal raises concerns about their environmental impact. The long-term effects of CNTs on ecosystems and human health require thorough investigation. Strategies for minimizing the release of CNTs and their associated risks should be considered in the design and implementation of wastewater treatment systems.

Compatibility with Existing Treatment Infrastructure: Integrating CNTs into existing wastewater treatment infrastructure can present challenges. Retrofitting treatment plants to accommodate the use of CNTs may require modifications or additional processes. Compatibility and scalability issues need to be addressed to ensure the successful implementation of CNT-based treatment technologies.

Reusability and Regeneration: While CNTs offer the advantage of renewability, the effectiveness of the regeneration process and the potential degradation or alteration of CNTs over multiple cycles need to be carefully evaluated. The regenerative capacity of CNTs may diminish over time due to fouling, aging, or structural changes, affecting their adsorption performance. Strategies for effective regeneration and preserving the integrity of CNTs need to be developed to ensure their long-term sustainability in wastewater treatment.

Mass Transfer Limitations: The efficient adsorption of pollutants by CNTs relies on the diffusion of contaminants from the bulk solution to the adsorbent surface. Mass transfer limitations can occur when pollutants have limited accessibility to the adsorption sites on CNTs, leading to reduced adsorption efficiency. Addressing mass transfer limitations through optimization of adsorbent dosage, contact time, and system design is crucial for maximizing the adsorption performance of CNTs.

Safety Considerations: CNTs are nanomaterials, and their potential impacts on human health and the environment require careful consideration. Occupational safety measures should be implemented during the production, handling, and disposal of CNTs to minimize exposure risks [192]. Additionally, comprehensive studies are needed to evaluate the long-term effects of CNTs in wastewater treatment systems and the potential risks associated with their presence in the treated effluent.

Scale-up and Application Challenges: The successful translation of laboratory-scale experiments to large-scale applications remains a challenge for CNTs in wastewater treatment. The scalability, efficiency, and economic viability of CNT-based treatment technologies need to be assessed. Factors such as process optimization, cost-effective production, and integration with existing infrastructure must be addressed to facilitate the widespread adoption of CNTs in wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite these limitations and challenges, carbon nanotubes continue to hold significant promise as adsorbents in wastewater treatment. Ongoing research and technological advancements are expected to address these limitations and further optimize the performance of CNTs, making them more accessible, cost-effective, and sustainable for the removal of emerging pollutants from wastewater.

Graphene, with its exceptional properties, has emerged as one of the most promising materials for next-generation filtration membranes in wastewater treatment. Its larger surface area, numerous functional units, cost-effectiveness, low sensitivity, good specificity, and excellent permeability make it highly desirable for the removal of a wide range of compounds, chemicals, and ions from wastewater [9,193,194]. Graphene offers a straightforward structure, making it attractive for various applications in the wastewater treatment industry. Graphene oxide (GO), a two-dimensional material, is obtained by chemically oxidizing the graphite sheet. Although GO is more expensive and challenging to produce compared to graphene, it has distinct advantages. GO differs from graphene chemically, and its chemical properties make it suitable for long-term durability as a membrane. GO exhibits enhanced adsorption effectiveness due to the suppression of oxidative defects [195,196]. The 2D layers of GO have nanopores with a diameter of 0.9 nm or less, allowing for precise control over the filtration process. One of the key advantages of using GO as a membrane in wastewater treatment is its exceptional resilience. The chemically inert nature of GO ensures its stability and resistance to chemical degradation, making it suitable for long-term use in harsh wastewater treatment conditions. The durable nature of GO membranes allows for continuous and efficient removal of contaminants from the wastewater stream, leading to improved treatment outcomes. In adsorption, contaminants are drawn to graphene through van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and π-π stacking, making use of the abundant surface area. Chemical adsorption, facilitated by oxygen-containing groups in GO, forms covalent bonds with contaminants. Electrostatic interactions exploit surface charge modulation to selectively adsorb ions based on pH. π-π stacking interactions are particularly effective for organic pollutants. Graphene-based materials also exhibit sorption behavior, encompassing adsorption and absorption. Absorption involves the uptake of gases and smaller organic molecules into graphene's interlayer spaces. The materials can often be regenerated, contributing to their sustainability. Chemisorption can involve chemical reactions between graphene-based materials and specific pollutants, forming covalent bonds for effective removal. Selective adsorption can be achieved through functionalization, enabling tailored pollutant capture. Furthermore, the unique properties of GO, such as its hydrophilicity and tunable surface chemistry, provide additional advantages in wastewater treatment applications. The hydrophilic nature of GO enables better water permeability through the membrane, facilitating the efficient transport of water while retaining contaminants. The tunable surface chemistry of GO allows for functionalization with specific groups or molecules, enhancing its selectivity towards targeted pollutants. This versatility makes GO membranes highly adaptable for various wastewater treatment scenarios [10,34,197–199]. Using an inexpensive freeze-drying technique, Jin et al. [37] created a unique aerogel from egg white protein that was crosslinked using GO. By examining affecting variables including media pH, dosage, contact duration and temperature, the adsorption abilities of the aerogels were examined. The Langmuir isotherm model was utilized to determine the maximal adsorption capability of MB onto the aerogels, which was 91.7 mg/g. Fig. 17 illustrates the impact of PGO aerogel's contact duration upon adsorption at two distinct baseline doses. The uncovered surface region of the MB molecules PGO aerogel and the quick contact with the active region could be credited for the apparent quick adsorption efficiency within just 100 min. The adsorption efficiency then progressively raised till equilibrium. The decline in the ultimate adsorption efficiency may be explained by dye molecules diffusing over extended lengths into the interior apertures of the adsorbent particulates. Furthermore, Fig. 17 shows that the preliminary MB dosage had a substantial impact on the adsorption capability and equilibrium adsorption duration. They expanded from 400 min to 750 min and from 38.2 mg/g to 55.4 mg/g as the beginning amount of MB improved from 15 to 20 mg/L, correspondingly.

In order to eradicate the MB from an aqueous medium, Nayl et al. [200] researched the development of GO@chitosan (GO@CS) composite beads utilizing continual scattering procedures. To synthesize GO@CS beads, 300 mg of synthesized GO was introduced to a media of (2 % CS/2 % acetic acid) in water, and the mixture was agitated for 24 h to produce a homogeneous dispersion. To create the GO@CS beads composition, a peristaltic compressor was used to constantly discharge homogenous dispersion in a 3 % NaOH aqueous medium. For effective cross-linking, the produced GO@CS beads were immersed in the NaOH media for 24 h. It was then extracted using a strainer, cleaned with distilled water multiple rounds, and allowed to air dry before being used again for practical use, an actual specimen was obtained from a dye manufacturer, and Fig. 18 shows the dye intensity in the actual specimen as a proportion of the time. It was found that the manufacturer dye's eradication effectiveness increased with contact duration, and the GO@CS beads could absorb 82.1 % of the manufacturer dye in 135 min showing the efficacy of the developed GO@CS beads.

In batches and column configurations, Diraki et al. [201] demonstrated the production of rGO—Fe2O3 nanocomposite and its use to extract and retrieve dispersed oil from an excessive-salinity oil-water dispersion. Analysis was done on how the adsorption variables affected adsorption capability and elimination effectiveness. Both empirically observed (1213 mg/g) and projected (1301 mg/g) adsorption ability of the rGO-Fe2O3 nanocomposite (rGO-Fe2O3-NC) showed superiority. More than 60 % of the oil was absorbed from the rGO-Fe2O3-NC within 10 min of the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption phase beginning. The excellent removal capabilities having excellent renewal and cycling capability within a brief contact period were proven by column. Furthermore, an emulsified salinity of up to 100,000 ppm increased the adsorption capability, indicating the appropriateness for high-salinity wastewater. Similarly, by using Principal Component Assessment (PCA), Mouhtady et al. [8] presented quantitative and artificial intelligence research to analyze the effectiveness of GO-based hydrogels in removing wastewater dyes. The purpose of this research was to evaluate 35 distinct hydrogels' adsorption capabilities. Alginate GO-based hydrogel increased adsorption capability and improved endurance in basic media, according to PCA. When greater adsorbent dosages were needed, polyvinyl alcohol sulfonated GO-based hydrogels were appropriate. In summary, PCA is a reliable method for identifying variables influencing hydrogel choice for pollutant extraction from aqueous mediums.

To evaluate the adsorption capacities of GO and rGO for a cationic dye, MB, Jahan et al. [45] synthesized GO and rGO from graphite powder. The existence of numerous oxygenated functional moieties over the GO substratum than on the rGO substratum, according to FTIR outcomes, indicates that GO was effectively converted to rGO. To effectively compare the adsorption capacities of GO and rGO for extracting MB, the effects of contact duration and adsorbent concentration were investigated. In just 4 h, GO and rGO both eliminated 98.67 % of the MB dye from the aqueous medium, demonstrating their superior adsorption capacities. GO and rGO each had optimum adsorption ability of 3333 mg/g and 2000 mg/g. The results show that the adsorption of MB upon GO and rGO was facilitated by electrostatic attraction, π–π interactions and H-bonds. The presence of several oxygen-containing functional moieties significantly increased GO's adsorption ability relative to rGO by a minimum of 60 %. Increased MB adsorption was also a result of GO's (9.10 m2/g) enhanced specified surface region contrasted to rGO's (0.60 m2/g) greater specified surface region. A pseudo-second-order kinetics approach that accurately predicted the adsorption mechanism suggested that chemisorption accounted for the majority of the influence over the adsorption of MB through rGO and GO. These outcomes indicated that GO could be referred in comparison to rGO for extracting cationic contaminants from wastewater.

In order to remove cationic contaminants from water, such as MB and mercury-(II) (Hg-(II)), Tene et al. [202] synthesized pure rGO using a green procedure. The environmentally benign synthesis method as well as associated morphological and spectroscopical characterization are all included in this paper. The examined rGO demonstrates an adsorption capability of 109.49 mg g1 for Hg (II) and 121.95 mg/g for MB at 298 K, which is crucial. For MB and Hg (II), maximum adsorption times of 20 and 30 min with corresponding efficiencies of roughly 89 and 73 % were discovered. Measured cationic contaminants were captured on rGO using a combination of chemisorption and physisorption techniques. Fig. 19 depicts the Hg (II) and MB adsorption kinetics over rGO at 298 K with a contact period of up to 60 min. Fig. 19a shows that following 30 min, rGO quickly absorbs MB molecules, whereas, for Hg (II), the equilibrium duration of adsorption was 20 min (Fig. 19b). These findings demonstrate how rGO is more efficient than traditional reference adsorbents at eliminating cationic contaminants from aqueous media. Particularly, the regenerated surface region following the reduction procedure and the existence of oxygen functional moiety is responsible for rGO's efficiency. In order to remove Hg(II) ions and MB, the current work reveals novel insights for cationic contaminant adsorbent centered on oxidized graphene.

Yadav et al. [34] attempted to solve the significant issue of antibiotic hazardous nature. In this research, two widely utilized fluoroquinolone (FQ) antibiotics, Ofloxacin (OFX) and Moxifloxacin (MOX) were effectively adsorbable to circular hydrogel beads made of methionine-functionalized GO/sodium alginate polymer (simplified Met-GO/SA) from liquid media. The morphological properties of GO/SA preceding adsorption are depicted in Fig. 20 A. This picture shows a surface having significant permeability and irregularity. Fig. 20B illustrates functionalized Met-GO/SA with distinct borders and a coarser surface prior to drug sorption. There might be additional active spots on the adsorbent interface as a result of potential amino acid agglomeration which led to the development of small pores. Following antibiotic sorption, the surfaces underwent considerable modifications, becoming finer and exhibiting a thick, intensive coating, as illustrated in Fig. 20C, and D.

The results revealed that, as contrasted to non (GO/SA) adsorbent, functionalized adsorbent (Met-GO/SA) demonstrated a superior adsorption efficacy. Furthermore, it was determined that the pseudo-second-order kinetic framework fitted best for both kinds of adsorbate and that the Langmuir isotherm was the optimum paradigm for describing adsorption equilibrium. For OFX and OFX, the highest adsorption capabilities of Met-GO/SA were 3.43 mg/g and 4.11 mg/g, respectively.

Similarly, Wernke et al. [203] demonstrated the impregnation procedure of the Ag/CuO NPs in the activated carbon (AC) substate and the usage of OGR-Ag/CuO in vegetal source AC to extract cephalexin in a batch manner. Both components attained equilibrium during the cephalexin adsorption procedure in about 750 min. On a view of the analyzed components, the adsorbent CA-OGR-NPsAg/CuO had a strong adsorption capability i.e., 756.8 mg/g. The carbon OGR-NPsAg/CuO was more effective in removing cephalexin at reduced cephalexin content ranges while having a lesser maximum capability (95.82 mg/g) than the CA-NPsAg/CuO (557.7 mg/g). Compact Ag/CuO NPs aggregates were seen on the substrates of the studied adsorbents, which displayed permeable and uneven morphologies with infinite pore diameters across the carbon substrates. These findings might imply that the recommended substances could be utilized to remediate water and wastewater to remove cephalexin before being discharged into the environment.

In order to remediate the Th(IV) and U(VI) ions in water, Ligamdinne et al. [204] created a polyvinyl alcohol-GO composite (PGO-Gd) that was adorned with gadolinium oxide. Both metal ions were successfully adsorbed by the PGO-Gd composite, having adsorption capabilities of 427.50 and 455.0 mg/g at pH 4.0. Supplemental research was done to examine the in-situ potential application of PGO-Gd for surface water treatment by assessing the adsorption reliability of the metal through kinetic experimentations. The aquifers surged with 10 mg/L of Th(IV) and U(VI) for the consistency studies, and 0.005 g of adsorbent was introduced. Five milliliters of each culture medium were then gathered at various times, and sifted, and the remnant intensity was calculated using ICP-OES assessment. U(VI) remnant intensity in treated wastewater did not meet EPA benchmarks, implying the impact of other correlate ions, which led to a decrease in U(VI) binding than those of Th (Fig. 21 ), whilst Th (IV) expulsion attained the EPA standard extent in comparison to U(VI) expulsion within 5 min. Furthermore, when mixed metals (U(VI) and Th (VI)) (each polluted at 10 mg/L) were added to the aquifers, neither Th(IV) adsorption nor U(VI) adsorption was impacted. These findings imply that both metal ions will only selectively sorb under the current testing conditions.

Using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process, Zhu et al. [12] created an extremely effective adsorbent for the blend of GO and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) to extract MB. The as-produced CMC/GO composite aerogel exhibits a mesoporous morphology, a significant specific surface region of 800.85 m−2 g−1, and a mean porosity diameter of 30 nm. Additionally, the CMC/GO composite aerogel exhibited a notable degree of specificity for dye adsorption, particularly for MB, where its adsorption rate reached 244.99 mg/g exhibiting exceptional renewability for more than nine periods. Due to its excellent efficacy, renewability, and environmental friendliness, the produced CMC/GO composite aerogel might be a successful adsorbent for dyes. Similarly, for the extraction of commonly used pharmaceuticals including ciprofloxacin, diclofenac, atenolol, carbamazepine, gemfibrozil, and ibuprofen, Khalil et al. [205] assessed the effectiveness of PG as a filter media. Various reactive layering arrangements, bed loading elevations (5–45 mm), filter diameters (18–40 mm inner diameter), adsorbent doses (100-500 mg-PG), and watercourses were all parameters examined in these column investigations. Following its usage as a filter media to remove medicines at significant quantities (10.5 ± 0.5 mg/L) and residual levels (1 mg/L), sustained PG production was completed. These results showed that for the eradication of the majority of the EPs, the double-layered PG-sand column functioned better than a PG single-layered arrangement. By enhancing PG doses and the thickness and breadth of the filter bed, EPs were removed from their medium more effectively. The remediation of greywater polluted with EPs was not significantly impacted by the negative interaction brought on by competitive water components, despite the fact that combined medicinal media from several water bodies were harmed. These results imply that the extraction of developing pharmaceutical pollutants from water and wastewater might be accomplished using PG, an extremely effective filter media.

In order to eliminate polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and their oxygen derivatives (OPAHs) from a water media, Song et al. [206] created nitrogen-doped rGO (NRGO) using a hydrothermal process. NRGO displayed excellent adsorption effectiveness to 2-methyl anthraquinone (2-MAQ) and anthracene (ANT), as well as significant mesoporosity (72.9 %) and hydrophobicity. At 298 K, pH 7, and preliminary content of 1 mg/L, the adsorption capability of 9.29 mg/g for 2-MAQ and NRGO was 5.77 mg/g for ANT. This corresponds to enhancements in adsorption of more than 2.76 and 2.29-fold, correspondingly, over GO and 1.4-fold over rGO. Depending on the characterization data and adsorption studies, it was feasible that hydrophobic impacts, intraparticle dispersion and π-π interaction were all engaged in the adsorption phase at the same time. Additionally, ethanol might successfully restore NRGO. This paper offers concepts for the ecological applicability of NRGO and its possible utility in the treatment of wastewater that comprises minimal quantities of OPAHs and PAHs.

To increase the ability to remove Cu and Al from acidic waters, Rodriguez et al. [207] researched the functionalization of GO using ZnO NPs. Different pH levels were used in the research. While the pH of zero charges (pHPZC) was greater for GO/ZnO (5.57) than for GO in both instances, embellished GO (GO/ZnO) nanocomposites demonstrated an enhancement in the efficient removal contrast to individual GO (3.98). The greatest elimination capabilities for Al and Cu in adsorption studies without pH modification were 29.1 mg/g and 45.5 mg/g, correspondingly. Al and Cu, the examined cations, had maximal elimination rates that exceeded 88 %. Additionally, utilizing GO/ZnO as an adsorbent, the maximal sorption capabilities for Al and Cu were 19.9 mg/g and 33.5 mg/g, correspondingly, in a more acidic environment (pH 4). Additionally, for Al and Cu, the elimination rates were 95.6 and 92.9 %, respectively. This demonstrates that even when the pH was unfavorable in terms of electrostatic attraction for cations, decorating with ZnO NPs is a promising alternative for enhancing the adsorption capabilities of GO for Cu elimination and to a reduced degree for Al. These results advance our knowledge of the capability and efficiency of GO functionalization using ZnO NPs to remediate acidic environments polluted with heavy metals and its promise for treating wastewater.

Aboelfetoh et al. [208] used an in situ reduction technique with green tea aqueous extracts to immobilize AgNPs over nanosheets of GO. Both the decorating of rGO with AgNPs and the conversion of GO to rGO took place concurrently. AgNPs having a crystalline configuration and a mean particle diameter of 25 × 3 nm were used to adorn the GO nanosheets. The kind of the resultant nanocomposites was strongly influenced by the extract quantity and temperature. Whereas the rGO/Ag nanocomposite was only produced at a greater temperature (95 °C) using a greater extract content, the GO/Ag nanocomposites were produced at ambient temperature (27 °C) utilizing varied extract intensity (2–18 %(v/v)). The MB dye was chosen as a contaminant to investigate the nanocomposites' capacity for adsorption. The observations on adsorption were consistent with the Langmuir isotherm, which predicts maximal adsorption of 633 mg/g. The dynamics of adsorption show excellent pseudo-second-order conformity. Thermodynamic characteristics confirmed that adsorption was spontaneous and thermolysis. Due to their high output and possibility for reuse, the nanocomposites may be utilized as efficient and environmentally acceptable adsorbents in wastewater treatment.

Utilizing a viable procedure, Pervez et al. [209] created a unique Fe3O4-infused GO (Fe3O4@GO)-activated persulfate solution (Fe3O4@GO + K2S2O8) that was evaluated on practical wastewater comprising dye contaminants. The biodegradability of Rhodamine B (RhB) was greatly improved to a value of about 95 % in the context of the PS-activated mechanism contrasted to that of Fe3O4 (about 25 %). Throughout further recirculating trials, this unit retained its catalytic performance and endurance with a small quantity of iron excreted. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) studies were used to precisely identify the deterioration stages, and substrate-bound SO4 was discovered to be the primary radical responsible for RhB breakdown. In addition, the Fe3O4@GO + K2S2O8 platform's RhB breakdown process was explored. Fe3+ illustrates a potential persulfate activation pathway of RhB. Additionally, the direct transformation of SO4• via the hydrolysis process with H2O revealed •OH radicals. According to these findings, transition metals were essential in producing strong radicals, which increased the degradation efficacy. Fig. 22 depicts a potential RhB activation process in the Fe3O4@GO + K2S2O8 complex.

With the possibility of using rGO, Arias et al. [210] utilized a modular and environmentally benign process to remove MB from water sources. To effectively illustrate the sorption of MB, the current work comprised the green methodology to make rGO and the corresponding morphological and spectroscopical descriptors, as well as numerous kinetics, isotherm models, and thermodynamic studies. The adsorption kinetics of MB over rGO were ascribed using the pseudo-second-order framework, which predicted an average period of 30 min. For this synthesis, Sigma Aldrich provided graphite powder (150 m, 99.99 %), KMnO4 (potassium permanganate), H2SO4 (sulfuric acid) and MB (C16H18N3ClS, CAS Number (St. Louis, MO, USA). NaOH (40.00 g/mol, sodium hydroxide, 1310-73-2) and H2O2 (30 % hydrogen peroxide) were purchased from Merk (Kenilworth, NJ, USA). As supplied, all chemicals were utilized. Fig. 23 depicts the procedure for manufacturing eco-friendly rGO as well as the adsorption dynamics (electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding) of MB-rGO.

Meanwhile, the Langmuir isotherm model, which predicts a maximal adsorption capability of 121.95 mg/g at 298 K, was a better fit to characterize the adsorption isotherms. Additionally, it was shown by thermodynamic and kinetics calculations that the sorption of MB over rGO may be viewed as a blended physisorption-chemisorption mechanism that was characterized by electrostatic, π−π interactions and H-bonding. These outcomes demonstrate the capability of rGO produced in a green manner to eliminate cationic dyes from wastewater streams.

To extract Ni2+ ions from wastewater, Aldalbahi et al. [211] created unique, environmentally friendly adsorbents called cellulose acetate/GO (CA-GO) nanocomposite using sugarcane bagasse agro-waste. By employing ferrocene and air as a catalyst during the oxidation of sugarcane bagasse, GO metal was created. Additionally, cellulose acetate (CA) was produced from sugarcane bagasse by simply extracting the cellulose and then subjecting it to a series of treatments, including H2O2, acetylation, NaOH, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, and H2SO4 (10 % v/v). By combining GO with CA in the context of calcium carbonate and varying GO quantities, such as 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 wt% compared to the mass of CA, CA-GO was created. The permeable micrographs and elevated surface areas of the CA-GO nanocomposite improve the material's capacity to absorb Ni2+ ions from wastewater. FT-IR and SEM were utilized to examine the structural characteristics of the produced adsorbents. The effectiveness of the CA-GO was investigated in relation to duration, temperature, and the overall concentration of Ni2+ ions in the effluent. The dosage region of 10–40 mg/L, the duration length of 15–90 min, and the temperature region of 25–55 C were studied for the Ni2+ ion adsorption assays. The elimination efficacy of 96.77 % showed a significant increase in the Ni2+ ion adsorption procedure by CA-GO-2. For the optimum fit of the Langmuir isotherm model, the isotherms were observed. Ultimately, the generated CA-GO nanocomposite coatings' adsorption efficiency showed promise as ecological, affordable, and ecological adsorbents for water contaminants.

By constructing the interface of GO with iron particles (Fe3+), Ain et al. [95] employed a straightforward, quick, and easy methodology for the manufacture of magnetic GO (MGO). Utilizing Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (AAS), the effective elimination of harmful heavy metal ions (Cu+2, Pb+2, Cr+3, Zn+2, and Ni+2) was explored. By examining the effects of various variables, including pH (3–9), temperature (25–55 °C), contact duration (10–65 min), and adsorbent concentration (0.002–0.016 g), the adsorption efficiency of MGO was assessed. The maximal adsorption capabilities for Cr (III), Pb (II), Zn (II), Cu (II), and Ni (II) ions were 24.330, 200, 63.694, 62.893 and 51.020 mg/g at pH 6, pH 5.0, pH 8, pH 7 and pH 6. The findings demonstrated the effectiveness of MGO as a water adsorbent and sterilizer.

Through a number of steps, Gong et al. [63] created the unique magnetic composite Fe3O4@SiO2/chitosan/GO/-cyclodextrin (MCGC). The geometry, content, organization, and magnetic characteristics of MCGC have all been well characterized using a variety of techniques. According to the observations, the composite could be swiftly segregated by an exterior magnetic field and exhibits excellent shape and crystalline organization. The process was comparatively simple, and the raw elements that were utilized to manufacture this substance were affordable, accessible, and eco-friendly. They investigated the effectiveness and adsorption process of this substance as an adsorbent for the removal of bisphenol F (BPF) and bisphenol A (BPA) from water. The adsorption mechanism for BPF and BPA over MCGC was discovered to reflect a pseudo-second-order kinetic framework and Redlich-Peterson isotherm in ideal variables, MCGC was shown to eliminate more than 90 % of BPF and BPA in a mixed media (20 mg/L, 50 mL). A mixture of different forces might be involved in the adsorption process for MCGC. Recycling studies revealed that MCGC maintained an over 80 % elimination efficacy for BPF and BPA following five usages, and with real specimen validation, MCGC could be utilized for wastewater treatment. Because of this, MCGC is affordable, ecofriendly, and simple to extract from and retrieve. It also has adequate durability and a wide range of application potential.

The adsorption performance of graphene in wastewater treatment is influenced by several factors that affect its efficiency and effectiveness. These factors include.
•
Surface Area: The surface area of graphene plays a crucial role in its adsorption performance. Graphene has a large surface area, providing ample adsorption sites for pollutants. The higher surface area allows for more interactions between graphene and contaminants, leading to enhanced adsorption capacity.
•
Functionalization: Graphene can be chemically modified or functionalized to enhance its adsorption properties. Functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amino (-NH2) can be introduced onto the graphene surface [212,213]. These functional groups can improve the affinity of graphene towards specific contaminants, leading to increased adsorption efficiency.
•
Porosity: Graphene can exhibit nanopores or micropores, which contribute to its adsorption performance. The presence of pores increases the accessible surface area and provides additional pathways for the adsorption of pollutants. The size and distribution of these pores can affect the adsorption capacity and selectivity of graphene.
•
pH: The pH of the wastewater significantly influences the adsorption performance of graphene. The surface charge of graphene is pH-dependent, and it affects the electrostatic interactions between graphene and contaminants. The optimal pH range for adsorption may vary depending on the nature of the pollutants [214,215]. Adjusting the pH to the appropriate range can enhance the adsorption efficiency of graphene.
•
Temperature: Temperature can impact the adsorption kinetics and thermodynamics of graphene. Higher temperatures generally promote faster adsorption kinetics. The adsorption capacity of graphene may also be affected by temperature due to changes in the solubility and diffusion rates of contaminants. Optimizing the temperature conditions can improve the adsorption performance of graphene.
•
Presence of Coexisting Ions: The presence of coexisting ions in the wastewater can influence the adsorption performance of graphene. Competitive adsorption can occur when multiple ions or compounds are present, affecting the adsorption capacity and selectivity of graphene. Understanding the interactions between coexisting ions and graphene is crucial for effective wastewater treatment.
•
Concentration of Pollutants: The concentration of pollutants in the wastewater directly affects the adsorption performance of graphene. Higher pollutant concentrations may lead to saturation of adsorption sites and reduce the adsorption capacity of graphene. Adjusting the adsorbent dosage and contact time can help optimize the adsorption process for different pollutant concentrations.
•
Interference from Background Matrix: The presence of organic matter, salts, and other substances in the wastewater can interfere with the adsorption process of graphene. Organic matter can compete with pollutants for adsorption sites, while salts can affect the ionic strength and pH of the solution. Understanding the composition and characteristics of the wastewater matrix is important for designing effective graphene-based adsorption systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advancements of graphene in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: Enhanced Adsorption Capacity: Graphene exhibits a large surface area and high adsorption capacity, making it an effective adsorbent for various pollutants in wastewater. Its unique two-dimensional structure allows for a high density of adsorption sites, providing ample opportunities for pollutant removal.

Selective Adsorption: Graphene can be functionalized with specific functional groups, enabling selective adsorption of targeted contaminants [216,217]. By modifying the surface chemistry of graphene, it is possible to enhance its affinity towards specific pollutants, improving the selectivity and efficiency of the adsorption process.

High Chemical Stability: Graphene is chemically stable, resistant to degradation, and can withstand harsh environmental conditions. This stability ensures the long-term effectiveness of graphene-based adsorption systems, making them a durable and reliable option for wastewater treatment.

Regenerability: Graphene can be easily regenerated and reused, reducing the overall cost and environmental impact of wastewater treatment. After adsorption, graphene can be regenerated through various methods such as thermal treatment or desorption, allowing for multiple cycles of pollutant removal.

Compatibility with Existing Treatment Methods: Graphene can be integrated with other wastewater treatment technologies, such as membrane filtration or activated carbon adsorption, to enhance their performance. It can act as a pre-treatment step by efficiently removing pollutants before further treatment processes, leading to improved overall treatment efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations of graphene in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: Cost: The production and large-scale implementation of high-quality graphene can be expensive, limiting its widespread use in wastewater treatment. However, advancements in production techniques and economies of scale are expected to address this limitation over time.

Scalability: Scaling up the production of graphene while maintaining its quality and properties is a challenge. Industrial-scale production methods that can meet the demand for graphene-based adsorbents in wastewater treatment need further development [218,219].

Membrane Fouling: Graphene membranes used in wastewater treatment can be prone to fouling, where contaminants accumulate on the membrane surface, leading to reduced permeability and adsorption efficiency. Mitigation strategies, such as membrane surface modifications or periodic cleaning, are required to manage fouling and maintain the long-term performance of graphene membranes.

Limited Knowledge of Long-Term Effects: The long-term effects of graphene-based adsorbents on the environment and human health are still not fully understood. Research is ongoing to evaluate the potential release of graphene nanoparticles during the adsorption process and their impact on ecosystems.

Environmental Impact of Production: The environmental impact of graphene production, including energy consumption, waste generation, and the use of chemicals, should be considered. Sustainable production methods and waste management practices need to be developed to minimize the environmental footprint of graphene-based wastewater treatment systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite these limitations, graphene shows great promise in wastewater treatment due to its unique properties and adsorption capabilities. Continued research and development efforts are focused on addressing these limitations and optimizing graphene-based adsorption systems for efficient and sustainable wastewater treatment.

Carbon Dots (CDs) have emerged as a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique properties, making them highly attractive for various applications, including wastewater treatment. CDs are typically small carbon nanoparticles with a graphitic core and a surface functionalization of different moieties, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups [220,221]. These nano-sized carbon-based materials are characterized by various attributes that make them valuable in pollutant removal. First and foremost, carbon dots feature a multitude of surface functional groups, including hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amino (-NH2) groups. These functional groups serve as active sites for interactions with pollutants. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, in particular, are conducive to hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions, making them adept at adsorbing charged and polar contaminants. Additionally, the small size and porous structure of carbon dots contribute to their efficacy in adsorption. Their diminutive dimensions translate to a substantial specific surface area, thus increasing the number of available adsorption sites for pollutants in wastewater. Chemical adsorption is another mechanism by which carbon dots effectively remove contaminants. The functional groups on their surfaces can form covalent bonds with specific pollutants. For instance, carboxyl groups are capable of reacting with amino compounds, while hydroxyl groups can establish ester or ether linkages with certain organic pollutants. Furthermore, carbon dots exhibit π-π stacking interactions with aromatic compounds. This type of interaction is particularly efficient in the removal of organic pollutants containing aromatic rings, such as dyes and phenolic compounds.

Under specific conditions, carbon dots can generate electron-hole pairs when exposed to light. These pairs of charged particles can participate in redox reactions, facilitating the degradation of certain contaminants, particularly organic pollutants. Electrostatic adsorption is another valuable mechanism at play. The presence of charged functional groups enables carbon dots to undergo electrostatic interactions with oppositely charged ions or molecules. For instance, amino-functionalized carbon dots can adsorb anionic pollutants through electrostatic interactions. In essence, carbon dots offer a versatile range of mechanisms for capturing contaminants from wastewater. Their diverse functional groups, small size, and unique chemical properties make them valuable tools in the multifaceted realm of wastewater treatment. Whether through chemical bonds, π-π stacking, or electrostatic interactions, carbon dots are equipped to address a wide spectrum of pollutants, underscoring their significance in environmental remediation [44,92,94,96]. By combining two separate reaction spots using a SiCDs-doped mesoporous silica molecular imprinted unit and europium complex unit into an NMs, Jia et al. [222] developed the dual-channel fluorescence sensor (SiCDs@mMIPs-cit-Eu). This approach showed excellent sensitivities to TC and OTC having a LOD of 5 and 16 nM, correspondingly, varying from 0 to 5.5 μM. Visual fluorescence visualization and fluorescence titration experiments were conducted to explore the SiCDs@mMIP-cit-excellent Eu's sensitivity capability for the identification of OTC and TC (Fig. 24 ). Fig. 24a demonstrates that following incorporation of additional quantities of TC, the fluorescence strength of SiCDs@mMIP-cit-Eu at 616 nm improved significantly whereas the fluorescence strength at 450 nm progressively dropped. The quantities of TC were significantly correlated with the proportion (I616/I450) of fluorescence intensities. Fig. 24b shows two linear relationships involving the fluorescence proportion I616/I450 and the quantities of TC in the ranges of 10 nM to 1.5 M and from 1.5 to 5.5 μM. Depending on a signal-to-noise proportion of 3, the LOD was around 5 nM. The chromaticity image produced by the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) displayed the variation in fluorescence intensity of SiCDs@mMIP-cit-Eu at various TC levels (Fig. 24c). It is evident with the Uv light that when the TC intensity gradually enhanced, the detecting system's fluorescence color gradually changed from blue to red (Fig. 24d). Furthermore, this technique may perform instantaneous visual detection while prior approaches typically required pretreatment, which was time-consuming and costly.

Similarly, Aijaz et al. [223] effectively performed sulfathiazole (STZ) extraction from wastewater sources using CNDs-embedded pullulan (PUL) nanofibers. To create M1, M2, M3, and M4 nanofibers (PUL-NFs), the CNDs were added to PUL at 0.0 %, 1.0 %, 2.0 %, and 3.0 %(w/w), correspondingly. For the most effective elimination of STZ, a pH variation of 4.0–6.0 was shown to be ideal. The kinetics sorption results were properly suited by intraparticle dispersion, and Elovich modeling (R2 = 0.82–0.99), whilst the equilibria adsorption statistics were adequately suited by Freundlich, Dubinin-Radushkevich and Langmuir isotherms (R2 = 0.88–0.99). PUL-NFs' STZ adsorption capability improved as more CNDs were inserted in them. The synthesized PUL-NFs had maximal STZ adsorption capabilities in the following sequence: M4 > M3 > M2 > M1 (133.68, 124.27, 93.09, and 35.04 mg/g, correspondingly). The primary STZ elimination processes in an acidic media were the donor-acceptor interactions, π-π electron and Lewis acid-base reaction, while the primary processes in a basic condition were H-bonding and propagation. As a result, PUL-NFs with CND incorporated might be used as an effective, safe, and non-harmful adsorbent to extract STZ from wastewater sources.

Xia et al. [224] developed a straightforward and environmentally friendly way for developing a CD-based dual-mode fluorescence sensor from the biomass waste of wintersweet flowers (FW-CDs) for sequential and semi-quantitative visual assessment of Fe3+ and Cr(VI). The fluorescence durations of FW-CDs prior and afterward introducing Fe3+ and Cr(VI) were investigated, accordingly, to elucidate the specific quenching processes of Fe3+ and Cr(VI) to FW-CDs. It was determined that the mean fluorescence duration of FW-CDs was approximately 6.6 ns, barely changing in the inclusion or exclusion of Fe3+ and Cr(VI), presiding out the possibility of dynamic quenching systems, such as the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) methods. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum was then examined in order to further elucidate the quenching process. Fig. 25 a shows that the UV–vis absorbance spectra of the FW-CDs/Cr(VI) blended media were very compatible with the cumulative Cr(VI) and FW-CDs absorption spectra, proving that no novel material was created throughout the quenching procedure and eliminating the probability of SQE. It was hypothesized that the quenching process of FW-CDs spurred on by Cr(VI) was an internal filter impact (IFE) since the excitation wavelength of FW-CDs had a significant overlap with the absorption spectra of Cr(VI). To additionally support the conclusion, the rectified fluorescence intensity (Fcor) of FW-CDs was computed utilizing the Parker eqn. prior to taking IFE into account. The correction aspect (CF = Fcor/Fobsd) statistics and the difference across Eobsd and Ecor enhanced constantly with increasing Cr(VI) composition, as shown in Fig. 25c, indicating that IFE accounts for a significant fraction of the measured overall suppression efficacy in the fluorescence-quenching method. IFE consequently dominates the procedure of quenching Cr(VI) to FW-CDs. As seen in Fig. 25b, the discrepancy among the UV–vis absorption region of the FW-CDs/Fe3+ blended media and the combination of the Fe3+ and FW-CDs absorption spectra indicates the production of the ground-state complex among the two molecules. The fact that the excitation frequency of FW-CDs barely coincided with the absorption region of Fe3+ was also significant since it shows that the IFE was not the main factor in quenching the fluorescent Fe3+ to the FW-CDs. In particular, the variance of absorption band levels of intensity approximately 3415 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1, which were connected to –OH, –NH, and –COOH, was exhibited to fluctuate. The exceptional sensitivity of the FW-CDs to Fe3+, in particular, might be due to the rapid chelating dynamics and the significant binding affinity of Fe3+ for the carboxyl and hydroxyl moieties over the substrate of the FW-CDs when contrasted to other metal ions. These elements might result in a nonradiative electron transmission from the excited phase of the FW-CDs, dimming their fluorescence. SQE as a whole served as the fundamental technique for quenching Fe3+ from the FW-CDs.

With broad linearity spans from 0.1 to 60 μM and 0.05–100 μM along with having LOD of 0.07 M and 0.15 μM, correspondingly, the FW-CD fluorescent detector demonstrated a remarkable sensibility to Fe3+ and Cr(VI). Thus, by combining dual-functional assimilation and dual-masking techniques, the FW-CD-based dual-mode sensor demonstrated decent simultaneous detecting performance against Fe3+ and Cr(VI) exhibited outstanding sensitivity and great capabilities. Fe3+ and Cr(VI) quantitative analysis in real specimens was efficiently carried out using the devised parallel sensing technology with excellent accuracy and rapid recuperation. In addition, an innovative FW-CD-based fluorescent hydrogel sensor was created and used in the concurrent and semi-quantitative sensory tracking of Cr(VI) and Fe3+ in effluent discharge and Fe probiotics, highlighting the substantial benefit of concurrently and monitoring sites detecting techniques.

Despite using any chemical additions, Sanni et al. [225] produced water-soluble fluorescent CDs using simple eco-friendly microwave pyrolysis of pine-cone biomass as precursors. The synthetic fluorescent pine-cone CDs (PC-CDs) had a circular form, a bimodal particle-size dispersion, a mean diameter of 15.2 nm and 42.1 nm, and a wide absorbance range around 280 and 350 nm, which was explained by an π-π* and n-π* transition. The fluorescent (FL) strength of the produced PC-CDs was greatest at 360 nm for excitation and 430 nm for maximal emission. In spite of the existence of other metal ions, the produced PC-CDs were superb fluorescent sensors for the specific monitoring of Cu2+ in the aqueous medium. Having a LOD of 0.005 g/mL, the FL intensity of PC-CDs was unusually quenched in the existence of Cu2+ ions. This was mostly attributed to Cu2+ ion binding engagements with the enhanced interface functional units on the PC-CDs. It is an efficient, affordable, and sensitive detector for identifying and tracking Cu2+ metal ions in wastewater.

For the remediation of textile wastewater (pH = 7 and T = 25 °C), Cardoso et al. [226] created the catalytic ozonation advanced oxidation procedure (AOP) employing a copper(II)-doped CD (Cu-CD) as the catalysts and polyethylene glycol (PEG) and l-cysteine as passivation and precursors reagents. In addition to a practical pollutant from the dyeing and print sector, four dyes i.e. Orange II sodium salt (O-II), Methyl Orange (MO), Remazol Brilliant Blue R (RBB-R) and Reactive Black 5 (RB-5) were examined. A one-pot hydrothermal process was used to effectively generate the Cu-CD, which has distinct catalytic ozonation capabilities. It has a charge of 3.7 mV, dimensions of 4.0 nm and a fluorescence quantum output of 31 %. The aqueous dye media discolored in a manner that appeared to be 1st-order dynamics, with the respective rate constants (kap in min1): RB-5, 0.177; MO, 0.210; O-II, 0.133; and RBB-R, 0.086. The corresponding evident first-order rate constants (kapc in min1) were determined in the addition of Cu-CD, and the rate constant lacking catalyst (%Inc) increased in accordance: RB-5, 0.709 (301 %); MO, 1.184 (464 %); O-II, 1.002 (653 %); and RBB-R, 0.230 (167 %). Owing to the production of additional radicals, including chlorine oxide and chlorine, as a consequence of the reaction between chloride and ozone, NaCl (at a dosage of 50 g/L) significantly enhanced the speed at which the dye media discolored. The discoloration first-order rate constants without and with Cu-CD (kap = 0.0097 min1 and kapc = 0.012 min1 (%Inc = 24 %) were comparatively limited when taken into account that the authentic textile discharge under study has an elevated carbonate intensity (>356 mg/L), which impedes ozone breakdown. It appears that the Cu-CD, whose surface was coated by a thick and extremely hydrated caramelized PEG covering, increased the breakdown of ozone and the sorption of dye, elevating the deterioration of the material.

Perumal et al. [227] developed nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) using Red Malus floribunda fruits with the aid of hybrid-spherical hydrogel particulates (CGCDs). The produced CGCDs served as HMI-(Hg(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), and Cr(III)) adsorbents for water. The existence of oxygen, nitrogen and carbon characteristics was confirmed in N-CDs having a dimension of about 4.0 nm. Before being utilized as HMI adsorbents, the produced spherical CGCDs were thoroughly characterized. The CGCDs ranged in size from 20 to 300 m, and the extent of inflation was estimated to be 1320 %. Additionally, FE-SEM indicates that CGCDs have a spherical structure. HMI media came in three distinct strengths: 500 mg/L, 1000 mg/L, and 1500 mg/L. In single metal ion complexes, CGCDs effectively removed Hg(II) ions (72 %), whereas, in numerous metal ion frameworks, Hg(II) ions were practically entirely removed (99 %). Furthermore, CGCDs effectively (>70 %) eliminated all metal ions, including Cd(II), Hg(II), Pb(II) and Cr(III), from different environments.

Similarly, the fabrication of luminous nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) via a one-step hydrothermal process was effectively produced by Ge et al. [228] using urea and green tea leaves as nitrogen and carbon sources, accordingly. The N-CDs were discovered to be nearly round, having a mean diameter of 2.32 nm, and to have a large number of nitrogen and oxygen functional units. Under UV light, the N-CDs fluoresced intensely blue, with the highest emission occurring at 455 nm. The fluorescence emission profiles of the obtained fluorescent N-CDs in aqueous media with various quantities of Fe3+ at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm were examined to investigate the sensitivities of the produced fluorescent N-CDs against the sensing of Fe3+ ions. Fig. 26 A showed that the maximal fluorescence emission level of the N-CDs steadily reduced as the quantity of Fe3+ raised, demonstrating that the presence of Fe3+ ions must be implicated in the observed fluorescence quenching. The association involving the maximal fluorescence level and the quantity of Fe3+ ions is shown in Fig. 26B. It was discovered that at a dosage of 1 mM Fe3+, 80 % of the fluorescence of the N-CDs might be suppressed, suggesting that the N-CDs might be employed for the quantitative monitoring of Fe3+ ions. The LOD was predicted to be 0.079 μM and Fig. 26C demonstrates a high linear correlation (R2 = 0.9948) in a broad spectrum from 0.1 μM to 400 μM, demonstrating the viability of using N-CDs as a sensory tool for Fe3+ ions. This N-CDs probe has a number of benefits over many other CDs for the measurement of Fe3+ documented in earlier publications, including better sensitivity (quick responsiveness), a substantially broader linear spectrum (0.1–400 μM), and a reduced LOD (0.079 M). The N-CD probe is more advantageous, and this is more significant. They assessed the variation in fluorescence intensities of N-CDs following the introduction of additional metal ions including Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Co2+, Gd2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Zn2+, and Ca2+ ions, to thoroughly explore the sensitivity of the N-CDs as a bio-sensor for Fe3+. The N-CD aqueous media was introduced to the metal ions, and following being left to stay for 10 min, the eventual content of all the metal ion media was equivalent (1 mM). A measurement of the mixture's fluorescence emission at 360 nm of the excitation spectrum was obtained. The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) has been displayed in Fig. 26D, and it was noted that the introduction of all metal ions apart from Fe3+ ions caused only a very little and insignificant alteration in the emission frequency of the N-CDs, but the introduction of Fe3+ ions resulted in a clear quenching. This suggests that compared to other metal ions, Fe3+ exhibits a significantly larger propensity for N-CDs. N-CDs could be utilized as a particular probe to capture Fe3+.

Furthermore, using gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy and CDs-decorated graphite carbon nitride composites (CDs/g-C3N4), Xu et al. [229] developed an inventive SPME covering for the accurate detection of chlorobenzenes (CBs) in water specimens. In contrast to pure g-C3N4, the CDs/g-C3N4 covering demonstrated better removal ability due to the CDs' augmentation of active moieties. Owing to the functionalities of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing group interaction, π-π layering, and SPME coverings' as-prepared state, the extraction capabilities of these coverings were greater than those of commercialized coverings. The suggested approach, including a comparative standard deviation of 5.3–9.7 % for single fiber and 7.5–12.6 % for five fibers, demonstrates an extensive linearity spectrum (0.25–2500 ng/L), exceptionally minimal LOD of 0.002–0.086 ng/L, and exceptional accuracy under optimum circumstances. Consequently, with increased recoveries varying from 73.4 to 109.1 %, the suggested approach was effectively used for the detection of CBs from actual riverwater specimens. In this work, a brand-new, effective SPME coating substance was created to remove organic contaminants from ecological specimens. Similarly, utilizing levofloxacin and dl-malic acid, Zhao et al. [230] created nitrogen and fluorine co-doped CDs (NFCDs) quickly, cheaply, and in just one stage. During UV illumination, the NFCDs fluoresced intensely green. The optical emission maxima were found at 490 nm following a 280 nm excitation, having a significant quantum output of 21.03 %. It's significant to observe that the existence of fluorine and nitrogen elements in the functional groups on the interface led to quantity-independent and excitation-independent single-color fluorescence as shown by the spectrum observations. Furthermore, using the “turn-off” function, NFCDs were used to selectively monitor Fe3+ and ascorbic acid. 1.03 and 4.22 μM were established as the LOD, correspondingly. The dynamic quenching process and the inner filter impact were used to investigate the quenching processes. This led to the effective development of an NFCDs fluorescent probe having single color emissions for the practical and quick screening of ascorbic acid and Fe3+ in the ecosystem.

The adsorption performance of carbon dots (CDs) in wastewater treatment can be influenced by various factors. Understanding these factors is crucial for optimizing the adsorption process and maximizing the efficiency of CDs as adsorbents. Some of the key factors affecting the adsorption performance of CDs in wastewater treatment are.
•
Surface functionalization: The surface functionalization of CDs plays a significant role in their adsorption performance. Different functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, can be introduced on the surface of CDs to enhance their adsorption capacity and selectivity. The type and density of functional groups determine the interactions between CDs and contaminants, affecting the adsorption efficiency [231,232].
•
pH of the solution: The pH of the wastewater solution can influence the surface charge of both CDs and the contaminants. The adsorption behavior of CDs can vary with pH due to changes in electrostatic interactions. Adjusting the pH can optimize the electrostatic interactions between CDs and contaminants, enhancing the adsorption performance.
•
The concentration of contaminants: The initial concentration of contaminants in the wastewater solution affects the adsorption performance of CDs. Higher concentrations of contaminants can result in increased adsorption capacity up to a certain limit. However, at very high concentrations, the adsorption sites on CDs may become saturated, leading to reduced adsorption efficiency.
•
Contact time: The contact time between CDs and the wastewater solution is an important parameter that affects adsorption performance. Longer contact times allow for more interactions between CDs and contaminants, leading to increased adsorption. Optimal contact time should be determined to achieve equilibrium adsorption and maximize the efficiency of CDs.
•
Temperature: Temperature can affect the adsorption kinetics and thermodynamics of CDs in wastewater treatment. Generally, higher temperatures promote faster adsorption kinetics. However, the effect of temperature on the adsorption performance of CDs may vary depending on the specific system and contaminants involved [232].
•
Interference from coexisting substances: In real wastewater systems, there may be coexisting substances such as salts, organic matter, and other contaminants that can interfere with the adsorption process of CDs. These substances can compete with the target contaminants for adsorption sites or interact with CDs, affecting their adsorption capacity and selectivity. Understanding the composition and characteristics of the wastewater matrix is essential for optimizing the adsorption performance of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advancements in carbon dots (CDs) in wastewater treatment have shown promising potential in recent years. Some of the notable advancements include

ce:list-item.ce:para: High adsorption capacity: CDs have demonstrated high adsorption capacities for various contaminants present in wastewater, including organic pollutants, heavy metals, and dyes. Their large surface area, presence of functional groups, and unique physicochemical properties contribute to their adsorption efficiency.

Selective adsorption: CDs can exhibit selective adsorption towards specific contaminants based on their surface functionalization and interactions. This selectivity allows for the targeted removal of specific pollutants, enhancing the overall efficiency of wastewater treatment processes.

Photocatalytic activity: CDs possess excellent photocatalytic activity, which can be harnessed for the degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater. Under suitable light conditions, CDs can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) that effectively break down organic contaminants, contributing to the remediation of wastewater [233].

Low toxicity and environmental friendliness: CDs are considered environmentally friendly due to their low toxicity and biocompatibility. They are non-toxic and exhibit low cytotoxicity, making them suitable for various applications in wastewater treatment. The use of CDs as adsorbents offers a sustainable and safe approach to wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Despite the advancements, there are also certain limitations associated with the use of CDs in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: Limited studies on real wastewater samples: Most studies on CDs as adsorbents for wastewater treatment have been conducted using synthetic wastewater samples. Limited research has been conducted on the performance of CDs in real wastewater matrices, which may contain complex compositions and competing ions that can affect the adsorption efficiency.

Stability and durability: The stability and durability of CDs under different operating conditions need to be further investigated. Factors such as pH, temperature, and exposure to light may affect the performance and longevity of CDs as adsorbents [234]. Ensuring the long-term stability of CDs is crucial for their practical application in large-scale wastewater treatment systems.

Cost-effectiveness and scalability: The cost-effectiveness and scalability of CDs for wastewater treatment need to be considered. The synthesis of CDs can involve complex procedures and expensive starting materials, which may limit their widespread application. Further research is required to develop cost-effective synthesis methods and explore their scalability for industrial-scale wastewater treatment.

Regeneration and reusability: The regeneration and reusability of CDs as adsorbents need to be explored to enhance their sustainability. Developing effective regeneration techniques to recover adsorbed contaminants and restore the adsorption capacity of CDs will contribute to their long-term viability and reduce waste generation.

ce:section.ce:para: Overall, the advancements of CDs in wastewater treatment offer promising solutions for the removal of contaminants. However, further research and development are necessary to address the limitations and optimize their performance for practical applications in real-world wastewater treatment scenarios.

Fullerenes, also known as buckyballs, are 0-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials that consist of carbon atoms arranged in hexagons and pentagons, forming a spherical structure [223,235–237]. They have gained significant attention due to their unique properties and potential applications in various fields, including wastewater treatment [238–242]. One of the notable advantages of fullerenes is their large surface area, which provides ample space for the adsorption of emerging pollutants (EPs) from wastewater. The high surface area allows for a greater number of EPs to come into contact with the fullerene surface, facilitating efficient adsorption. This makes fullerenes effective adsorbents for the removal of EPs, such as organic pollutants and heavy metals, from contaminated water [117,243]. Fullerenes possess inherent electron-accepting properties, which further enhance their adsorption capabilities. The ability of fullerenes to accept electrons from EPs facilitates the formation of charge transfer complexes, leading to strong interactions and adsorption of contaminants. This electron-accepting nature of fullerenes contributes to their effectiveness in the removal of EPs from wastewater. In comparison to macro-sized fullerene aggregate particles, the nano-sized self-assembled fullerene (C60) collates demonstrated enhanced naphthalene and 1,2-dichlorobenzene (1,2-DCB) adsorption. In addition to taking advantage of the fullerenes' enormous surface area, it is assumed that the sorption of adsorbates upon fullerene occurs because adsorbates have the ability to penetrate voids or surface flaws [121,244,245]. Owing to the appearance of two different kinds of contaminant adsorptions—one at the exterior interface and the other at the interior interfaces of fullerene aggregates—the desorption and adsorption on fullerene indicate the establishment of a hysteresis cycle [246–248].

In order to synthesize functionalized magnetic fullerene nanocomposite (FMFNc) on a wide level, Elessawy et al. [30] presented a simple, eco-friendly, one-phase, and solvent-free method using reusable PET bottle trash as a source and ferrocene as a catalyst and precursor to magnetite NPs. In contrast to assessing the adsorption sensitivity against MB, Acid Blue 25 dye (AB25), and cationic and anionic dyes, detailed characterization was used to explore and validate the proliferation process of FMFNc. Additionally, a technique for evaluating the dye adsorption mechanism by an assessment of the impacts of three parameters including dye intensity, duration, and temperature, was developed using the RSM. Ten sequential adsorption-desorption operations were also carried out to investigate how easily the produced FMFNc could be recycled. The investigation of surface properties during adsorption validated the adsorption process, which was primarily accomplished through π-π stacking interaction, hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction. It was discovered that the FMFNc derived from the “wastes-treat-wastes” concept could be employed as a potential alternative possibility of affordable absorbents.

As an absorptive probe and fluorescent for heavy-metal ions, Ciotta et al. [249] investigated a unique class of GQDs created by oxidizing and releasing the enclosure of C60 buckminsterfullerene. These unfolding fullerene QDs (UFQDs) have a different lattice configuration than graphene because they contain both carbon pentagons and hexagons. Furthermore, the fundamental optical characteristics were comparable to those of conventional GOQDs. However, when exposed to heavy metal ions, UFQDs respond very significantly. For example, simultaneous sensitivity to Pb2+, Cu2+ and As(III) was demonstrated by equivalent suppression of the fluorescence emission and various changes in the transmittance spectra. Pb2+, Cu2+ and their inclusion also caused changes to the UFQD suspension's transmittance spectra, whereas As(III) had no discernible impacts up to a dosage of 100 M. (Fig. 27 ). Cu2+ particularly reduced transmittance at lower wavelengths beginning at 340 nm, exhibiting a noticeable flattening of the peak at 300 nm owing to the n-π * transitions. With reduced dispersion of the 300-nm peak, Pb2+ apparent absorbance rise was noticeably greater and began at extended wavelengths. In all instances, the impact expanded with ion content in a manner comparable to that of PL quenching, i.e., roughly linearly up to 50 μM. As this impact is insignificant between 250 and 500 nm, which covers both the emission and excitation wavelengths of the current study, the increase in apparent absorption might not be attributed to the absorbance of the metal ions in media by them. On the other hand, the metal ion absorption is present beneath 250 nm and accurately rises with ion quantity. In other terms, the interaction between ions and UFQDs is what causes the drop in absorbance in the region of 250–340 nm. In particular, they observe that the PL quenching observations were performed utilizing a normal 90° orientation, decreasing the specimen length encountered by both the excitation and the emission light to 1 mm, in contrast to the UV–vis spectrum, which was acquired with a 10-mm optical pathway. Potential aberrations resulting from transmittance fluctuations and inner-filter impacts that could have an impact on the PL quenching operation could be checked out in this manner. Rayleigh light dispersion, which results from the creation of massive UFQD clumps in the context of metal ions, is a better explanation for the increase in perceived absorption at declining wavelength. By chelating with the –OH and –COOH groups found at the margins and on the interface of nanosheets, the Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions might attach more UFQDs collectively and cause them to aggregate.

Rather et al. [250] discussed the creation of a precise, robust, and focused sensor for the detection and removal of bisphenol-A (BPA) and its green technology-based treatment. For the ultrasensitive screening of BPA, an electrochemical sensor made of fullerene (C60) was created. On the analytical efficiency of the sensor, the impact of measurement factors like C60 loading and pH was assessed. In ideal circumstances, the oxidation peak current had an LOD of 3.7 nM and was linear over the content region of 74 nM to 0.23 μM. The constructed sensor was effectively used to measure BPA in wastewater specimens, and it offers potential analytic uses for measuring BPA directly at trace concentrations. Due to fullerene's poor water solubility (and 1.3 × 10−10 for C70 and 11.3 × 10−11 for C60 ng mL−1), very little study has been done on using it to remediate wastewater. In aquatic environments, one of their key properties is the propensity to develop (meta)stable colloidal aggregate (nC60).
