They have a far higher perceived dispersibility in an aqueous medium than single fullerene molecules, which is due to their aggregation. It must be noted that based on various environmental conditions, including organic or salinity material, nC60 clusters thermodynamically possess a thermodynamic nature in aqueous media. As a result, ecological variables may cause these clusters to transform back into single molecules.

The adsorption performance of fullerenes in wastewater treatment can be influenced by several factors. These factors include.
•
Size and Structure: The size and structure of fullerenes play a crucial role in their adsorption performance. Different sizes of fullerenes can have varying surface areas and pore sizes, which directly affect the adsorption capacity. The arrangement of carbon atoms in the fullerene structure can also impact the accessibility of adsorption sites and the diffusion of contaminants.
•
Surface Chemistry: The surface chemistry of fullerenes, including the presence of functional groups, can significantly influence their adsorption performance. Functional groups on the fullerene surface can enhance the interaction with target contaminants through chemical bonding or electrostatic interactions [251]. The type and density of functional groups can affect the adsorption capacity and selectivity of fullerenes.
•
pH and Solution Chemistry: The pH of the wastewater and the solution chemistry can affect the adsorption performance of fullerenes. Changes in pH can alter the surface charge of fullerenes and the ionization state of contaminants, thereby influencing their interactions. Additionally, the presence of other substances in the wastewater, such as coexisting ions and natural organic matter, can compete with the contaminants for adsorption sites and affect the overall adsorption efficiency.
•
Contact Time and Concentration: The contact time between fullerenes and wastewater, as well as the initial concentration of contaminants, can impact the adsorption performance. Longer contact times allow for more interactions between fullerenes and contaminants, leading to higher adsorption efficiency. However, there may be a point of saturation where further contact time does not significantly enhance the adsorption. The initial concentration of contaminants also affects the adsorption capacity and may require adjustments to optimize the adsorption process.
•
Temperature: The temperature of the wastewater can influence the adsorption performance of fullerenes. Generally, higher temperatures promote faster adsorption kinetics due to increased molecular mobility. However, the specific effect of temperature on fullerene adsorption may vary depending on the nature of the contaminants and the adsorption mechanisms involved.
•
Presence of Coexisting Substances: The presence of other substances in the wastewater, such as dissolved organic matter, salts, and competing ions, can affect the adsorption performance of fullerenes. These substances may compete with the target contaminants for adsorption sites or interfere with the interaction between fullerenes and contaminants. Understanding the complex interactions between fullerenes and coexisting substances is important for optimizing the adsorption process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advancements of fullerenes in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: High Adsorption Capacity: Fullerenes have shown promising adsorption capacity for various emerging pollutants (EPs) found in wastewater. Their unique structure and large surface area provide ample adsorption sites, allowing for the efficient removal of contaminants such as heavy metals, organic pollutants, and pharmaceuticals [252].

Chemical Stability: Fullerenes exhibit excellent chemical stability, making them suitable for long-term applications in wastewater treatment. They are resistant to degradation and can maintain their adsorption properties over extended periods, ensuring consistent performance in removing EPs from wastewater.

Electron-Accepting Properties: Fullerenes possess electron-accepting properties, which enable them to participate in redox reactions with certain contaminants. This characteristic allows fullerenes to not only physically adsorb EPs but also undergo chemical reactions, leading to their transformation or degradation.

Scalability and Cost-Effectiveness: Fullerenes can be synthesized using various cost-effective methods, including arc discharge, laser ablation, and chemical vapor deposition [159,253]. These techniques allow for the large-scale production of fullerenes, making them economically feasible for wastewater treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations of fullerenes in wastewater treatment

ce:list-item.ce:para: Limited Studies: Despite their potential, there is still a limited number of studies on the application of fullerenes in wastewater treatment compared to other carbon-based nanomaterials. Further research is needed to explore and optimize the use of fullerenes as adsorbents for different types of contaminants and under various environmental conditions.

Surface Functionalization Challenges: Surface functionalization of fullerenes can be challenging due to their closed-cage structure. Modifying the surface of fullerenes with specific functional groups can enhance their adsorption performance and selectivity. However, achieving uniform and controllable functionalization remains a technical challenge.

Cost and Availability: Fullerenes, particularly higher fullerenes such as C70 and C84, can be relatively expensive compared to other carbon-based nanomaterials [254–256]. The high production cost and limited availability of these fullerene derivatives may hinder their widespread use in large-scale wastewater treatment applications.

Environmental Impacts: The environmental impacts of fullerenes, particularly their potential toxicity and fate in the environment, are still not fully understood. As with any nanomaterial, there is a need to assess their potential risks and develop appropriate disposal methods to prevent their release into the environment during or after wastewater treatment.

Integration Challenges: Integrating fullerenes into existing wastewater treatment processes can pose challenges. The compatibility of fullerenes with different treatment systems, such as membrane filtration or activated carbon adsorption, needs to be evaluated. Additionally, the recovery and recycling of fullerenes from wastewater require efficient separation techniques.

ce:section.ce:para: Overall, fullerenes show promising advancements in wastewater treatment due to their high adsorption capacity, chemical stability, and electron-accepting properties. However, further research is needed to address their limitations and ensure their safe and effective use in practical wastewater treatment scenarios.

Diamond films, primarily composed of synthetic diamonds, have become a subject of growing interest within the realm of wastewater treatment. These films are valued for their remarkable hardness, chemical stability, and unique surface properties. One of their notable applications lies in electrochemical processes where diamond films serve as anodes. In this method, organic pollutants in wastewater are effectively oxidized at the diamond film's anode surface. What sets diamond films apart in this application is their impressive electrochemical stability and resistance to corrosion. The mechanism involves the release of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) at the diamond film surface, which play a pivotal role in degrading organic contaminants through advanced oxidation processes. Beyond serving as anodes, diamond films are also employed in electrochemical sensors for detecting various analytes found in wastewater, including heavy metals, organic pollutants, and microbial contaminants. These sensors operate based on changes in electrical current or potential arising from the interaction between the analyte and the diamond film surface. The precise mechanism varies depending on the type of sensor and its intended application.

To obtain diamond films for these applications, the CVD process is frequently employed. Here, a gaseous mixture containing carbon precursors is introduced into a chamber with a substrate, ultimately leading to the growth of diamond on the substrate surface. Diamond electrodes for electrochemical wastewater treatment are a common outcome of this process. In addition, diamond films can be engineered to exhibit superhydrophobic properties, meaning they repel water and possess a high contact angle. These coatings are utilized on surfaces within wastewater treatment systems to prevent fouling, reduce biofilm formation, and enhance the separation of contaminants. The mechanism in this context predominantly revolves around preventing water adhesion and facilitating the removal of droplets carrying pollutants. Furthermore, diamond films can be modified to exhibit photocatalytic properties by doping with certain elements or by introducing defects in the diamond lattice. In this case, the mechanism hinges on the absorption of light (typically in the ultraviolet range) by the diamond film, generating electron-hole pairs. These reactive species participate in the degradation of organic pollutants through redox reactions. The application of diamond films in wastewater treatment is a dynamic field of study, with specific mechanisms varying according to the application and adaptations made to the diamond film. These films offer several advantages, such as high chemical and thermal stability, durability, and resistance to fouling, making them a promising choice for enhancing the efficiency of diverse treatment processes. Nevertheless, ongoing research is necessary to optimize their application and gain a deeper understanding of the underlying mechanisms within specific contexts. For instance, in an experiment, He et al. [257] utilized nitrogen and oxygen co-doped porous carbon materials (NOPC-x) to activate peroxymonosulfate (PMS) for efficient degradation of bisphenol A (BPA). In this experiment, through electrochemical characterization, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests, quenching, and PMS adsorption experiments, the degradation mechanism of BPA was clarified. Notably, the findings revealed the complete removal of BPA within 20 min (kobs = 0.30 min−1) in the NOPC/PMS system, dependent on the electron transfer pathway and the presence of a moderate number of surface unpaired electrons, crucial for facilitating electron transfer. This parameter was regulated by adjusting the modifier dosage. Conversely, an excess of surface unpaired electrons hampers PMS adsorption, resulting in a decline in BPA degradation. The study also investigated the stability and regenerability of NOPC in the PMS/NOPC system. After five cycles, BPA degradation efficiency decreased from 100 % to 80.61 %, indicating a decline in NOPC's performance upon reuse. Subsequent analysis of adsorption capacity revealed a significant reduction after five cycles, attributed to the adsorption of degradation intermediates on NOPC's surface, obstructing active sites. However, a promising observation was that the catalytic activity and adsorption capacity of used NOPC was fully restored through calcination at 400 °C in an N2 atmosphere for 2 h. This rejuvenation results from the removal of surface-adsorbed degradation products. Furthermore, the study explored the impact of dissolved organic matter (DOM) derived from NOPC on total organic carbon (TOC) removal, which was found to be negligible. The exceptional catalytic activity of NOPC in achieving 72.30 % TOC removal of BPA within 20 min was attributed to its remarkable properties, such as higher specific surface area, graphitic nitrogen content, large surface area, and optimal pore structure. These attributes facilitate mass transfer between the catalyst and PMS and promote efficient electron transport. Similarly, Park et al. [258] presented an innovative approach to address the high-cost challenges associated with the electrochemical degradation of recalcitrant organic pollutants. The strategy involved the utilization of a boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrode to enhance charge transfer capability and expedite the electrochemical oxidation of compounds like 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). In this investigation, to ensure the effective performance of BDD electrodes, a thin BDD nanofilm, approximately 400 nm thick, was fabricated on a titanium (Ti) substrate (BDD@Ti). This was achieved using hot-filament chemical vapor deposition under optimized conditions, thereby addressing the issue of BDD delamination from the Ti substrate. Comparing the BDD@Ti electrode with a conventional commercial electrode (C-BDD), which featured a micron-thick BDD film on a niobium (Nb) substrate, revealed a noteworthy advantage. The formation of an inevitable metal-carbide layer at the interface between the BDD film and the metal substrate in the BDD@Ti electrode was significantly thinner than that observed in the C-BDD electrode. The exceptional structural attributes of BDD@Ti were found to facilitate rapid charge transfer and the degradation of both 4-CP and PFOA compared to C-BDD. This enhanced performance can be attributed to the shortened electron pathway from the surface to the conductive substrate. Addiitonally, the critical physicochemical and electrochemical characterizations of the BDD@Ti and C-BDD electrodes revealed that the BDD film on the Ti substrate was ten times thinner than that on the C-BDD electrode. Furthermore, the metal-carbide interlayer between the BDD film and the Ti substrate was approximately nine times thinner than that in the C-BDD electrode. Although the surface BDD film resistance of BDD@Ti was slightly higher, primarily due to the relatively low dopant concentration and the high quality of the BDD film without sp2-carbon impurities, the reduced electric resistance layer (attributed to the nano-thickness of the BDD film and the very thin metal-carbide layer) facilitated the efficient migration of electrons from the surface into the highly conductive metal substrate. Consequently, free electrons generated during the anodic reaction on the BDD@Ti electrode surface could swiftly transition to the current collector through this shortened electron pathway. This unique advantage resulted in a significantly reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct) for the BDD@Ti electrode, making it a promising candidate for cost-effective electrochemical oxidation of challenging organic pollutants.

Vasconcelos et al. [259] addressed the electrochemical degradation of Reactive Black 5 (RB5) dye from textile wastewater using non-commercial BDD film anodes. The study focused on elucidating the degradation pathway of RB5 on BDD films and proposes a degradation route based on the intermediates and byproducts identified through high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. In this experiment, BDD films doped with 5000 or 15,000 ppm of boron were grown on titanium (Ti) plates, creating electrodes denoted as E1 and E2, respectively. Remarkably, total color removal from the RB5 solution was achieved with either electrode, even at a low current density of 50 mA cm−2. Electrolysis with electrode E2 at an applied current density of 100 mA cm−2 led to complete mineralization of the dye solution. The proposed electro-oxidation process resulted in the cleavage of the aromatic rings of the dye, as confirmed by the removal of 91 % of the chromatographic area associated with RB5. This cleavage generated aliphatic compounds, including oxalic acid, which can be readily converted into CO2. The electrochemical treatment resulted in the disappearance of specific bands in the UV-VIS spectrum of the electrolyte, particularly in the visible region and the UV region, corresponding to color removal and the rupture of bonds in the chromophoric azo group (-NN-) and aromatic moieties. The removal efficiency was not significantly different between the two electrodes at current densities of −75 mA cm−2. However, at lower current densities, electrode E2 demonstrated higher efficiency in color removal. Remarkably, the BDD/Ti electrodes remained intact with no cracks or delamination observed after hours of electrolysis. The films retained their homogeneity and adhesion to the Ti substrate, confirming their excellent performance in the treatment of textile wastewater. This study presents an effective and promising approach to address the removal of challenging organic pollutants from industrial wastewater through electrochemical means.

Kuchtov et al. [260] characterized BDD electrodes with varying micro-/macro-structures and their application in treating model water contaminated with azo dye Direct Red 80, combined with chlorides, sulfates, and their mixtures. The research findings revealed that structured silicon contributes to surface enlargement, resulting in up to 10.2 % and even a 34.4 % increase when combined with a structured diamond layer. The electrochemically active surface area of flow-through electrodes was significantly larger compared to planar electrodes, indicating greater efficiency. The flow-through electrode demonstrated the highest removal rate and the lowest energy consumption, being 5.9 kWh m−3, approximately half that of planar electrodes. All electrodes achieved over 95 % decolorization of the model water within 60 min. Further experiments using the flow-through electrode showed a more than 90 % reduction in chemical oxygen demand (COD) within 12 h. Electrolysis in the presence of sulfates was found to be the most economical. During electrooxidation, the aromatic structure of Direct Red 80 undergoes degradation, breaking azo bonds, and resulting in the formation of smaller structures like substituted benzene nuclei or naphthalene. The presence of chlorinated degradation products was attributed to sodium chloride in the electrolyte, but these products were eventually eliminated during the degradation process. The study also identified oxidation products, including the conversion of nitrogen atoms (from –NH2 to NO2) and the potential formation of oxide (NOx) and quinones. The highest decolorization rate was observed in the presence of chlorides, which effectively attack the azo bonds in the dye. These findings underscore the potential of BDD electrodes with varying micro-/macro-structures in the electrochemical degradation of azo dyes in industrial wastewater. Additionally, Acuna-Bedoya et al. [261] introduced a novel approach for the efficient degradation of stubborn pharmaceutical compounds, specifically diclofenac (DCF) and acetylsalicylic acid (ASA), in water treatment. Herein, a groundbreaking three-dimensional electrode reactor (3DER) was designed and assessed, marking a pioneering application of activated carbon monoliths (ACm) fabricated through 3D printing. These ACm structures were incorporated as a third electrode in the electro-oxidation process. Impressively, the 3DER system exhibited exceptional degradation and mineralization percentages, reaching 81 % and 74 %, respectively. Notably, this represented an increase of up to 2.5 times compared to a system lacking the third electrode. Furthermore, the study conducted synergy calculations, revealing a significant 35 % enhancement when employed the integrated adsorption-oxidation technology, in contrast to separate sequential processes. In this research, several critical operating parameters were evaluated, including the initial ACm saturation time (ranging from 60 to 240 min) and the applied voltage (0.6 and 0.9 V). The findings showed that kinetic constants (kapp) were 3.2 times greater when the saturation time was shorter and the voltage was higher. Additionally, ACm regeneration tests were performed to assess the material's reusability during the process, yielding impressive regenerations of up to 99 %. Finally, the study calculated the energy consumption by order of magnitude (EEO) for the most favorable operational conditions in the 3DER, resulting in a value of 2.06 kWh/m3. This implies a remarkable 50 % reduction in energy consumption compared to a system without ACm. Despite the intricate nature of understanding the integrated processes of adsorption and electro-oxidation, this study suggests that compact and well-structured 3D printed activated carbon monoliths can offer a promising alternative for optimizing the 3DER system. The substantial regeneration percentages achieved, up to 99 %, signify a notable improvement over studies using granular activated carbon. This improvement is attributed to the compact structure and morphology of ACm fabricated through 3D printing, allowing for superior energy distribution and homogeneous regeneration. The use of these structures also minimizes damage and mass loss, which is often incurred by the friction between granules in traditional adsorbent beds during solution pumping.

He et al. [262] focused on investigating the potential of BDD film electrodes on porous titanium (Ti) substrates for the electrochemical oxidation of organic pollutants. In this investigation, it was revealed that the incorporation of porous Ti imparts a three-dimensional porous structure to the Ti/BDD film. Notably, this structural modification significantly enhanced the electrochemical effective surface area of the porous Ti/BDD anode, measuring at 8.37 cm2/cm2, which was 3.2 times greater than the two-dimensional flat Ti/BDD electrode. Furthermore, the porous structure of the BDD film on the porous Ti substrate improved the generation capacity and rate of hydroxyl radicals (OH). Additionally, from chronoamperometry curves, it was evident that the step currents on porous Ti/BDD were approximately twice those observed on the flat Ti/BDD electrode. This phenomenon signified the superior indirect electrochemical oxidation ability of porous Ti/BDD. The study also conducted electrodegradation experiments using several typical organic pollutants, including oxalic acid, phenol, sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate, and xylenol orange. The results revealed that current efficiencies on the porous Ti/BDD electrode were approximately 30 % higher compared to the flat BDD electrode, underscoring the enhancement of electrochemical oxidation achieved through the introduction of the porous Ti substrate. Additionally, the study delves into the disparity between the promotion of the chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal rate and the enhancement of OH. These findings unveil that the introduction of the porous Ti substrate amplifies the indirect oxidation process of pollutants on the porous Ti/BDD electrode, thereby providing more active sites for electrochemical reactions. Moreover, the response current was found to be correlated with the type of organic pollutants, with different organics yielding distinct response currents on a specific electrode. These observations demonstrated that porous Ti/BDD electrodes can effectively boost electrooxidation activity against a range of organic pollutants.

In the context of using diamond films for wastewater treatment, several factors influence the adsorption performance of these films. Diamond films, with their unique properties, can be effective adsorbents for certain applications. Here are the key factors that affect their adsorption performance in wastewater treatment.
•
Diamond Film Properties: The specific characteristics of the diamond films, such as surface area, porosity, and crystallinity, are crucial. A larger surface area provides more sites for adsorption, while well-defined crystalline structures can enhance adsorption efficiency.
•
Contaminant Properties: The nature of the contaminants present in the wastewater plays a significant role. Factors like the size, charge, and chemical nature of the contaminants determine their affinity for diamond surfaces. Diamond films are particularly effective at adsorbing organic pollutants and certain heavy metals.
•
Surface Chemistry: The surface chemistry of diamond films can be modified to enhance adsorption. Functional groups or chemical modifications on the diamond surface can tailor it to specific contaminants. For instance, introducing oxygen-containing groups can improve the adsorption of organic compounds.
•
pH of Wastewater: The pH of the wastewater affects both the surface charge of the diamond film and the ionization state of the contaminants. The optimal pH range for adsorption should be determined for each specific case.
•
Temperature: Temperature can influence the kinetics of adsorption on diamond films. While some adsorption processes are exothermic, higher temperatures can enhance desorption, impacting the reversibility of the process.
•
Contaminant Concentration: The initial concentration of contaminants in the wastewater directly affects adsorption. Higher concentrations typically result in higher adsorption capacity.
•
Contact Time: The duration of contact between the diamond film and wastewater is essential. Longer contact times provide more opportunities for adsorption, but excessively long durations may not be practical.
•
Flow Rate: The rate at which wastewater flows over the diamond film can affect the efficiency of adsorption. A slower flow rate allows for more contact time, while a higher flow rate may reduce contact time and influence adsorption.
•
Diamond Film Thickness: The thickness of the diamond film can affect the adsorption capacity. Thicker films may provide more surface area for adsorption, but they can also limit mass transfer.
•
Interference and Competition: Other substances present in the wastewater may compete with the target contaminants for binding sites on the diamond film. This competition can affect selectivity and overall adsorption efficiency.
•
Regeneration: The ability to regenerate diamond films for reuse is essential for cost-effective wastewater treatment. Regeneration methods should be considered to maintain the long-term efficiency of diamond-based adsorption systems.

ce:list-item.ce:para: High Adsorption Capacity: Diamond films exhibit a high adsorption capacity, making them effective in removing various contaminants from wastewater, including heavy metals and organic pollutants. Their large surface area and strong surface chemistry contribute to this high adsorption capacity.

Chemical Stability: Diamond films are chemically stable and can withstand harsh environments, ensuring their longevity in wastewater treatment applications. They are resistant to chemical corrosion and can maintain their adsorption efficiency over time.

Electrochemical Properties: Diamond films possess excellent electrochemical properties, allowing them to participate in electrochemical reactions that can further enhance their effectiveness in wastewater treatment. These properties are particularly useful for the removal of certain contaminants through electrochemical processes.

Surface Modification: Researchers have developed various methods for modifying the surface chemistry of diamond films. These modifications can be tailored to specific contaminants, increasing their adsorption efficiency for target pollutants.

Selective Adsorption: Diamond films can be functionalized to exhibit selective adsorption behavior, allowing them to target specific contaminants while leaving others untouched. This selectivity minimizes the generation of waste and maximizes treatment efficiency.

Regeneration Potential: Diamond films can be regenerated and reused, reducing the overall operational costs of wastewater treatment. Regeneration methods such as thermal treatment or chemical desorption are employed to restore their adsorption capacity.

Environmental Compatibility: Diamond is an environmentally friendly material, and using diamond films for wastewater treatment aligns with sustainability goals. They do not introduce harmful byproducts into the environment during the treatment process.

Production Costs: The synthesis of diamond films can be costly, primarily due to the methods involved in creating high-quality diamond layers. This can hinder their widespread adoption, especially in resource-constrained regions.

Energy Consumption: Diamond film production typically requires high-temperature processes such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD). These processes consume a significant amount of energy, which may not be sustainable for large-scale applications.

Complex Fabrication: The fabrication of diamond films necessitates precision and expertise. It involves complex equipment and skilled personnel, which may limit their accessibility for certain applications.

Limited Contaminant Range: While diamond films are effective for certain types of contaminants, their efficiency may vary for different pollutants. They may not be the optimal choice for all wastewater treatment scenarios.

Lack of Comprehensive Guidelines: There is a need for standardized guidelines and regulations governing the use of diamond films in wastewater treatment. Ensuring the safe and responsible utilization of diamond films is critical.

Limited Reusability: Although diamond films can be regenerated, there are limits to their reusability. Continuous regeneration may lead to wear and degradation of the film's surface properties over time.

Potential Ecological Impact: The release of nanoparticles during the manufacturing, use, and disposal of diamond films may have unintended ecological consequences. Proper risk assessment and management strategies are required to minimize these impacts.

ce:section.ce:para: 3D diamonds, with their intricate three-dimensional structure, have emerged as a compelling choice for wastewater treatment, primarily due to their unique properties and efficient contaminant removal capabilities. These materials possess substantial surface areas, making them exceptionally effective in adsorbing a diverse range of pollutants commonly found in wastewater, including heavy metals, organic compounds, and dyes. Additionally, their chemical inertness ensures stability and resilience when exposed to aggressive or corrosive substances, a vital feature for practical application. The electrochemical activity exhibited by 3D diamonds further extends their utility in wastewater treatment. Their electrochemical properties enable the removal and degradation of specific contaminants through electrochemical reactions. This characteristic proves valuable for targeting and eliminating contaminants that may not be effectively removed through traditional adsorption methods. Furthermore, the surface of 3D diamond materials can be modified to enhance their adsorption capabilities. Surface functionalization allows for the selective removal of specific contaminants and tailoring of the material for optimal performance in varied wastewater treatment scenarios.

Contaminant removal mechanisms associated with 3D diamonds encompass physical and chemical adsorption as well as electrochemical oxidation. The extensive surface area of these materials promotes physical adsorption, where contaminants adhere through interactions such as van der Waals forces, electrostatic attractions, and chemical binding to active sites. Chemical adsorption is another key mechanism, involving the formation of bonds between contaminants and the functional groups present on the 3D diamond surface. Electrochemical oxidation, catalyzed by 3D diamonds, leads to the breakdown of organic pollutants into less harmful byproducts, contributing to their removal from wastewater. While 3D diamonds offer substantial promise in wastewater treatment, several considerations and challenges warrant attention. The production of these materials can be costly due to the intricate manufacturing processes required to achieve their three-dimensional structure. Additionally, many methods for 3D diamond synthesis involve energy-intensive, high-temperature, and high-pressure conditions, which may limit their sustainability for large-scale applications. The complex fabrication processes and the need for specialized equipment and expertise pose additional challenges. The selectivity of 3D diamonds for specific contaminants can vary, and their performance may require tailoring to target pollutants effectively. Furthermore, their reusability depends on their ability to be regenerated without significant degradation of their surface properties. Lastly, the release of nanoparticles during the production, use, and disposal of 3D diamonds raises environmental concerns, necessitating proper risk assessment and mitigation strategies.

For instance, in an experiment, Maio et al. [263] focused on the development of 3D BDD film material on a foamed Cu/W substrate, designated as Cu/W/BDD. In this experiment, during thermal treatment, a cone-shaped structure forms due to BDD's thermal weight loss, ultimately yielding an intermingled macro-micro structure of BDD (3D-BDD1). The intermingled 3D-BDD1 structure exhibited a significantly larger electrochemically active surface area, measuring 7.9 times greater than the 2D planar BDD structure (2D-BDD). Later on, Tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) was chosen as a benchmark to compare the degradation performance of the 3D intermingled structure with the 2D planar structure. Notably, 3D-BDD1 achieved 1.51 times higher TCH removal and a remarkable 5.31 times higher TOC removal compared to 2D-BDD. Furthermore, 2D-BDD consumed 7.95 times more energy per unit of TOC removal than 3D-BDD1, with energy requirements of 28.76 and 3.43 kWh/m³ order-1, respectively. Additionally, fluid dynamics simulations, combined with the distinctive fluid turbulence generated by the 3D structure and its influence on fluid flow, were considered the primary drivers behind the enhancement of degradation performance. Additionally, this article explored ECTOC (Electric Energy per unit TOC) as an important indicator in the degradation process. The ECTOC ranking of different electrodes was observed as 2D-BDD > 3D-BDD0 > 3D-BDD1. Over 120 min, 2D-BDD exhibited significantly higher ECTOC (1.933 kWh/g) compared to 3D-BDD0 (0.392 kWh/g) and 3D-BDD1 (0.243 kWh/g). This increase in ECTOC over time could be attributed to higher initial TCH concentration at the anode/solution interface. As the degradation progresses, TCH diffusion becomes a limiting factor, resulting in decreased utilization efficiency of strong oxidizing substances and, consequently, an increase in ECTOC. Similarly, Li et al. [264] performed electrochemical advanced oxidation processes (EAOP) to effectively eliminate refractory organic compounds in wastewater. This study presents a novel approach involving the creation of 3D-BDD anodes through a combination of metal printing and chemical vapor deposition. The advantages of 3D-BDD technology include lower energy consumption, improved color removal, and enhanced total organic carbon removal, making it a superior alternative to conventional 2D-BDD electrodes. In this investigation, the unique 3D-BDD structure was designed using metal printing and chemical vapor deposition. During thermal treatment, the process resulted in a cone-shaped structure, forming an intermingled macro-micro structure on the BDD electrode. This 3D-BDD structure exhibited a much larger electrochemically active surface area, approximately 7.9 times that of the 2D planar BDD structure. The 3D-BDD anode was demonstrated to be particularly effective in the removal of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), achieving 1.51 times higher TCH removal and an impressive 5.31 times greater total organic carbon (TOC) removal compared to the 2D-BDD electrode. Furthermore, the energy consumption for reducing TOC concentration by one order of magnitude was found to be significantly lower in the 3D-BDD system compared to the 2D-BDD system. A computational fluid dynamics (CFD) analysis highlighted the intrinsic through-holes in the 3D-BDD electrode, which promote the mass transfer of chemical substances. Moreover, electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements confirmed a higher production of ⋅OH on the 3D-BDD anode. The 3D-BDD anode's unique structure, coupled with its increased ⋅OH generation, not only leads to improved organic compound degradation but also offers potential for more cost-effective and energy-efficient wastewater treatment processes.

Saputra et al. [265] focused on the development and optimization of heterogeneous catalysts for the degradation of pulp and paper mill effluent (PPME) using peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. In this experiment, three catalysts, namely ZIF-67 (zeolitic imidazolate framework-67), Co@NCF (Co@Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Framework), and 3D NCF (Three-Dimensional Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Framework), were synthesized and characterized through various techniques, including SEM, XRD and N2 adsorption. Among these catalysts, 3D NCF exhibited exceptional efficiency in generating sulfate radicals for the degradation of PPME. Its catalytic activity surpassed that of Co@NCF and ZIF-67. 3D NCF could efficiently degrade organic pollutants in PPME within 30 min at specific conditions. Furthermore, the degradation of PPME using 3D NCF was found to follow first-order kinetics, with an activation energy of 40.54 kJ/mol. The study also demonstrated that the effectiveness of PPME removal using heterogeneous PMS activation depended on various operational factors. Co@NCF and 3D NCF were selected for optimization due to their superior performance over ZIF-67. Optimization led to the identification of optimal conditions for PPME removal: Co@NCF achieved a removal efficiency of 79.1 % (final COD concentration = 240.7 mg/L), while 3D NCF reached an efficiency of 87.67 % (final COD concentration = 148.98 mg/L). The optimal dosage for 3D NCF was 0.4 g/L catalyst and 1.6 g/L PMS, whereas Co@NCF required 0.35 g/L catalyst and 2 g/L PMS. These conditions ensured the effective activation of PMS, resulting in excellent removal efficiency. Additionally, the study revealed that excess catalyst in the reaction caused self-scavenging of radical sulfate, reducing the degradation efficiency of both catalyst types. The excessive addition of PMS also diminished the catalyst's performance in the degradation process. Furthermore, Park et al. [258] presented an innovative approach to address the high-cost challenge associated with the electrochemical degradation of refractory organic pollutants, focusing on the utilization of a BDD electrode. Specifically, the study investigated the impact of the charge transfer capability of the BDD electrode on the electrochemical oxidation of 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). In this experiment, a thin BDD nanofilm (approximately 400 nm thick) was deposited on a titanium substrate (referred to as BDD@Ti) to mitigate issues related to BDD delamination from the titanium substrate. This was achieved using a hot-filament chemical vapor deposition process under optimized conditions. nIn comparison to a commercial electrode (C-BDD), which consists of a micron-thick BDD film on a niobium (Nb) substrate, the formation of an inevitable metal-carbide layer at the interface between the BDD film and the metal substrate was significantly reduced in the case of the BDD@Ti electrode. The structural characteristics of BDD@Ti allowed for substantially faster charge transfer and more efficient degradation of both 4-CP and PFOA than C-BDD. This enhanced performance can be attributed to the shorter electron pathway from the surface to the conductive substrate. Specifically, the advantages of the BDD@Ti electrode over the C-BDD electrode are as follows.
•
The BDD film deposited on the metal substrate was ten times thinner than that of the commercial electrode.
•
The formation of a metal-carbide layer at the interface between the BDD film and the metal substrate was minimized, with a thickness of only 2 μm, as opposed to the 18 μm thick layer on the C-BDD electrode.

While the surface area of the commercial electrode was slightly larger (approximately 1.05 times that of BDD@Ti), it is anticipated that the unique structural characteristics of BDD@Ti confer significant advantages in terms of electrochemical performance. The thick metal carbide interlayers in the C-BDD electrode act as electric resistance layers, potentially obstructing the fast migration of electrons from the active BDD surface to the metal substrate.

Li et al. [266] introduced periodic porous boron-doped diamond (PP-BDD) anodes, prepared through a combination of 3D printing and hot-filament chemical vapor deposition (HF-CVD) techniques. In this investigation, the PP-BDD anode exhibited superior PFOA degradation kinetics (kapp = 0.022 min−1, normalized rate constant of 6.6 m s−1) compared to conventional 2D-BDD anodes (kapp = 0.006 min−1, normalized rate constant of 1.8 m s−1) at an applied density of 20 mA cm−2. The calculated mass transfer coefficient (km) for perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) towards PP-BDD (2.6 × 10−5 m s−1) was 6.1 times higher than that for 2D-BDD (0.42 × 10−5 m s−1). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements confirmed that PP-BDD generates more •OH and SO4 •− radicals during electrolysis compared to 2D-BDD. The unique periodic pore structure of PP-BDD provided a higher electrochemical active surface area (EASA) compared to 2D-BDD, enhancing mass transfer efficiency and increasing the accessible diamond anode surface area for electrolyte and PFOA molecules. In terms of PFOA decomposition rate, PP-BDD outperformed 2D-BDD. PP-BDD achieved 100 % PFOA decomposition with a kapp of 0.022 min−1 and a normalized rate constant of 6.6 m s−1 within 150 min. In contrast, 2D-BDD achieved 67 % PFOA decomposition with a kapp of 0.006 min−1 and a normalized rate constant of 1.8 m s−1 within 180 min. The trends in PFOA decomposition were characterized by an initial linear phase followed by slower kinetics, suggesting that the electrolytic removal of PFOA was governed by a combination of heterogeneous charge transfer and mass transfer processes. Furthermore, the PP-BDD electrode demonstrated higher defluorination efficiency (88 %) compared to 2D-BDD (52 %) after 180 min of electrolysis. TOC measurements reveal that PP-BDD achieves approximately 80 % TOC removal for PFOA degradation, while 2D-BDD reaches 54 % TOC removal. These results highlight the remarkable efficiency of the PP-BDD electrode in the substantial decomposition of PFOA to CO2. Similarly, Kuchtova et al. [260] focused on characterizing structurally distinct boron-doped diamond electrodes and their effectiveness in treating water contaminated with the azo dye Direct Red 80 in the presence of chlorides, sulfates, and their combination. The degradation pathway was elucidated based on the identification of degradation products using liquid chromatography and high-resolution mass spectrometry (LC-HRMS), alongside the determination of adsorbable organically bound halogens (AOX) in chloride-containing samples. The results revealed that incorporating structured silicon contributes to surface enlargement, with increases of up to 10.2 % and 34.4 % when combined with the structured diamond layer. In comparison to planar electrodes, the flow-through electrode exhibited a significantly larger electrochemically active surface area. Although the electrochemically active surface area of planar electrodes ranges between 6 and 7 cm2, the flow-through electrode surpasses this area by a considerable margin. The flow-through electrode attained the highest removal rate and displays the lowest energy consumption at 5.9 kWh m−3, approximately half the energy consumption of planar electrodes. On all electrodes, decolorization of model water exceeds 95 % within 60 min. Additional experiments involving the flow-through electrode reveal a more than 90 % reduction in COD over 12 h, with electrolysis in the presence of sulfates proving to be the most cost-effective option. Addiitonally, during the electrooxidation process, the AOX levels gradually decreased. The percentage of chlorinated carbon decreased to 4.2 % in the presence of sulfates after 24 h and 6 % without these anions. The graphical representation of COD reduction over time suggested that the electrolysis was influenced by mass transport control. The number of intermediates formed during electrolysis was significantly affected by the operational regime, with fewer intermediates produced under mass transport control than under current limited control. In the latter regime, organics degrade to CO2 as the final product. It's important to note that mediated oxidation with the BDD anodes is only necessary when the mass-transfer regime limits treatment efficiency. The production of strong oxidants circumvents mass-transfer limitations, thereby enhancing treatment efficiency.

The adsorption performance of 3D diamonds in wastewater treatment is influenced by various factors that need careful consideration to optimize their efficiency. Understanding these factors is essential for harnessing the full potential of 3D diamonds in water purification. Some key factors affecting the adsorption performance of 3D diamonds in wastewater treatment include.
•
Surface Area: The surface area of 3D diamonds significantly influences their adsorption capacity. Larger surface areas provide more sites for contaminants to attach, increasing the overall adsorption efficiency. This factor highlights the importance of precise manufacturing techniques to create high-surface-area 3D diamond materials.
•
Surface Chemistry: The surface chemistry of 3D diamonds plays a crucial role in adsorption. It determines the type and strength of interactions between the material and contaminants. Surface functionalization can modify the surface chemistry to enhance selectivity and affinity for specific pollutants.
•
Contaminant Characteristics: The type and properties of contaminants present in wastewater, such as molecular size, charge, and chemical structure, impact adsorption performance. Different 3D diamond materials may excel at adsorbing specific contaminants due to their distinct surface properties.
•
Solution pH: The pH of the wastewater solution affects both the surface charge of 3D diamonds and the ionization state of contaminants. Variations in pH can alter the electrostatic interactions between the material and pollutants, influencing adsorption efficiency.
•
Temperature: Temperature can impact the adsorption process. While adsorption is typically an exothermic process, changes in temperature may affect the rate and extent of adsorption. The optimum operating temperature for 3D diamonds should be considered for maximum efficiency.
•
Contact Time: The duration of contact between 3D diamonds and the wastewater solution is crucial. Longer contact times can lead to increased adsorption, but the point of equilibrium must be determined to avoid unnecessary treatment duration.
•
Initial Concentration: The initial concentration of contaminants in the wastewater can affect adsorption performance. Generally, lower initial concentrations favor higher removal efficiencies due to fewer interactions between pollutants.
•
Particle Size: The particle size and morphology of 3D diamonds influence their packing density and accessibility of active adsorption sites. Smaller particles may have better dispersion and more accessible surface area.
•
Ionic Strength: The presence of salts and ions in the wastewater can affect the electrostatic interactions during adsorption. High ionic strength solutions may compete with contaminants for adsorption sites.
•
Regeneration and Reusability: The ability to regenerate and reuse 3D diamond adsorbents without significant loss of performance is essential for cost-effective and sustainable wastewater treatment.

ce:list-item.ce:para: High Adsorption Capacity: One of the significant advancements in using 3D diamonds is their exceptional adsorption capacity. Their large surface area and highly porous structure allow for the efficient removal of various contaminants, including heavy metals, organic pollutants, and dyes. This high adsorption capacity makes them suitable for water treatment.

Enhanced Selectivity: Surface functionalization and modifications have improved the selectivity of 3D diamonds for specific contaminants. These modifications can tailor the material's surface chemistry to target particular pollutants, increasing treatment efficiency.

Regenerability: Some 3D diamond materials are regenerable, meaning they can be reused after adsorption, reducing operational costs and minimizing waste generation. Regeneration processes are under development to optimize the material's reusability.

Cost-Effective Production: Advancements in the synthesis of 3D diamond materials have made their production more cost-effective. This makes them a viable option for large-scale wastewater treatment applications, potentially reducing treatment costs.

Environmental Compatibility: 3D diamonds are considered environmentally friendly materials due to their low toxicity and chemical stability. They have minimal adverse effects on the ecosystem when properly managed.

Complex Manufacturing: The production of 3D diamond materials can be complex and may require specialized techniques and equipment. This complexity can increase initial setup costs and limit accessibility.

Particle Size Distribution: Achieving uniform particle size distribution is essential for effective adsorption. Variations in particle size may lead to uneven adsorption and reduced treatment efficiency.

Contaminant Specificity: While 3D diamonds are highly efficient adsorbents, they may not be equally effective for all types of contaminants. Their adsorption performance can vary based on the specific properties of pollutants.
Resource-Intensive Synthesis: Some methods of synthesizing 3D diamonds may require resource-intensive processes, such as high-temperature treatments. This can contribute to higher production costs.
Need for Skilled Personnel: Managing 3D diamond-based wastewater treatment systems requires skilled personnel with expertise in material synthesis, system operation, and maintenance.

Scale-Up Challenges: Transitioning from laboratory-scale experiments to large-scale wastewater treatment facilities presents challenges. Maintaining the material's efficiency and addressing potential issues during scale-up requires thorough consideration.

Safety Concerns: Although 3D diamonds are generally considered safe materials, the potential release of nanoparticles during their lifecycle raises safety concerns. Understanding and managing any ecological and health risks associated with their use is essential.

Long-Term Effects: Long-term studies on the effects of 3D diamonds in wastewater treatment on ecosystems and human health are limited. Further research is needed to assess any unforeseen consequences.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanodiamonds have emerged as a promising solution for efficient contaminant removal in wastewater treatment. These tiny diamond particles, typically measuring less than 100 nm in size, exhibit unique properties that render them effective adsorbents. Their mechanism of action involves several key processes. One crucial aspect is their substantial surface area. Nanodiamonds possess an exceptionally high surface area due to their nanoscale dimensions and porous structure. This extensive surface area provides ample space for the adsorption of contaminants from water, making them highly efficient in capturing various substances [267–270]. Moreover, nanodiamonds can be modified with a variety of surface functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. These functional groups play a pivotal role in the adsorption process, as they enable chemical bonding, electrostatic interactions, and van der Waals forces to attract and retain contaminants. The ability to tailor their surface chemistry enhances their adsorption capabilities.

Electrostatic interactions are another significant contributor to their effectiveness. Nanodiamonds can be engineered to have a specific surface charge, allowing them to attract and bind oppositely charged contaminants. This electrostatic attraction facilitates the removal of ions and other charged species from water. Additionally, nanodiamonds exhibit π-π interactions with organic contaminants. Their carbon structures enable them to engage in π-π stacking interactions with organic molecules, such as dyes and pharmaceutical compounds. This physical trapping within the carbon lattice results in the effective removal of organic pollutants. Nanodiamonds demonstrate distinct mechanisms for specific contaminants. They effectively adsorb heavy metal ions through ion exchange, electrostatic attraction, and surface complexation, immobilizing these metals in the diamond structure. Organic pollutants are removed through π-π interactions and chemical bonding, physically trapping and breaking down their chemical structures. Furthermore, the adsorption of pharmaceutical compounds is achieved through functional groups on nanodiamond surfaces, enhancing the removal of complex organic molecules.

For emerging pollutants (EPs), nanodiamonds offer selective adsorption tailored through surface modifications and functionalization. They can target specific EPs, such as endocrine-disrupting compounds and personal care products. Some nanodiamonds also possess photocatalytic activity, producing reactive oxygen species when exposed to UV light. These ROS contribute to the degradation and removal of certain contaminants. In addition, nanodiamonds can inactivate microorganisms like bacteria and viruses through a combination of physical adsorption, surface interactions, and photocatalytic disinfection. For instance, in an experiment, Raeiszadeh et al. [271] developed a series of novel chitosan/nanodiamond (CTS/ND) composites with varying surface carboxyl groups and concentrations of NDs using a solution casting method. These powdery CTS/ND composites were utilized as adsorbents for a model anionic dye, methyl orange (MO). Their experimental findings demonstrated that the inclusion of NDs with a high carboxylic content (ND-H) into CTS significantly increased the maximum adsorption capacity of pure CTS from 167 mg/g to 454 mg/g. This exceptional adsorption capacity can be attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing groups on the outer surface of NDs, which facilitated interactions with dye molecules through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces. NDs with higher carboxylic content improve dispersion within the CTS matrix and enhance the accessible surface area of NDs and the adsorption affinity of CTS/ND composites. The adsorption kinetics of the CTS/ND adsorbent were well described by pseudo-second-order kinetics, and the Langmuir isotherm model provided an excellent fit to the adsorption data. The MO adsorption capacity of CTS/ND-H slightly increased with higher ND-H concentrations up to 1 wt%, followed by a relative decrease at 1.5 wt%. Prior morphological studies and AFM analysis revealed that the agglomeration of ND in the CTS matrix is promoted at 1.5 wt%, leading to a reduced accessible specific surface area. Consequently, fewer active sites were available for MO interaction, resulting in a relative decrease in adsorption capacity. With the comparison of the equilibrium adsorption capacity of CTS incorporated with UND, ND-L, and ND-H at various concentrations. It was evident that the adsorption capacity increased with higher carboxyl group content of NDs at any concentration. Thus, ND-H, with the highest carboxyl content, exhibited the highest adsorption capacity in the composite. Moreover, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm assumed that the adsorption process was homogeneous and independent of the adjacent sites, whether they were filled or not. In this model, all active sites on both CTS and CTS/ND-H possessed equal adsorption affinity, and adsorbed molecules formed a monolayer on their surfaces, with interactions between adsorbed molecules being negligible.

Lei et al. [272] presented a pioneering one-step method based on aryl diazonium chemistry for the synthesis of benzenesulfonic-modified nanodiamonds (NDs-SO3H), which were employed to remove MB from dyestuff wastewater. The isotherm equations' goodness-of-fit was assessed using the average relative error (ARE). The adsorption kinetics confirmed that the adsorption process followed the pseudo-second-order model, indicating chemisorption. NDs-SO3H exhibited MB removal following the Freundlich model, suggesting a multilayer adsorption process on a heterogeneous surface. Thermodynamic parameters revealed that adsorption was a spontaneous, exothermic process with reduced entropy. Additionally, NDs-SO3H displayed excellent adsorption performance at various initial solution pH levels, indicating adaptability over a wide pH range. The experimental results indicated rapid MB adsorption by the adsorbents within 3 min, reaching equilibrium within 15 min. The adsorption capacity of NDs-SO3H reached 385.27 mg/g with an initial concentration of 250 mg/L, significantly surpassing the adsorption capacity of pristine NDs. In the case of the organic compound methylene blue, characterized by a weak charge, the Langmuir equilibrium constant can be reasonably employed to determine ΔG0. The negative entropy change derived from the slope implies an exothermic reaction, and the decrease in randomness at the solid-liquid interface is indicated by the negative ΔS0. Furthermore, the increasing ΔG0 with rising temperature signifies more effective adsorption at lower temperatures, consistent with the experimental findings. This surface modification of NDs through diazonium grafting technology highlights NDs-SO3H as a promising adsorbent for MB removal and suggests the wider applicability of this unique modification method in environmental engineering. Similarly, Ahmadijokani et al. [273] addressed wastewater treatment issues through a two-step approach: hybridizing UiO-66 MOFs with organic nanodiamonds (ND) and integrating these hybrid nanoparticles into electrospun polymeric nanofibers made of chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). In this investigation, they demonstrated the electrospinning of polymer/MOFs as a powerful technique to create highly efficient adsorbents for water purification, showcasing their ability to remove cationic methylene blue and anionic Congo red dyes. They also explored the impact of nanoparticle type and loading in four nanofiber composites containing thermally oxidized nanodiamond (TOND), ND, UiO-66, and TOND@UiO-66 on dye adsorption capacity. Their findings revealed that even a small loading of nanoparticles in electrospun chitosan/PVA nanofibers (ECPN) substantially enhanced maximum dye adsorption capacity. Particularly, the engineered hybrid TOND@UiO-66 nanoparticles exhibited the best dye adsorption performance, achieving an 80 % increase in maximum adsorption capacity, from 769 to 1429 mg/g, when compared to unfilled ECPN. Moreover, the designed adsorbent displayed notable regeneration ability after six adsorption-desorption cycles. In this research, four widely recognized isotherms (Langmuir, Freundlich, Dubinin-Radushkevich, and Tempkin) were employed to gain further insights into the interaction of dye molecules with the adsorption sites on the nanofibers. The data associated with these isotherms reveals high R2 values for the Langmuir isotherm model in the case of CR removal (ranging from 0.990 to 0.998), suggesting monolayer adsorption of CR molecules on the homogeneous surface of the nanocomposite nanofibers. Additionally, the findings indicated significantly higher maximum equilibrium adsorption capacities for anionic CR dye over the nanofibers compared to cationic MB dye. This was attributed to the substantial role of electrostatic interactions in the dye removal mechanism. The maximum equilibrium adsorption capacity for anionic CR dye over the fabricated nanofibers increased in the following order: ECPN (644 mg/g) < ECPN/T (719 mg/g) < ECPN/N (722 mg/g) < ECPN/U (733 mg/g) < ECPN/T@U (1085 mg/g). This indicated an approximate 68 % improvement in the adsorption performance of ECPN/T@U compared to ECPN.

Yang et al. [274] explored the influence of carbon phase transformation on the catalytic activity of nanodiamonds in persulfate activation. In this experiment, they subjected nanodiamonds to graphitization at different temperatures and examined their persulfate activation capabilities. Their findings revealed that temperatures exceeding 1000 °C, where substantial sp3-to-sp2 transformation occurs within the inner carbon structure, significantly enhanced persulfate activation efficiency. Specifically, nanodiamonds annealed at 2000 °C outperformed benchmark nanocarbons in terms of persulfate activation. Additionally, density functional theory calculations of binding energies, based on slab models constructed by stacking graphitic carbon layers on the diamond plane (Bernal stacking), were consistent with their findings. Isothermal titration calorimetry measurements indicated that the binding affinity of peroxydisulfate decreased with an increasing sp2/sp3 ratio. Consequently, the enhancement in graphitization stemmed from the increased electrical conductivity of nanodiamonds, which correlated with the extent of graphitization. Furthermore, Pan et al. [275] developed an NDs coating on the surface of UiO-66-NH2 octahedrons to create an NDs/UiO-66-NH2 heterojunction photocatalyst using a simple solvothermal method. The synthesized NDs/UiO-66-NH2 composite photocatalysts demonstrate efficient antibiotic degradation performance under visible light irradiation. It's worth noting that the photocatalytic activity of pristine NDs was approximately 55 %, which was due to the tendency of nanoparticles to agglomerate, leading to reduced specific surface area and limited active sites. However, with the introduction of NDs on the surface of UiO-66-NH2, the photocatalytic activity for TC degradation in the NDs/UiO-66-NH2 heterojunction significantly increased compared to pristine UiO-66-NH2. Among the samples, the 3 % NDs/UiO-66-NH2 displayed the highest degradation rate, exceeding 90 % within 120 min. It should be noted that an excessive introduction of NDs could weaken light absorption and reduce the number of active centers, resulting in inefficient contact between reactants and materials and subsequently reducing photocatalytic performance. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of the NDs/UiO-66-NH2 heterojunction can be attributed to three main factors: (i) the introduction of NDs effectively increased the light absorption capacity of UiO-66-NH2; (ii) the construction of the heterojunction improved the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers over UiO-66-NH2; (iii) the enriched functional groups of NDs further promoted the adsorption capacity of UiO-66-NH2. This work expands the application of NDs in constructing high-performance and stable semiconductor composite photocatalysts with visible light responsiveness for environmental restoration.

Ahmadijokani et al. [276] focused on the adsorption capabilities of unmodified NDs and thermally oxidized nanodiamonds (Ox-ND) for the removal of various heavy metal ions, including Fe (III), Cu (II), Cr (VI), and Cd (II), from wastewater. In this investigation, it was found that the adsorption capacities of these ions onto the adsorbents are notably higher, following this order: Ox-ND-3 > Ox-ND-1.5 > ND. The adsorption capacities for these adsorbents were measured at 26.8, 31.3, and 45.7 mg/g for Fe (III), 25.2, 30.5, and 44.5 mg/g for Cu (II), 33.6, 44.1, and 55.9 mg/g for Cr (VI), and 40.9, 52.9, and 67.9 mg/g for Cd (II) over ND, Ox-ND-1.5, and Ox-ND-3, respectively. The equilibrium was reached in 120 min, and the adsorption data fit well with both the pseudo-second-order kinetic and the Freundlich isotherm models. The adsorption process was found to be spontaneous and exothermic. Additionally, to evaluate the interaction between the adsorbate and the adsorbent nanoparticles and determine the maximum adsorption capacity, the obtained isotherm data were analyzed using Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models. The calculated isotherm model parameters revealed higher correlation coefficients for the Freundlich isotherm model for all metal ions. Additionally, the calculated nF values indicated favorable adsorption processes for the removal of the metal ions. These findings lead to the conclusions that: (i) the Freundlich model was a suitable approach to predict the adsorption of Fe (III), Cu (II), Cr (VI), and Cd (II) onto Ox-ND-3; (ii) the adsorption process of the metal ions onto ND was multilayered; (iii) active sites were heterogeneously distributed on the surface of Ox-ND-3 nanoparticles. The maximum removal efficiency for Cd (II) ions was achieved at a pH of 6.9 and a dosage of 4 g/L. These findings highlight that thermally oxidized nanodiamonds (Ox-ND) can serve as versatile adsorbents for the removal of Cd (II) ions from wastewater.

Several factors play a crucial role in affecting the adsorption performance of nanodiamonds in wastewater treatment. Understanding these factors is essential for optimizing their use in contaminant removal processes. Some of the key factors include.
•
Nanodiamond Characteristics: The intrinsic properties of nanodiamonds, such as particle size, surface area, and surface charge, significantly influence their adsorption performance. Smaller nanodiamond particles tend to have larger surface areas, providing more active sites for adsorption. Additionally, tailoring the surface charge of nanodiamonds can enhance their affinity for specific contaminants.
•
Contaminant Properties: The type of contaminants present in the wastewater plays a crucial role. Factors like the size, charge, and chemical structure of contaminants affect their interactions with nanodiamonds. For instance, charged contaminants may be attracted or repelled by the surface charge of nanodiamonds, impacting adsorption.
•
Solution pH: The pH of the wastewater solution can affect both the surface charge of nanodiamonds and the ionization state of contaminants. Changes in pH can influence the electrostatic interactions between nanodiamonds and charged contaminants, leading to variations in adsorption efficiency.
•
Contaminant Concentration: The initial concentration of contaminants in the wastewater solution can impact adsorption performance. At lower concentrations, nanodiamonds may exhibit higher adsorption efficiencies, but as the concentration increases, saturation effects may occur.
•
Contact Time: The duration of contact between nanodiamonds and the wastewater solution is a critical factor. Longer contact times allow for more adsorption to take place, but there may be diminishing returns after a certain point. Optimal contact times should be determined for specific wastewater treatment applications.
•
Temperature: Temperature can influence the kinetics of adsorption. Generally, higher temperatures can enhance the rate of adsorption. However, the effect of temperature on adsorption may vary depending on the specific contaminants and nanodiamond characteristics.
•
Competing Ions: In real wastewater streams, various ions and molecules may compete for adsorption sites on nanodiamond surfaces. Understanding the composition of the wastewater and the presence of potential interfering substances is essential for predicting adsorption performance accurately.
•
Nanodiamond Surface Functionalization: The modification of nanodiamond surfaces with different functional groups can tailor their adsorption properties. Functionalization can enhance selectivity for specific contaminants or introduce new adsorption mechanisms.
•
Regeneration and Reusability: The ability to regenerate and reuse nanodiamonds is a crucial factor in their long-term cost-effectiveness. Regeneration methods, such as thermal or chemical treatments, should be considered when assessing adsorption performance.
•
Wastewater Matrix: The composition of the wastewater matrix, including its ionic strength and presence of organic matter, can affect nanodiamond adsorption. Understanding the specific characteristics of the wastewater is essential for predicting performance.
•
Hydrodynamic Conditions: The flow rate and hydrodynamics within a treatment system can impact the contact time between nanodiamonds and contaminants. Efficient mixing and flow control can optimize adsorption performance.
•
Nanodiamond Dosage: The quantity of nanodiamonds added to the wastewater is a critical factor. The optimal dosage should be determined to achieve the desired level of contaminant removal without excessive use of nanodiamonds.

ce:list-item.ce:para: High Surface Area: Nanodiamonds possess an exceptionally high surface area due to their nanoscale structure. This property provides numerous active sites for the adsorption of contaminants, making them efficient adsorbents for a wide range of pollutants, including heavy metals, dyes, and organic compounds.

Surface Functionalization: Nanodiamond surfaces can be readily functionalized with various chemical groups. This functionalization enhances their selectivity and specificity for target contaminants. Researchers have explored surface modifications to tailor nanodiamonds for specific wastewater treatment applications.

Reusability: Nanodiamonds can often be regenerated and reused in multiple adsorption cycles. This reusability not only reduces the operational cost but also minimizes waste generation, contributing to a more sustainable treatment process.

Kinetic Performance: Nanodiamonds exhibit rapid adsorption kinetics, enabling efficient removal of contaminants within shorter contact times. This advantage is particularly valuable for continuous flow treatment systems.

Low Environmental Impact: Compared to some other nanomaterials, nanodiamonds have shown lower cytotoxicity and a reduced impact on ecosystems. Their use in wastewater treatment poses fewer environmental risks.
Chemical Stability: Nanodiamonds are chemically stable, ensuring their performance under varying pH conditions and in the presence of different wastewater constituents.

Cost and Scalability: While nanodiamonds are effective, their production can be costly and complex. The scalability of nanodiamond synthesis methods is a challenge that needs to be addressed to make them economically competitive for large-scale wastewater treatment.

Health and Safety: The health and safety aspects of nanodiamonds, especially regarding potential long-term exposure and cytotoxicity, require thorough investigation. Understanding their impact on human health and the environment is crucial.

Regeneration Efficiency: The effectiveness of nanodiamond regeneration methods varies depending on the contaminants and operational conditions. Finding optimal regeneration protocols is an ongoing challenge.

Environmental Fate: Understanding the fate of nanodiamonds in the environment, particularly in aquatic ecosystems, is necessary to prevent any unintended consequences. This includes assessing their potential accumulation in organisms and ecosystems.

Contaminant Removal Selectivity: While nanodiamonds exhibit adsorption capabilities for a broad range of pollutants, improving their selectivity for specific contaminants remains a focus of research and development.

Surface Chemistry Complexity: The complexity of surface functionalization can make the selection of appropriate functional groups challenging. Ensuring that functionalization does not negatively impact other treatment objectives is a consideration.

Mechanical Limitations: Integrating nanodiamond-based adsorption technologies into water treatment processes may require addressing mechanical and engineering challenges, such as designing suitable filtration systems and optimizing flow rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Novel discoveries related to wastewater treatment, microbial control and less toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: In the domain of wastewater treatment and microbial control, recent breakthroughs have unveiled a host of novel discoveries that hold the promise of reducing toxicity and increasing efficiency in these critical processes. These advancements are reshaping the landscape of environmental protection and public health. From the development of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) employing cutting-edge materials like graphene-based composites and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) to the emergence of eco-friendly disinfection methods such as ozone and non-thermal plasma, researchers are pioneering innovative solutions. Additionally, the application of nanotechnology, bioremediation techniques, and electrochemical technologies is playing a pivotal role in reducing the environmental impact of wastewater treatment. Herein, we have discussed several such breakthroughs that are poised to drive the transformation of wastewater management, offering more sustainable and less toxic alternatives for the removal of contaminants and microbial control. In a recent breakthrough, Walter et al. [277] determined the effectiveness in decolorizing and degrading two commonly encountered dye pollutants, namely rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) and crystal violet (CV). This study monitored the decolorization of the dyes using UV–visible spectroscopy while quantifying mineralization through chemical oxygen demand analysis. Notably, as a colloidal nanomaterial, the 1D structure exhibited an outstanding maximum adsorption capacity for Rh6G and CV, measuring at 1850 mmol/kg and 1930 mmol/kg, respectively. Moreover, these materials demonstrated the intriguing ability to self-sensitize, enabling them to effectively decolorize Rh6G and CV using visible light alone. This resulted in an average decolorization of approximately 90 % and 64 % for Rh6G and CV, respectively, within a mere 30-min timeframe, assuming an initial catalyst-to-dye mass ratio of 1:1. Moreover, the significance of this self-sensitization behavior in a first-generation material cannot be overstated. Several previous studies involving TiO2 photocatalysts required hours of illumination, sometimes at low pH levels, to achieve measurable decolorization [278–281]. The self-sensitization exhibited by 2D titanates was also far less pronounced than the remarkable effect demonstrated in this study. Even with the incorporation of precious metals like gold and palladium or the functionalization with peroxo-titanium complexes, 2D titanates did not exhibit the level of visible light activity that the 1D lepidocrocite titanium oxide catalyst demonstrated. To the best of our knowledge, there are very few studies that exhibit comparable self-sensitization behavior, with one exception showing over 90 % decolorization of rhodamine B in about 20 min. However, this particular study did not employ TiO2 as an electron mediator but instead utilized periodate (IO4 −) as the electron mediator in an experimental setup with a visible light intensity exceeding 2.5 suns compared to the 1 sun employed in this study. During the reaction, IO4 − was reduced to IO3 −, while the dye underwent oxidation and was ultimately decolorized. Importantly, in the case of a homogeneous catalyst system such as the periodate system, the ability to adsorb any dye was entirely removed. In this study, the kinetics of dye degradation proved significant, particularly considering that the 1D lepidocrocite structures adsorbed nearly 60 % of the dye in solution before light exposure began.

Gao et al. [282] extended the application of iron-carbon micro-electrolysis (ICME) to address electroplating wastewater, which includes surfactants, and pairs it with an anaerobic-anoxic-oxic (A2O) biological process. During the experiment, it was observed that when the proportion of ICME-treated wastewater remained low (at 10 %), there was virtually no significant impact on nutrient removal efficiency. This was gauged based on parameters such as chemical oxygen demand, ammonia, total nitrogen, and total phosphate. As the proportion of ICME-treated wastewater was increased to 50 %, the removal efficiency gradually decreased, although the quality of the effluent water still adhered to discharge standards. This shift in the treatment process led to a notable alteration in microbial communities within the three treatment zones over a 50-day incubation period. Specifically, the abundance of certain bacterial taxa, such as Hydrogenophaga, Chitinophagaceae, and Lentimicrobiaceae, increased in response to the changing conditions, suggesting their involvement in the degradation of refractory organic compounds within the oxic and anaerobic zones. Furthermore, the analysis of functional genes pointed to the adaptation of microbial communities, which appeared to be linked to cell-to-cell communication and the transport of molecules across cell membranes. Additionally, high-throughput 16S rRNA sequencing was the method of choice for studying the shifts in microbial communities before and after the addition of ICME-treated wastewater to the A2O process. The principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) was conducted based on Bray-Curtis dissimilarity among six selected samples. Notably, the first principal coordinate (PC1) was highly correlated with the variations among the samples, explaining a substantial portion (71.40 %) of the genetic variability. PC2 contributed 22.44 % to the variations, and in combination, PC1 and PC2 accounted for 93.84 % of the observed variations. Interestingly, the microbial communities in the sludge samples taken before the introduction of ICME-treated wastewater (ANA-1, ANX-1, and OXIC-1) formed a closely clustered group, emphasizing their strong similarity. However, upon the addition of ICME-treated wastewater and a 50-day incubation, significant changes in the microbial community structures were observed in different chambers. Notably, the distances between the three chambers (ANA-2, ANX-2, and OXIC-2) became substantial, signifying the distinctive shifts in microbial communities within these chambers along unique trajectories. These observations collectively underscore the adaptation of microorganisms to the challenges presented by industrial wastewater, offering valuable insights into the potential of ICME to enhance the biodegradability of electroplating effluents in preparation for A2O treatment. Similarly, Eroglu et al. [283] designed a novel ternary heterojunction photocatalyst by incorporating carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) onto a binary heterojunction of reduced graphene oxide and black phosphorus (rGOBP) using the ultrasound-assisted liquid exfoliation method. In this experiment, various proportions of the rGOBP binary and CNQDs@rGOBP ternary heterojunctions were prepared to examine how the content influences the cooperative interactions at their interfaces, resulting in superior photo-physical properties. Herein, the amalgamation of polar functional groups found in the as-synthesized rGO and CNQDs, along with the non-bonding electrons of black phosphorus (BP) on the surface, gave rise to a chemically stable photocatalyst exhibiting high catalytic performance. This symbiotic approach, harnessing the unique characteristics of each material, led to a remarkable 2.5-fold increase in the degradation efficiency of organic pollutants when compared to their pristine individual components in aqueous media. The photocatalytic performance of CNQDs@rGOBP heterojunctions was systematically evaluated by investigating different pollutants and varying catalyst concentrations.
