Furthermore, the migration pathway of photogenerated charges was elucidated through scavenger experiments and assessments of material work functions. The underpinning mechanism for the exceptional photocatalytic activity of CNQDs@rGOBP ternary heterojunctions was attributed to a nonclassical type-I heterojunction, which occurred at the farthest edges of the band structures. This highly efficient photocatalytic process was substantiated through the use of various analytical techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM), coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping. These analyses unveiled the morphological features and compositional aspects of rGO, BP, rGOBP, and CNQDs@rGOBP. Furthermore, to assess the photocatalytic efficiency of the various compositions, adsorption-desorption steady-state experiments were conducted under dark conditions. The results indicated that while individual components, rGO and BP, exhibited degradation efficiencies of 15 % and 22 %, respectively, the binary heterojunction rGOBP15 significantly enhanced the photodegradation performance to 43 %. This improvement was further augmented as the proportion of BP increased in the binary structure, with rGOBP65 achieving an impressive degradation efficiency of 72 %. In summary, this study presents a novel approach to photocatalysis, demonstrating the potential of CNQDs@rGOBP ternary heterojunctions as highly efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants in aqueous media. These materials hold significant promise in advancing the field of photocatalysis, offering a sustainable and effective solution for environmental remediation.

Atnafu et al. [284] introduced a novel approach to enhance environmental value by utilizing biodegradable plasticized materials for phosphate removal. In this experiment, a magnetic starch-based Fe3O4 clay polymer nanocomposite (PNC), plasticized with glycerol (G), was formulated for efficient phosphate removal from aqueous solutions. Later on, the PNC was synthesized by incorporating activated carbon (AC), coated iron oxide nanoparticles (CIONP), nanoclay (NC), and glycerol (G) as a plasticizer. Both the PNC and its constituent, CIONP, were subjected to phosphate removal assessments through batch adsorption experiments. It was observed that the adsorption capacity increased with higher adsorbent doses while decreasing with rising phosphate concentrations. Significantly, the synthesized PNC effectively adjusted the optimal phosphate ion adsorption pH from acidic (pH 3) to slightly acidic (pH 6). At the optimal pH, the maximum phosphate adsorption capacity (MPAC) for CIONP and PNC was measured at 3.12 mg P/g and 2.31 mg P/g, respectively. Furthermore, the phosphate removal efficiency of PNC (ranging from 45 % to 95 % at pH 6) was found to be comparable to that of CIONP (ranging from 58 % to 97 % at pH 3) across initial phosphate concentrations ranging from 2 mg P/L to 100 mg P/L. The adsorption kinetics and isotherm study revealed that the pseudo-second-order and Freundlich models provided the best fits to the experimental data. The SEM images demonstrated that the PNC exhibited a heterogeneous porous surface, confirming that phosphate ions adsorption occurred. The observed heterogeneity can be attributed to the admixed adsorbents and may have implications for the material's surface tensile properties and degradation processes. Furthermore, the effect of varying adsorbent doses (ranging from 0.25 g to 2 g) on phosphate adsorption was examined. The results indicated a declining trend in phosphate adsorption capacity (qe) with increasing adsorbent doses. Specifically, when the CIONP dose increased from 0.25 g to 2 g, phosphate removal efficiency improved, suggesting an increase in available active sites for adsorption. However, an excess addition of adsorbent dose led to reduced adsorption capacity, potentially due to particle aggregation, which diminished adsorption efficiency. Similarly, Verma et al. [285] reported the successful synthesis of quercetin-stabilized Bi2S3 nanoparticles (QBi2S3 NPs) for the degradation of three toxic cationic dyes and the eradication of harmful bacteria. Herein, the QBi2S3 NPs were prepared via a straightforward precipitation method under ambient conditions. The photocatalytic activity of QBi2S3 NPs was evaluated under visible light against three cationic dyes: MB, brilliant green (BG), and rhodamine-B (Rh–B), individually and as a mixture. The assessment was conducted at different pH levels (pH 2, 4, 6, and 8) over 120 min. QBi2S3 NPs demonstrated effective degradation of all three cationic dyes, with maximum degradation observed for BG and Rh–B at 120 min, and MB at 60 min. The antibacterial investigations revealed that the combination of quercetin and Bi2S3 NPs exhibited significantly higher antibacterial activity compared to pristine quercetin. The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) values for QBi2S3 NPs (20 ± 2 μg/mL for E. coli, 30 ± 3 μg/mL for E. faecalis, and 40 ± 3 μg/mL for B. subtilis) were lower than those for native quercetin, indicating enhanced synergistic antibacterial effects. Cytotoxicity studies on HaCat and HeLa cell lines confirmed the non-toxic nature of QBi2S3 NPs up to a concentration of 200 μg/mL. Furthermore, a phytotoxicity assay was performed on green gram plants to compare the toxic effects of MB, BG, Rh–B, and QBi2S3 NPs. The results indicated minimal toxicity of BG compared to MB and Rh–B. The morphological analysis of the QBi2S3 NPs revealed their spherical shape and an average size of less than 20 nm. The particles exhibited uniformity, indicating precise control over their dimensions and the prevention of aggregation. HRTEM micrographs further confirmed the uniform and spherical shape of the NPs. EDX mapping analysis provided insights into the elemental composition and distribution within the nanoparticles, with peaks confirming the presence of Bi2S3 and quercetin stabilizer. In conclusion, this study demonstrates the synthesis of quercetin-stabilized Bi2S3 nanoparticles with multifaceted applications in the degradation of cationic dyes and antibacterial activities. The thorough characterization of these nanoparticles emphasizes their potential for various environmental and biological applications.

Salem et al. [286] developed a groundbreaking nano-hybrid carbon-based structure to serve as a versatile chemosensor for natural hazards, enabling the active adsorption of Rose Bengal dye and the detection of hazardous pollutants at the parts per billion (ppb) level. This remarkable advancement, denoted as the MOs@CNT hybrid, was achieved through the amalgamation of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and metal oxides (MOs) via a hydrothermal synthesis process. As, it is known that the MOs@CNT hybrid materials are highly regarded for their instrumental role in combating environmental pollution, owing to their exceptional characteristics. These include their chemical inertness, structurally homogeneous composition, and expansive specific surface areas, which facilitate physical adsorption at active sites. In this experiment, the MOs@CNT hybrid demonstrated its prowess as an adsorbent by efficiently capturing Rose Bengal (RB), showcasing an outstanding adsorption capacity of 245.6 mg/g at 298 K. This performance significantly surpassed that of pristine MOs, which only adsorbed RB at a rate of 48.8 mg/g. The study also delves into the proposed mechanism underlying the adsorption of dyes, shedding light on this process. What adds to the intrigue of this nanomaterial is its remarkable sensing capability, particularly in detecting nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). MOs@CNT hybrid exhibited a sensitive and selective luminescence quenching response when exposed to Cr(VI), nitrobenzene (NB), m-nitroaniline (m-NA), and p-nitroaniline (p-NA) in an aqueous environment. It achieved a notably high quenching rate constant (Ksv) of 3.3 × 105 M−1 for NB and boasts impressively low detection limits, going down to 2.02 × 10−9 M for NB. Consequently, MOs@CNT hybrid emerges as a versatile, long-term solution for the detection of harmful NACs and Cr(VI) in aqueous settings, all while excelling as a potent adsorbent for dyes. Furthermore, the adsorption behavior of RB on MOs@CNT hybrid adheres closely to the Langmuir isotherm model, indicative of monolayer adsorption and the uniform arrangement of active sites on the adsorbent. The favorable nature of RB adsorption was supported by the dimensionless separation factor RL (0.998). Notably, the maximum adsorption capacity (qmax) reaches an impressive 245.6 mg/g.

Additionally, Zhang et al. [287] introduced a novel approach involving the synthesis of copper oxide (CuO) using a straightforward precipitation method and its characterization through various techniques. The primary objective was to harness the efficient activation of periodate (PI) for the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP), a commonly used, yet refractory antibacterial drug. In this experiment, the combined CuO (0.5 g/L) and PI (0.5 mM) process was compared with systems utilizing either PI alone (0.5 mM) or CuO alone (0.5 g/L). Remarkably, the CuO/PI system demonstrated significantly enhanced CIP degradation, with an impressive 98 % removal efficiency achieved within just 30 min, using a 20 mg/L pollutant concentration. The study identified both radical and nonradical degradation pathways as key contributors in this CuO/PI system, with the nonradical pathway, involving holes and electrons, being the primary driver of CIP degradation. Notably, the system exhibited superior efficiency for CIP degradation under both acidic (pH = 3) and near-neutral (pH = 6) conditions when compared to alkaline conditions (pH = 10). This was attributed to the more effective adsorption of PI ions onto CuO nanoparticle surfaces, facilitated by electrostatic interactions between the PI ions and the CuO nanomaterial. Further analysis of degraded samples using ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UHPLC Q-TOF MS) unveiled the primary degradation pathways for CIP during this oxidation process. Cleavage of the piperazine ring and defluorination were identified as the dominant pathways. The CuO/PI system's effectiveness extended beyond CIP degradation, demonstrating excellent removal efficiency for other refractory organic contaminants such as sulfamethoxazole (SMX) and methylene blue (MB) across various water matrices, including real wastewater. Importantly, respiration tests confirmed that the toxicity of the effluents was reduced after undergoing the treatment process. Similarly, Hassanien et al. [288] presented optimized backpropagation neural network (BPNN) prediction models designed to estimate the concentration of titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanomaterials essential for removing Acid Black 24 dye, a prevalent textile dye, from industrial wastewater. While previous studies have addressed the prediction of nanomaterial quantities required to degrade dyes in industrial wastewater through BPNN, few have examined the influence of optimized BPNN models and the degree to which the degradation potential can be enhanced [289–292]. Consequently, this study employed six distinct optimization algorithms: genetic algorithms (GAs), ant colony optimization (ACO), particle swarm optimization (PSO), evaluation strategy (ES), biogeography-based optimization (BBO), and population-based incremental learning algorithm (PBIL) to optimize and assess the BPNN models. The experimental data from chemical laboratory tests form the basis for training and testing these models, with multiple experiments conducted to explore the effects of various factors such as TiO2, H2SO4, NaIO4, K2S2O8, dye, and silver nitrate. They evaluated the performance of the optimized BPNN models through metrics like mean squared error (MSE), root mean squared error (RMSE), and R2. The results underscore the effectiveness of the proposed models, with ACO consistently yielding the most favorable outcomes across all models. R2 values for the TiO2, H2SO4, NaIO4, and K2S2O8 models post-optimization indicated precise predictions, compared to R2 values ranging from 0.888 to 0.984 before optimization. PSO performed admirably for most models, except for the K2S2O8 model. GA leads to improvements in most models, with exceptions in the silver nitrate model. ES only enhances the Dye model, and PBIL doesn't significantly affect the TiO2 model. In summary, this optimized BPNN models demonstrated impressive accuracy in estimating the required TiO2 concentration for the removal of Acid Black 24 dye from industrial wastewater, with ACO exhibiting the most promising results among the optimization techniques.

The efficient removal of EPs)from wastewater is a pressing environmental concern. Carbon-based nanomaterials have shown great promise as adsorbents due to their unique properties, including high surface area, tunable surface chemistry, and structural versatility [293,294]. To optimize the adsorption process and design effective carbon-based nanomaterials, it is essential to understand the underlying adsorption mechanisms. This section explores the adsorption mechanisms involved in the removal of EPs by carbon-based nanomaterials, focusing on both physical and chemical interactions.

Physical adsorption, also referred to as physisorption or non-specific adsorption, plays a significant role in the adsorptive removal of EPs by carbon-based nanomaterials. This mechanism relies on weak intermolecular forces, including van der Waals forces and π-π interactions, to attract EPs to the nanomaterials' surfaces. van der Waals forces are a type of intermolecular force that arises from the temporary fluctuations in electron distribution within molecules. Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, exhibit a large surface area and a high density of π-electrons [295,296]. This unique electronic structure gives rise to substantial van der Waals forces. EPs present in wastewater are attracted to the nanomaterials' surfaces through these intermolecular forces. The strength of van der Waals forces depends on various factors, including the distance between the EPs and the nanomaterials, the surface area of the nanomaterials, and the polarizability of the EPs.

In addition to van der Waals forces, π-π interactions play a crucial role in the physical adsorption of EPs onto carbon-based nanomaterials. π-π interactions occur between aromatic compounds, which possess delocalized π-electron systems, and the conjugated π-electron systems present in carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphene oxide. The aromatic rings of EPs can align and interact with the carbon-based nanomaterials' surface through π-π stacking, resulting in the adsorption of EPs [297–299]. The strength of π-π interactions is influenced by various factors, such as the size, shape, orientation, and electron density distribution of the aromatic rings. The physical adsorption of EPs onto carbon-based nanomaterials offers several advantages. First, it is a non-specific adsorption process, allowing a wide range of EPs to be captured by the nanomaterials. This broad adsorption capability makes carbon-based nanomaterials versatile for the removal of various EPs from wastewater. Second, the large surface area of carbon-based nanomaterials provides ample adsorption sites, enhancing the efficiency of the adsorption process [300–302]. The high surface area-to-volume ratio allows for a greater contact area between the nanomaterials and EPs, facilitating their adsorption. Lastly, the weak nature of the intermolecular forces involved in physical adsorption allows for the desorption of EPs under appropriate conditions, enabling the regeneration and reusability of carbon-based nanomaterials.

Chemical adsorption, also known as chemisorption or specific adsorption, is an important mechanism involved in the adsorptive removal of EPs by carbon-based nanomaterials. Unlike physical adsorption, chemical adsorption involves the formation of chemical bonds between the EPs and the functional groups present on the surface of the nanomaterials. This adsorption mechanism offers higher selectivity and stronger adsorption compared to physical adsorption. One type of chemical bond involved in chemical adsorption is covalent bonding. Some carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide and functionalized carbon nanotubes, possess oxygen-containing functional groups, including hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and epoxy groups [303,304]. These functional groups can undergo chemical reactions with EPs, leading to the formation of covalent bonds between the nanomaterials and the EPs. Covalent bonds are characterized by their strength and stability, ensuring strong and irreversible adsorption of the EPs onto the nanomaterials' surfaces. Electrostatic interactions are another important aspect of chemical adsorption. Carbon-based nanomaterials often exhibit charged surfaces due to the presence of surface functional groups. These functional groups may carry positive or negative charges [239]. EPs that possess opposite charges can interact with the charged surface of the nanomaterials through electrostatic interactions. For example, heavy metal ions or organic molecules with ionizable functional groups can be attracted to the charged surface of the nanomaterials. This type of adsorption is particularly effective for the removal of charged EPs, as the electrostatic interactions facilitate their binding to the nanomaterials.

The presence of specific functional groups on the nanomaterials' surfaces is crucial for enabling chemical adsorption. These functional groups can be tailored through surface modifications or synthesis techniques to enhance the adsorption capacity and selectivity for specific EPs. By introducing functional groups with specific chemical properties, the nanomaterials can selectively target and remove certain types of EPs from wastewater. Chemical adsorption offers several advantages in the removal of EPs [302,305]. The formation of covalent bonds or electrostatic interactions between the nanomaterials and EPs ensures a strong attachment, minimizing the risk of desorption. This leads to a higher removal efficiency and lower leaching of the adsorbed EPs back into the environment. Furthermore, the selective nature of chemical adsorption allows for the targeted removal of specific EPs, reducing the impact on non-targeted species and enhancing the overall efficiency of the wastewater treatment process.

Synergistic effects in the adsorptive removal of EPs by carbon-based nanomaterials refer to the combined action of physical and chemical interactions, which result in enhanced adsorption capacity and selectivity. These effects arise from the unique properties and characteristics of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as their large surface area, unique structure, and functional groups. The large surface area of carbon-based nanomaterials provides ample adsorption sites for the attachment of EPs [304,306,307]. The high surface area-to-volume ratio allows for a greater contact area between the nanomaterials and the EPs, facilitating their adsorption. Physical adsorption mechanisms, such as van der Waals forces and π-π interactions, play a role in the initial attraction and attachment of EPs to the nanomaterials' surfaces. The weak intermolecular forces involved in physical adsorption provide reversible binding, allowing for desorption under certain conditions.

In addition to physical interactions, the presence of functional groups on the surface of carbon-based nanomaterials enables chemical interactions with EPs. Functional groups, such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amino (-NH2) groups, can form covalent bonds or undergo electrostatic interactions with EPs [160,308,309]. Chemical adsorption mechanisms provide a stronger and more selective binding between the nanomaterials and EPs, contributing to the overall adsorption capacity and selectivity. The synergistic effects of physical and chemical interactions in adsorption processes offer several advantages. The combination of reversible physical adsorption and irreversible chemical adsorption provides a more robust and stable adsorption system. Physical adsorption allows for the rapid initial capture of EPs, while chemical adsorption ensures a strong attachment, minimizing the risk of desorption. The selectivity of chemical adsorption enables the targeted removal of specific EPs, reducing the impact on non-targeted species and improving the overall efficiency of wastewater treatment.

The synergistic effects also allow for the versatility of carbon-based nanomaterials in treating various types of EPs. The large surface area and functional groups can be tailored through surface modifications or synthesis techniques to enhance the adsorption capacity and selectivity for specific EPs. This flexibility enables the design and optimization of carbon-based nanomaterials for the removal of a wide range of EPs, including heavy metals, organic dyes, pharmaceuticals, and emerging micropollutants. To illustrate the adsorption process of carbon-based NMs as adsorbents, Zhang et al. [129] created a unique gelatin-based functionalized CNTs@metal-organic framework (F- CNTs@MOF@Gel) adsorbent for MB. The MOF type was decided upon as Cu-BTC, commonly referred to as HKUST-1. Gelatin was used to bind the F-CNTs@Cu- BTC components, preventing environmental contamination from carbon NMs particulate. The F–C-G beads' adsorption method for MB is depicted in Fig. 28 . Initially started, because MB is a cationic dye, its molecules are positively electric in the media. As a result, the negative amino (-NH2), hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) moieties over the F–C-G were electrostatically attracted to the MB particles. Next, the F-CNs and TCu-BTC in the F–C-G interacted with the benzene ring configuration on the MB particles via the π-π electron donor-acceptor (π-π EDA) interface. Furthermore, the accessible Cu2+ in the Cu-BTC ligand and the MB particles were associated via a metal cation binding link. van der Waals interactions may occur among tiny MB particles and the adsorbent substrate in addition to hydrogen bonding across the F–C-G and MB molecules. Additionally, n-π EDA interactions might well be present during the sorption of MB over F–C-G. The electron contributor in this case is n, which might either be a nitrogen or oxygen atom. The carbonyl oxygen (=O) on the F–C-G adsorbent serves as the electron generator, while the aromatic ring around the molecular framework of MB serves as the electron receiver, resulting in the n-π EDA interactions among the MB and adsorbent.

Similarly, in order to remove ibuprofen from an aqueous solution, Hanbali et al. [106] evaluated the utilization of MWCNTs that were derivatized and magnetized as a novel and reusable absorbent. The MWCNTs were first oxidized, and the oxidized substance was then derived using hydrazine (m-MWCNT-HYD), hydroxyl amine (m-MWCNT-HA) and amino acid (m-MWCNT-CYS). The findings indicate that for three adsorbents, a pH of 4 was ideal for the rapid, practically full elimination of Ibu. The pseudo-second-order kinetics of the adsorption was following the Langmuir isotherm model. Ibuprofen elimination rates for m-MWCNT-CYS, m-MWCNT-HYD, and m-MWCNT-HA were up to 98.4 %, 93 %, and 61.5 %, correspondingly. At pH = 4, the greatest adsorption capacities for m-MWCNT-CYS, m-MWCNT-HA and m-MWCNT-HYD were discovered. The proportions of elimination for m-MWCNT-HA, m-MWCNT-HYD, and m-MWCNT-CYS were 61.5 %, 84.5 %, and 98.4 %, correspondingly. As seen in Fig. 29 , when negatives were present, the adsorbent functional moiety (N–H) and the adsorbate anion formed a significant dipole-dipole association (H-bonding). The π-π interactions among the pi-electrons of MWCNT and pi-electrons of the Ibu phenyl ring, which are a major criterion in definite adsorption procedures, constitute a secondary attraction coercion that might additionally relate to adsorption effectiveness in relation to the earlier referenced attraction coercion. n- π interactions may be conceived of as a contributing component, particularly those that take place involving the lone electrons of nitrogen and oxygen (-NH, –OH) and unhybridized p-orbitals of aromatic bonds. The absorption of Ibu into the MWCNT apertures and getting entrapped is another important aspect that must be taken into account in this case's adsorption procedure. Although this sort of adsorption is thought to be pH-independent, if the Ibu is in a protonated form, it may be improved at a lower pH.

Additionally, at pH = 4, both the adsorbate and adsorbent operate as H-bonding acceptors and donors, which results in a stronger association [310,311]. At greater pHs, the adsorbate solely exhibits an H-bond receiver behavior. Additionally, both molecules' electron concentrations are significant at increased pH levels, which could result in some sort of repulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unique properties contributing to adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials contribute to their exceptional adsorption capacity for the removal of EPs from wastewater. These properties include high surface area, tunable surface chemistry, unique structural characteristics, and functional groups. Understanding and harnessing these properties are crucial for optimizing the adsorption process and designing efficient carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, and carbon dots, possess an extremely high surface area. This is due to their unique nanostructures, which provide a large number of accessible surface sites. The high surface area-to-volume ratio of these nanomaterials offers a significant advantage in adsorption applications [312]. The abundant adsorption sites allow for greater interaction between the nanomaterials and EPs, facilitating enhanced adsorption efficiency. The high surface area also provides more opportunities for EPs to come into contact with the adsorbent, increasing the chances of successful adsorption.

Carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit tunable surface chemistry, which is crucial for adsorption processes. The surface chemistry of these nanomaterials can be modified through various methods, such as chemical functionalization, to introduce specific functional groups or enhance their affinity for targeted EPs. Functional groups, such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), amino (-NH2), and sulfonic acid (-SO3H) groups, can be incorporated onto the surface of carbon-based nanomaterials [313–315]. These functional groups can interact with EPs through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, or chemical reactions, leading to enhanced adsorption. The ability to tailor the surface chemistry of carbon-based nanomaterials allows for the selective adsorption of specific EPs and the optimization of adsorption efficiency.

Carbon-based nanomaterials possess unique structural characteristics that contribute to their adsorption capacity. Carbon nanotubes, for example, have a hollow cylindrical structure with a large aspect ratio, providing a large number of exposed surfaces for adsorption. The sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in graphene and graphene oxide form a two-dimensional lattice structure with a high degree of π-conjugation, resulting in a large surface area and excellent adsorption properties [316,317]. The unique structural properties of carbon-based nanomaterials allow for the efficient transport of EPs to adsorption sites, enhancing the adsorption kinetics.

In addition to their tunable surface chemistry, carbon-based nanomaterials can also possess intrinsic functional groups that contribute to their adsorption capacity. For instance, graphene oxide contains oxygen-containing functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which can form hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions with EPs [30]. These functional groups provide additional adsorption sites and enhance the adsorption affinity towards specific EPs. Similarly, carbon dots can possess functional groups on their surface, such as carboxyl, amino, or thiol groups, which facilitate the adsorption of EPs through chemical interactions. The presence of these functional groups further expands the range of EPs that can be effectively adsorbed by carbon-based nanomaterials [318–320].

Carbon-based nanomaterials also exhibit unique electrochemical properties that contribute to their adsorption capacity. The high electrical conductivity of carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphene oxide allows for efficient electron transfer during the adsorption process. This property is particularly advantageous for the adsorption of electroactive EPs, such as heavy metal ions, which undergo redox reactions during adsorption. The electrochemical properties of carbon-based nanomaterials enable not only the physical adsorption of EPs but also electrochemical interactions, enhancing the overall adsorption capacity.

In a nutshell, it could be stated that adsorption is widely recognized as one of the most effective and commendable methods for treating EPs found in wastewater. It involves the transfer of hazardous materials onto the accessible surface of an adsorbent through physical or chemical mechanisms. Compared to traditional treatment approaches, adsorption offers several advantages, including cost-effectiveness, low operational expenses, and reduced generation of waste products during the removal of EPs [321–323]. Furthermore, adsorption techniques are considered environmentally friendly since adsorbents can be recycled and reused multiple times for efficient pollutant removal. Several key qualities need to be considered when selecting adsorbents for EP removal, including their economical efficacy, high surface area, particle size dispersion, presence of functional groups, and polar properties [324,324,325]. These characteristics play a significant role in determining the adsorbent's effectiveness in capturing and retaining EPs from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent developments in carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment

ce:section.ce:para: Novel synthesis approaches have become a focal point in recent developments in carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment. These approaches aim to enhance the properties and adsorption capacities of carbon-based nanomaterials by exploring innovative methods of synthesis. By manipulating various synthesis parameters and precursor materials, researchers can control the size, morphology, surface chemistry, and functional groups of the nanomaterials, ultimately improving their performance in adsorbing emerging pollutants from wastewater [326,327]. One of the novel synthesis approaches being explored is modified chemical vapor deposition (CVD) techniques. CVD involves the deposition of carbon-based nanomaterials onto a substrate by the reaction of precursor gases at high temperatures. By modifying the CVD process, researchers can achieve better control over the growth and structure of the nanomaterials as shown in Fig. 30 . This Figure demonstrates the CVD process for synthesizing graphene. It starts with a molybdenum wetting-layer foil with a polycrystalline copper chunk/foil being purged with argon gas (a). The substrate is then placed in a high-temperature furnace (1135 °C) for cleaning in the Ar/CO2 atmosphere (b), followed by cooling to 1085 °C in a CO + CO2 atmosphere to grow graphene and solidify copper (c). Subsequently, rapid transfer into the water-cooled flange region is performed (d). The figure also includes an optical image of the polycrystalline sample (e), a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of an individual single-crystal graphene grain on the copper catalyst surface (f), and a typical Raman spectrum of graphene transferred from copper to a quartz substrate (g). Additionally, the use of precursor gases with specific functional groups can lead to the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials with tailored surface chemistry, enabling them to selectively adsorb certain pollutants [299]. Electrochemical methods have also emerged as a promising approach for synthesizing carbon-based nanomaterials with improved adsorption properties. These methods involve the electrochemical deposition or electrochemical exfoliation of carbon-based materials. For instance, electrochemical deposition can be used to fabricate carbon nanotubes or graphene on conductive substrates, allowing for the controlled growth of the nanomaterials [328,329]. Electrochemical exfoliation, on the other hand, involves the delamination of graphite or graphite oxide to produce graphene or graphene oxide nanosheets. The advantage of electrochemical methods lies in their ability to produce carbon-based nanomaterials with high purity and specific structural features, which can contribute to enhanced adsorption performance.

Template-assisted synthesis is another novel approach that utilizes templates to guide the growth of carbon-based nanomaterials. Templates can be in the form of porous materials, nanoparticles, or biological entities, which serve as scaffolds for the deposition of carbon species [328,331]. By selecting appropriate templates with desired pore sizes and morphologies, researchers can fabricate carbon-based nanomaterials with well-defined structures and controlled porosity. The templating process allows for the customization of the nanomaterials' surface area, pore volume, and pore size distribution, which are critical factors influencing their adsorption capacity. Bottom-up synthesis strategies are also gaining attention in the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment. These strategies involve the assembly or self-assembly of molecular precursors or building blocks to form carbon-based nanomaterials [332]. One example is the bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots, where small organic molecules are chemically transformed into carbon nanoscale particles with unique optical and surface properties. Bottom-up synthesis offers precise control over the composition and structure of the nanomaterials, enabling the design of materials with tailored adsorption properties and specific functionalities.

The development of novel synthesis approaches for carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment holds significant promise. These approaches allow for the fine-tuning of the nanomaterials' properties, such as surface area, pore size, and functional groups, to optimize their adsorption performance for specific pollutants [333,334]. Additionally, the ability to control the size and morphology of the nanomaterials opens up possibilities for their integration into various treatment systems and processes. However, there are challenges and limitations associated with these novel synthesis approaches. The scalability and cost-effectiveness of the synthesis methods need to be considered for the large-scale production of carbon-based nanomaterials. Moreover, the stability and long-term performance of the synthesized nanomaterials under real-world wastewater conditions should be thoroughly evaluated. Additionally, the potential environmental impacts of the synthesis processes and the disposal of precursor materials or by-products need to be carefully assessed. Furthermore, the synthesis approaches must be compatible with the desired applications and treatment requirements. Different types of carbon-based nanomaterials may exhibit varying adsorption capacities and selectivity towards specific pollutants [335]. Therefore, the selection of the appropriate synthesis approach and precursor materials is crucial to achieving the desired properties and functionality of the nanomaterials.

In recent years, significant advancements have been made in the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment. For example, researchers have successfully synthesized carbon nanotubes with controlled diameters and lengths, which directly impact their adsorption capacity. By using novel catalysts and optimizing growth conditions, the synthesis of carbon nanotubes with enhanced adsorption properties has been achieved. Similarly, the synthesis of graphene and graphene oxide has seen significant progress. Modified synthesis techniques, such as the modified Hummers' method, have been developed to produce graphene oxide with a high degree of functionalization, leading to improved adsorption capabilities. Moreover, the functionalization of graphene and graphene oxide with various functional groups has been explored to enhance their selectivity towards specific pollutants.

Hybrid materials and composites have emerged as a significant advancement in the field of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment. These materials combine the unique properties and functionalities of carbon-based nanomaterials with other materials to create synergistic effects and enhance their adsorption capabilities [336]. The incorporation of carbon-based nanomaterials into hybrid materials and composites allows for improved adsorption efficiency, higher stability, and enhanced selectivity towards specific pollutants in wastewater. One approach to developing hybrid materials is the integration of metal nanoparticles with carbon-based nanomaterials. Metal nanoparticles, such as silver (Ag), gold (Au), or iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles, can be synthesized and incorporated into carbon-based nanomaterials to form hybrid structures [337,338]. The combination of metal nanoparticles with carbon-based nanomaterials provides additional adsorption sites and catalytic activities. Metal nanoparticles can effectively interact with pollutants, leading to enhanced adsorption capacity and the potential for catalytic degradation of pollutants. The synergistic effects between metal nanoparticles and carbon-based nanomaterials result in improved overall performance in wastewater treatment. Another approach is the combination of carbon-based nanomaterials with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs). MOFs are porous materials composed of metal ions or clusters coordinated with organic ligands. The incorporation of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene or carbon nanotubes, into MOFs can enhance the porosity, surface area, and adsorption properties of the materials [339]. The unique structure of MOFs, combined with the high surface area and adsorption capacity of carbon-based nanomaterials, provides an ideal platform for efficient adsorption of EPs from wastewater. The hybrid materials formed by the combination of MOFs and carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit excellent adsorption capabilities and can be tailored for specific pollutants. Polymers are another category of materials that can be integrated with carbon-based nanomaterials to create hybrid materials for wastewater treatment. Polymers offer advantages such as high surface area, tunable porosity, and chemical stability. By incorporating carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene or carbon nanotubes, into polymer matrices, the adsorption performance and selectivity can be improved. The combination of carbon-based nanomaterials with polymers enhances the overall adsorption capacity, stability, and mechanical properties of the hybrid materials. These materials can be fabricated into various forms, such as membranes or adsorbent beads, for efficient removal of EPs from wastewater.

Inorganic materials, such as metal oxides or zeolites, can also be combined with carbon-based nanomaterials to form hybrid composites for wastewater treatment. The incorporation of inorganic materials with carbon-based nanomaterials provides additional functionalities, such as ion exchange or specific adsorption sites, which enhance the adsorption performance. For example, the combination of graphene oxide with metal oxides, such as titanium dioxide (TiO2) or zirconium oxide (ZrO2), can improve the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants in addition to the adsorption capacity [339–341]. These hybrid composites offer synergistic effects and multifunctionality, making them effective for the removal of a wide range of EPs from wastewater. The development of hybrid materials and composites in carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment offers several advantages. Firstly, the combination of different materials allows for the optimization of adsorption properties, selectivity, and stability, leading to improved overall performance. The incorporation of additional functionalities, such as catalytic activity or ion exchange capacity, enhances the versatility of the materials and enables the removal of a broader range of pollutants. Moreover, hybrid materials and composites can be tailored for specific EPs or environmental conditions, providing customized solutions for different wastewater treatment applications.

Despite the advantages, there are also limitations to consider in the use of hybrid materials and composites for wastewater treatment. One limitation is the complexity and cost associated with the synthesis and fabrication of these materials. The integration of different materials requires careful design and optimization to ensure compatibility and achieve the desired properties. The fabrication processes may involve multiple steps and require specialized equipment, which can increase the production cost. Another limitation is the potential leaching of the incorporated materials from the hybrid composites into the treated water. The stability and durability of the hybrid materials need to be assessed to ensure that the incorporated materials do not leach out and contaminate the water during the treatment process. Additionally, the long-term stability and performance of the hybrid materials under continuous adsorption-desorption cycles need to be investigated to evaluate their suitability for practical applications. Furthermore, the scalability of the synthesis and fabrication processes is an important consideration for practical implementation. The synthesis methods and fabrication techniques need to be scalable to produce the hybrid materials in large quantities, especially for industrial-scale wastewater treatment. The cost-effectiveness of the production processes and the availability of precursor materials also play a significant role in the scalability and practicality of hybrid materials. In terms of adsorption performance, the selectivity of the hybrid materials toward specific EPs should be carefully evaluated. The combination of different materials may influence the adsorption affinity and selectivity toward certain pollutants [342,343]. Understanding the interactions between the incorporated materials and the target pollutants is crucial for designing hybrid materials with high selectivity and efficiency. Moreover, the regeneration and reuse of hybrid materials and composites need to be considered. Some hybrid materials may lose their adsorption capacity or structural integrity after multiple adsorption cycles, limiting their reuse potential. Developing effective regeneration techniques, such as thermal treatment, chemical regeneration, or photocatalytic regeneration, can prolong the lifespan and improve the sustainability of hybrid materials.

Despite these limitations, the advancements in the development of hybrid materials and composites for wastewater treatment offer promising solutions for the efficient and effective removal of EPs from wastewater. The combination of carbon-based nanomaterials with other materials provides opportunities for tailoring the adsorption properties, selectivity, and stability of the materials to meet specific treatment requirements. Continued research and development efforts are needed to address the challenges and optimize the performance of hybrid materials and composites, ultimately facilitating their practical implementation in wastewater treatment systems.

Integration with advanced treatment technologies is a significant development in the field of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment. This approach aims to combine the unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials with existing advanced treatment technologies to create more efficient and effective systems for removing EPs from wastewater [344–347]. By integrating carbon-based nanomaterials with advanced treatment technologies, the overall treatment performance can be enhanced, leading to improved water quality and environmental protection. One area where carbon-based nanomaterials have been successfully integrated is in membrane filtration systems. Membrane filtration is a widely used technique for water treatment, and the incorporation of carbon-based nanomaterials into membrane materials can significantly improve their adsorption and separation capabilities [348]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene or carbon nanotubes, can be used as coatings or fillers in membrane materials to increase their surface area and create additional adsorption sites. This allows for more effective removal of EPs, including organic compounds, heavy metals, and emerging contaminants, from the wastewater. The unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as their high adsorption capacity, tunable porosity, and excellent mechanical strength, make them ideal candidates for enhancing membrane filtration systems.

Activated carbon filters are another technology that can be improved through the integration of carbon-based nanomaterials [349,350]. Activated carbon is widely used as an adsorbent in water treatment due to its high surface area and adsorption capacity. However, the incorporation of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene or carbon dots, into activated carbon filters can further enhance their adsorption performance. The nanoscale features of carbon-based nanomaterials provide additional adsorption sites and improve the accessibility of the adsorbent material, resulting in higher removal efficiency for EPs. Moreover, the combination of activated carbon with carbon-based nanomaterials can also enhance the regeneration and reusability of the adsorbent material, making the process more cost-effective and sustainable. In addition to membrane filtration and activated carbon filters, carbon-based nanomaterials can be integrated into advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) for wastewater treatment [351,352]. AOPs involve the generation of highly reactive species, such as hydroxyl radicals, to oxidize and degrade organic pollutants. Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide or carbon nanotubes, can act as catalysts or photocatalysts in AOPs, promoting the generation of reactive species and facilitating the degradation of EPs. The unique electronic and catalytic properties of carbon-based nanomaterials make them suitable for enhancing the performance of AOPs. Furthermore, the integration of carbon-based nanomaterials with AOPs can also improve the stability and reusability of the catalyst, reducing the overall operational costs. The integration of carbon-based nanomaterials with advanced treatment technologies offers several advantages in wastewater treatment [353,354]. Firstly, it enhances the overall treatment efficiency by combining the specific adsorption properties of carbon-based nanomaterials with the established mechanisms of advanced treatment technologies. This synergistic effect allows for a more comprehensive removal of EPs and other contaminants from wastewater, leading to improved water quality. Secondly, the integration of carbon-based nanomaterials can enhance the selectivity and specificity of the treatment process. Carbon-based nanomaterials can be tailored to target specific EPs or classes of pollutants, enabling the removal of specific contaminants of concern. This selective adsorption or catalytic degradation capability is particularly valuable in the context of emerging contaminants that may require specific treatment approaches.

Moreover, the integration of carbon-based nanomaterials with advanced treatment technologies can improve the durability and longevity of the treatment systems. Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene or carbon nanotubes, possess excellent mechanical strength and chemical stability, making them ideal for use in long-term wastewater treatment applications. Their high surface area and adsorption capacity contribute to the longevity of the treatment system by ensuring efficient contaminant removal over an extended period. Additionally, carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit resistance to fouling and fouling mitigation due to their unique surface properties and structure. This reduces the frequency and intensity of system maintenance, leading to cost savings and operational efficiency. Furthermore, the integration of carbon-based nanomaterials with advanced treatment technologies can also enhance the environmental sustainability of the wastewater treatment process [355–358]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are typically derived from renewable carbon sources, such as biomass or waste materials, making them more environmentally friendly compared to traditional adsorbents or catalysts. Their use in wastewater treatment can contribute to the reduction of overall environmental impact by providing a sustainable and resource-efficient solution. Additionally, the regeneration and reusability of carbon-based nanomaterials further enhance their environmental sustainability, as they can be utilized in multiple treatment cycles, reducing the need for frequent replacement and minimizing waste generation.

Despite the numerous advancements and advantages associated with the integration of carbon-based nanomaterials with advanced treatment technologies, there are some limitations and challenges that need to be addressed. One limitation is the cost associated with the production and implementation of carbon-based nanomaterials. The synthesis and fabrication processes for these nanomaterials can be complex and require specialized equipment, which may increase the overall cost of the treatment system [359,360]. Additionally, the scalability of the production processes and the availability of precursor materials may pose challenges for large-scale implementation. Another challenge is the potential release of carbon-based nanomaterials into the treated water or the environment. The stability and potential leaching of nanomaterials need to be thoroughly evaluated to ensure their safe and sustainable use. Efforts should be made to understand the fate and behavior of carbon-based nanomaterials during and after the treatment process to minimize any potential adverse effects on water quality or the ecosystem.

Regeneration and reuse of adsorbents are critical considerations in the development of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment [361,362]. Unlike traditional adsorbents that often lose their adsorption capacity after use, carbon-based nanomaterials offer the potential for regeneration, allowing for their reuse and reducing the overall environmental impact of the treatment process. One common method for regenerating carbon-based nanomaterials is thermal treatment. This involves subjecting the used adsorbents to high temperatures to remove the adsorbed pollutants and restore their adsorption capacity. The thermal treatment process causes desorption of the pollutants from the nanomaterials due to the increased temperature, resulting in the regeneration of the adsorbent. The adsorbents can then be reused in subsequent treatment cycles, reducing the need for frequent replacement and minimizing waste generation. Chemical regeneration is another approach used to restore the adsorption capacity of carbon-based nanomaterials. In this method, chemical agents are employed to desorb and remove the adsorbed pollutants from the nanomaterials. The choice of chemical agent depends on the nature of the pollutants and the specific properties of the nanomaterials [363–365]. The regeneration process involves soaking the used adsorbents in a solution containing the appropriate chemical agent, which leads to the desorption of pollutants from the nanomaterials. After regeneration, the adsorbents can be rinsed and reused in subsequent treatment cycles.

Photocatalytic regeneration is a promising technique that utilizes the photocatalytic properties of carbon-based nanomaterials to regenerate the adsorbents. By incorporating photocatalytic materials, such as graphene oxide or carbon dots, into the nanomaterials, the adsorbents can be regenerated under light irradiation. The photocatalytic regeneration process involves the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under light irradiation, which leads to the degradation of the adsorbed pollutants and the restoration of the adsorption capacity of the nanomaterials [366,367]. This approach offers the advantage of simultaneously regenerating the adsorbents and degrading the pollutants, making it a sustainable and efficient method for adsorbent regeneration. The regeneration process of carbon-based nanomaterials can be optimized by considering various factors. One important factor is the regeneration temperature or the conditions under which thermal treatment or chemical regeneration is carried out. The temperature and duration of the regeneration process should be carefully controlled to ensure the complete removal of adsorbed pollutants without causing any damage to the nanomaterials. Additionally, the choice of chemical agents for chemical regeneration should be compatible with the adsorbents and should effectively desorb the pollutants without negatively affecting the nanomaterials' structure or properties [368,369]. The effectiveness of regeneration techniques also depends on the type and nature of the pollutants being treated. Different pollutants may require specific regeneration approaches, and the selection of the appropriate regeneration method should be based on the characteristics of the pollutants and the adsorbents. Moreover, the regeneration process should be evaluated in terms of its efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and environmental impact to ensure its practical application in wastewater treatment.

The reuse of regenerated adsorbents offers several benefits. Firstly, it reduces the amount of waste generated from the treatment process, contributing to environmental sustainability. By extending the lifespan of the adsorbents through regeneration, the need for frequent replacement and disposal is minimized, resulting in cost savings and reduced environmental burden. Secondly, the reuse of adsorbents reduces the overall operational costs of wastewater treatment [159,370,371]. The regeneration process is typically less expensive than acquiring new adsorbents, and the reuse of regenerated adsorbents eliminates the need for continuous procurement, saving resources and reducing treatment costs. However, there are challenges and limitations associated with the regeneration and reuse of carbon-based nanomaterials. One challenge is the potential loss of adsorption capacity or performance degradation over multiple regeneration cycles. Repeated exposure to high temperatures, chemical agents, or photocatalytic processes may cause structural changes or surface modifications in the nanomaterials, leading to a reduction in their adsorption capacity [372]. Additionally, the presence of residual pollutants or chemical agents after regeneration can affect the adsorbents' performance in subsequent treatment cycles. Another limitation is the selectivity of regeneration methods. Different pollutants may have different affinities for the adsorbents, and the regeneration process may not completely remove all types of pollutants from the nanomaterials. This can result in the accumulation of certain pollutants over multiple regeneration cycles, leading to a decline in the overall treatment efficiency. Furthermore, the scalability and practical implementation of regeneration techniques need to be considered. The regeneration processes should be scalable to accommodate large-scale wastewater treatment operations. Additionally, the integration of regeneration steps into existing treatment systems may require modifications or additional equipment, which can impact the overall feasibility and cost-effectiveness of the regeneration process.

To overcome these challenges, further research is needed to optimize regeneration methods and develop strategies for maintaining the adsorption capacity and performance of carbon-based nanomaterials over multiple regeneration cycles. This can involve exploring novel regeneration techniques, such as combination approaches or the use of synergistic agents, to enhance regeneration efficiency and preserve the adsorbents' properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The connection between adsorptive processes and decarbonization
ce:section.ce:para:
The connection between adsorptive processes and decarbonization is indeed a critical and topical issue. Adsorptive processes, which involve the adsorption of gases or other substances onto solid surfaces or porous materials, play a crucial role in various aspects of decarbonization efforts. Here are some key connections between these two areas.
•
Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) and the Role of Adsorptive Processes

Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technologies are crucial in mitigating greenhouse gas emissions and addressing climate change. These technologies employ various methods to capture carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from industrial processes and power plants before they are released into the atmosphere. Adsorptive processes, a key component of CCS, involve the use of solid adsorbents like activated carbon or metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) to capture CO2. These solid materials possess large surface areas and a strong affinity for CO2 molecules, making them effective in trapping and adsorbing the greenhouse gas. In industrial processes, CO2 is generated as a byproduct of chemical reactions or combustion. These emissions are directed to a capture unit where solid adsorbents, in the form of beds or columns, capture the CO2. Similarly, in power plants, especially those burning fossil fuels, flue gases containing CO2 are treated using adsorption units. As the gases pass through the adsorbent material, CO2 molecules adhere to its surface. The efficiency of CCS with adsorptive processes depends on multiple adsorption-desorption cycles. When the adsorbent reaches saturation, controlled heating or pressure changes release the adsorbed CO2, providing a pure CO2 stream. This captured CO2 can either be stored in geological formations like depleted oil and gas reservoirs or deep saline aquifers, or it can be utilized for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) or the production of chemicals and fuels. By significantly reducing the amount of CO2 emissions that reach the atmosphere, CCS with adsorptive processes contributes to global efforts to decarbonize and reduce the impact of climate change. It is a critical tool in meeting regulatory requirements and achieving sustainability goals.
•
Adsorption Heat Pumps for Efficient Building Climate Control

Adsorption-based heat pumps are innovative systems that play a vital role in the quest for more sustainable and energy-efficient heating and cooling solutions in modern, decarbonized buildings. These advanced heat pump systems are designed to harness the principles of adsorption, making them highly efficient in maintaining the desired indoor climate while minimizing energy consumption and environmental impact. Traditional Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) systems often rely on energy-intensive processes, such as vapor compression, to provide heating and cooling. In contrast, adsorption heat pumps utilize the adsorption-desorption cycle of working fluids to achieve thermal regulation. This unique approach brings several benefits to table. At the core of these systems are solid adsorbents, which have a remarkable ability to adsorb specific substances, such as water vapor. The adsorption process occurs at low temperatures, and when the adsorbent is exposed to the substance it captures, it releases heat. This heat release is harnessed for space heating during the colder months. Conversely, during warm weather, the adsorbent can be desorbed by applying heat, causing the captured substance to evaporate. This phase change absorbs heat from the surrounding environment, effectively providing cooling.

One of the key advantages of adsorption heat pumps is their energy efficiency. They can operate using low-grade waste heat sources, such as solar thermal energy or industrial waste heat, making them ideal for integration into decarbonized building designs. Moreover, these systems have the potential to reduce electricity consumption for space conditioning, especially in regions with a significant demand for both heating and cooling. Adsorption-based heat pumps contribute to the overall decarbonization of buildings by reducing the reliance on conventional HVAC systems that are often powered by fossil fuels. They align with sustainability goals and environmental regulations aimed at minimizing carbon emissions from the built environment.
•
Revolutionizing Renewable Energy Storage for Decarbonization

The transition to a low-carbon future requires a fundamental shift in how we produce, store, and manage energy. Key to this transformation is the integration of renewable energy sources like solar and wind into our energy grids. However, the intermittent nature of renewables poses challenges in matching supply with demand. To address this issue and promote decarbonization, innovative solutions are needed. Adsorptive processes, particularly the concept of renewable energy storage, have emerged as a game-changing technology in this context. Renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind, generate electricity when conditions are favorable, but energy demand doesn't always align with these intermittent energy sources. This misalignment necessitates effective energy storage solutions, allowing excess energy to be saved for use when renewable energy generation is insufficient. This is where adsorptive processes come into play. The principle of adsorption involves the adhesion of molecules, referred to as adsorbates, to the surface of a solid material, usually a porous one. This process can be employed to capture and store energy in the form of adsorbates. In the context of renewable energy, one common approach is to use porous materials like MOFs or activated carbon to capture and retain gases such as hydrogen or methane produced from surplus renewable electricity.

During periods of excess renewable energy generation, electricity can be used to generate these gases through processes like electrolysis. The produced gases are then adsorbed onto the porous material. This stored energy can be retained for extended periods, making it an effective and flexible energy storage solution. When energy demand exceeds the renewable energy supply, the stored gases can be desorbed from the material and converted back into electricity, serving as a reliable and green energy source. The use of adsorptive processes in renewable energy storage offers several advantages. First, it facilitates the leveling of energy supply and demand, helping to stabilize the grid and ensure a continuous power supply. Second, it contributes to the reduction of carbon emissions by reducing reliance on fossil fuels to meet energy demands during low renewable energy periods. Additionally, adsorptive energy storage systems can be applied at various scales, from small residential setups to large utility-scale systems, making them highly adaptable. In the broader context of decarbonization, the integration of adsorptive energy storage complements the transition to renewable energy sources. By efficiently storing excess energy, adsorptive processes become a linchpin in building a sustainable and greener energy future. As renewable energy adoption continues to grow, the role of adsorptive processes in energy storage becomes increasingly significant, paving the way for a more resilient and eco-friendly energy grid.
•
Revolutionizing Hydrogen Production for a Decarbonized Future

The decarbonization of various sectors, including transportation and industry, hinges on a critical component: green hydrogen. Hydrogen, when produced using renewable energy sources and stored without carbon emissions, can serve as a clean and versatile energy carrier. To enable the widespread adoption of green hydrogen, innovative and sustainable production methods are essential. Adsorptive processes offer a promising solution in the realm of hydrogen production, as they facilitate the efficient generation and storage of this clean energy resource. At the core of the decarbonization challenge is the need to transition from conventional, carbon-intensive hydrogen production methods to sustainable alternatives. The conventional approach relies on steam methane reforming (SMR), a process that consumes natural gas and produces CO2 as a byproduct. The emissions associated with SMR hinder the environmental benefits of using hydrogen as a clean energy carrier. To address this issue, green hydrogen production methods that neither rely on fossil fuels nor produce harmful emissions must be embraced.

Adsorptive processes present a novel approach to green hydrogen production. These processes leverage the unique properties of solid materials, often referred to as adsorbents, to selectively capture and store hydrogen gas. Key to this technology is the reversible adsorption-desorption cycle, which allows for the capture, storage, and subsequent release of hydrogen. Adsorbents such as MOFs, activated carbon, and various other porous materials play a central role in this cycle. The green hydrogen production process typically involves using renewable energy sources, such as solar or wind power, to perform water electrolysis. During electrolysis, water (H2O) is split into its constituent elements: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). The hydrogen gas produced in this manner can then be adsorbed onto the adsorbent material, where it is safely stored for later use.

This adsorption-based hydrogen storage approach offers numerous advantages. It provides a means to efficiently capture, store, and transport hydrogen without releasing harmful emissions or relying on carbon-based energy sources. When energy demand calls for the use of hydrogen, the stored gas can be readily desorbed from the adsorbent and converted into electricity or used in various industrial processes. As the transition to a decarbonized future gains momentum, the role of adsorptive processes in green hydrogen production becomes increasingly significant. The ability to harness renewable energy for clean hydrogen generation and storage is a key enabler of sustainable energy systems. It not only reduces the carbon footprint of hydrogen production but also opens doors to eco-friendly transportation and industrial applications.
•
Direct Air Capture (DAC): A Promising Carbon Removal Approach

In the global battle against climate change, it's clear that reducing CO2 emissions at their source is paramount. However, addressing the historic and ongoing accumulation of CO2 in the atmosphere is equally crucial. This is where Direct Air Capture (DAC) technologies come into play, and at their heart lies the ingenious application of adsorption. DAC technologies are pioneering the path towards large-scale removal of CO2 directly from the atmosphere. Their overarching goal is to contribute to carbon removal efforts, effectively reducing the atmospheric concentration of CO2 and mitigating the impacts of global warming. Unlike traditional carbon capture technologies, which capture CO2 emissions at their source, DAC focuses on extracting CO2 from the open air.

At the core of DAC lies the principle of adsorption. In simple terms, adsorption is the process of capturing molecules or particles from a gas or liquid onto a solid material, known as the adsorbent. In the context of DAC, the target is CO2, and the adsorbents are specialized materials capable of selectively capturing and holding CO2 molecules. The DAC process begins with large structures or facilities, often equipped with arrays of fans or collectors that draw in air from the environment. As the air passes through these collectors, it comes into contact with the adsorbent material. CO2 molecules within the air are adsorbed onto the surface of the solid adsorbent. This captured CO2 is then separated from the adsorbent, concentrated, and typically compressed for storage or utilization. One key advantage of DAC technologies is their versatility. They can be deployed in various locations and are not dependent on the source of CO2 emissions, making them valuable tools for addressing historical carbon accumulation. DAC systems can be strategically placed near CO2-intensive industrial sites, power plants, or even in remote areas where carbon removal is particularly vital.

Adsorption plays a central role in the selectivity and efficiency of DAC systems. The choice of adsorbent material is critical, as it determines which molecules are captured. Various adsorbents, such as amine-based materials or MOFs, have been developed and tested for their effectiveness in capturing CO2. The ability to selectively target CO2 from the atmosphere while avoiding the capture of other gases is essential for the efficiency of DAC systems. Furthermore, the captured CO2 can be used in a variety of applications, including carbon sequestration, enhanced oil recovery, or even as a feedstock for the production of valuable chemicals and fuels. In the quest for innovative carbon removal technologies, DAC stands out as a powerful and flexible solution. By harnessing the principles of adsorption, DAC systems have the potential to significantly contribute to the reduction of atmospheric CO2 concentrations and help combat climate change. As the world continues to seek effective and sustainable ways to address the carbon challenge, the role of DAC in carbon removal and the science of adsorption remain at the forefront of innovative solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constraints and potential benefits of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment
Cost-effectiveness, scalability, energy efficiency, Life Cycle Analysis and sustainability in wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Cost-effectiveness and scalability play vital roles in evaluating the practical application of carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment. Although the initial production cost of these nanomaterials can be relatively high, their unique characteristics can offset these costs, resulting in long-term benefits. The cost of production can be attributed to various factors, such as complex synthesis processes and the cost of raw materials, like carbon precursors. To improve cost-effectiveness, researchers are exploring cost-reduction strategies and scalable synthesis methods, ensuring their viability for large-scale wastewater treatment applications. Despite the initial production cost, carbon-based nanomaterials offer several advantages contributing to their cost-effectiveness. Their large surface area and high adsorption capacities reduce the amount of adsorbent material required, leading to cost savings in material procurement and disposal. Moreover, their renewability and reusability further enhance their cost-effectiveness, as they can be regenerated and reused multiple times, lowering operational costs. Additionally, the scalability of these nanomaterials is crucial in meeting the growing demand for wastewater treatment. Researchers are developing techniques for upscaling synthesis processes while maintaining desired characteristics, ensuring their practicality in industrial-scale facilities.

In terms of scalability, the sourcing of raw materials, such as graphene oxide or carbon nanotubes, plays a significant role in long-term cost-effectiveness. Sustainable and environmentally friendly methods for sourcing these materials can contribute to scalability and overall cost-effectiveness. Energy efficiency is a critical factor in wastewater treatment, directly impacting operational costs and environmental sustainability. Carbon-based nanomaterials offer opportunities to improve energy efficiency, particularly in membrane filtration systems and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs). By integrating these nanomaterials into membrane structures, separation efficiency can be enhanced, reducing energy consumption, especially in desalination processes. Furthermore, in AOPs, carbon-based nanomaterials act as catalysts or photocatalysts, enhancing energy efficiency by facilitating pollutant degradation under milder conditions. Sustainability considerations throughout the lifecycle of carbon-based nanomaterials are essential. Responsible raw material extraction, energy-efficient synthesis processes, and the potential for recycling or upcycling these materials contribute to sustainability.

Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) plays a crucial role in evaluating the environmental sustainability of scaling up adsorptive processes for wastewater treatment. However, conducting LCA in this context is not without its challenges. Firstly, collecting reliable and representative data for the assessment can be difficult, especially for emerging materials and processes. The complexity of adsorptive wastewater treatment systems poses another challenge, making it intricate to account for all inputs and outputs comprehensively. These systems are also interconnected with other wastewater treatment methods, complicating the assessment of their collective environmental impact. Furthermore, the dynamic nature of adsorption, which involves cycles of adsorption and regeneration, adds complexity to the evaluation. Uncertainties arise due to data limitations and the dynamic nature of adsorption, impacting the accuracy of impact assessments. Quantifying resource and energy inputs for large-scale adsorption processes can be challenging, particularly when considering the production and regeneration of materials. Determining appropriate end-of-life scenarios for adsorbent materials, including recycling or disposal, can be complex and have significant environmental implications. Additionally, comparing adsorptive processes to other wastewater treatment technologies requires a comprehensive and fair analysis, considering all relevant factors.

Meeting regulatory requirements for environmental impact assessments, which can vary by region, poses another challenge in LCA. Finally, effectively communicating the findings of LCA to stakeholders, decision-makers, and the public is essential but can be challenging. Overcoming these challenges necessitates collaboration among experts, refined data collection methods, standardized LCA practices for wastewater treatment, and staying updated with the latest research and innovations in LCA.

Ensuring a reliable and high-quality supply of adsorbent materials, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, as wastewater treatment demands increase is a complex challenge. The transition from small-scale laboratory production to large-scale industrial production can introduce variations in material quality and properties, necessitating rigorous quality control measures to maintain uniformity. Additionally, the availability of critical resources and catalysts required for material synthesis must be assured to avoid potential shortages. The environmental impact of material production should also be considered in the context of the overall environmental sustainability of the wastewater treatment process. Moreover, energy plays a pivotal role in both the production and operation of adsorptive processes. The energy-intensive synthesis of advanced adsorbent materials like carbon nanotubes and graphene poses a significant challenge when scaling up production. Finding innovative and energy-efficient synthesis methods becomes essential. Furthermore, large-scale adsorption systems require energy for operation, including equipment like pumps and monitoring devices. Minimizing energy consumption during this phase is crucial for sustainability. Some adsorption processes involve regeneration, and developing energy-efficient methods for this phase is a substantial challenge. The choice of energy sources, including the transition to renewable sources like solar or wind, also impacts the overall energy consumption. Balancing cost-effectiveness and energy efficiency is a persistent challenge, as energy-efficient methods may require higher initial investments but offer long-term energy savings. Efficient design, monitoring, and control systems are fundamental for reducing energy consumption while maintaining high performance in large-scale adsorption processes.

Addressing these challenges necessitates a multifaceted approach, combining the expertise of researchers, engineers, and industry professionals. It involves ongoing innovation in materials synthesis and process design, a strong commitment to sustainability, and a focus on resource and energy efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Health, safety, and environmental implications of carbon-based nanomaterials in wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: In the use of carbon-based nanomaterials for wastewater treatment, it is crucial to consider the potential health, safety, and environmental implications, along with strategies to minimize associated risks. The release of nanoparticles from carbon-based nanomaterials can occur during production, use, or disposal. These nanoparticles may enter the environment, necessitating risk assessment and mitigation. Researchers study nanoparticle behavior, stability, aggregation, and potential transformations impacting toxicity and mobility. Interactions with environmental matrices like soils and sediments are vital for risk assessment. Tailoring the surface properties and functional groups of nanomaterials can modify their environmental behavior, enhancing stability and reducing aggregation. In wastewater treatment, carbon-based nanomaterials aid in reducing environmental burdens. Their high adsorption capacities efficiently remove a range of contaminants, resulting in cleaner effluents and reduced impacts on aquatic ecosystems. Regeneration and reuse of these nanomaterials reduce waste and resource consumption, contrasting with traditional, single-use adsorbents. A holistic approach to ensure safe and sustainable use encompasses responsible sourcing, environmentally friendly synthesis, and effective management to minimize nanoparticle release.

To address health and safety in carbon-based nanomaterial use for wastewater treatment, potential risks must be acknowledged and minimized. The unique properties of nanomaterials necessitate different safety measures compared to bulk materials. Containment measures and protective equipment are essential to prevent inhalation or dermal exposure during production, handling, and use. Engineering controls like ventilation systems minimize airborne nanoparticle release. Toxicological studies are crucial to understanding the potential health effects of carbon-based nanomaterials, considering factors such as size, surface chemistry, shape, and dose. These studies evaluate impacts on cellular function, organ systems, and overall health, including oxidative stress, inflammation, and genotoxicity.
